Before running *Place & Route*, you must complete *Synthesis*. Before running *Static Timing Analysis (STA)*, the design must be placed and routed. Tools and steps form a **dependency chain** — exactly like university course prerequisites.

> **Real-World Analogy — Course Prerequisites:**
> To register for "VLSI Design" (V), you must first complete "Digital Electronics" (D) and "CMOS Circuits" (C). Each course is a vertex; each prerequisite is a directed edge. A topological sort produces a *legal semester plan* — a sequence you can actually follow without violating any constraint.

### 1.3 Importance in Electronic Design Automation (EDA)

Topological sort is one of the most frequently invoked graph algorithms in EDA tool flows. Its primary applications are:

| EDA Application | Role of Topological Sort |
|---|---|
| **Logic Synthesis** | Orders combinational logic gates so each gate is evaluated *after* its fan-in cone is ready. |
| **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** | Propagates arrival times through combinational paths in topological order. |
| **Circuit Netlist Traversal** | Determines the *evaluation order* of cells/gates in a gate-level netlist. |
| **Scheduling in High-Level Synthesis** | Allocates operations to clock cycles respecting data dependencies. |
| **Cycle / Latch-Loop Detection** | Identifies combinational loops in a flattened netlist. |
| **Design Compiler Flow Ordering** | Sequences RTL Compiler, Floorplan, Place & Route, CTS, and Optimization passes. |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics / Constants to Remember:**
> - **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — *linear in graph size*.
> - **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ for the in-degree array, queue, and result list.
> - **Number of Orderings:** Can be as high as $\vert V \vert !$ (worst case) for sparse DAGs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Prerequisites — Graph Terminology

Before applying topological sort, you must internalize these definitions because KTU questions frequently test them as 2–3 mark sub-parts:

- **Directed Graph (Digraph):** A graph $G = (V, E)$ where every edge has a direction. An edge $(u, v)$ is *incident from* $u$ *to* $v$.
- **In-Degree:** The number of edges entering a vertex.
  $$\text{in-degree}(v) = \vert \{ u \in V : (u, v) \in E \} \vert$$
- **Out-Degree:** The number of edges leaving a vertex.
  $$\text{out-degree}(v) = \vert \{ w \in V : (v, w) \in E \} \vert$$
- **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph):** A directed graph with **no directed cycles**.
- **Source Vertex (In-Degree Zero):** A vertex with **no incoming edges** — a natural starting point of a topological order.
- **Sink Vertex (Out-Degree Zero):** A vertex with **no outgoing edges** — typically a design output or primary output pin in a netlist.

### 2.2 Why Does a Topological Sort Exist Only on a DAG?

> **Theoretical Justification (proof sketch):**
> 1. A finite DAG must have at least one source vertex (a vertex with in-degree 0). *(Proof: any maximal directed path must terminate at such a vertex.)*
> 2. Removing a source vertex and its incident edges yields a *smaller* DAG.
> 3. By induction, recursively removing sources produces a valid linear ordering.
> 4. Conversely, if a topological ordering exists, then by transitivity the graph cannot contain a cycle $v_1 \to v_2 \to \cdots \to v_k \to v_1$ because it would require $v_1$ to appear both before and after itself.

### 2.3 The Two Classical Algorithms

#### **Algorithm A: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS / Source-Removal Method)**

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Compute the in-degree of every vertex; store in array `in_deg[v]`.
2. Initialize a **queue** (or priority queue / stack) with all source vertices (in-degree = 0).
3. While the queue is non-empty:
   - Dequeue a vertex $u$ and append it to the output list.
   - For every outgoing edge $(u, v)$:
     - Decrement `in_deg[v]` by 1.
     - If `in_deg[v]` becomes 0, enqueue $v$.
4. If the output list contains all $n$ vertices → **valid topological order**.
   Else → **cycle detected** (the remaining vertices form at least one cycle).

#### **Algorithm B: DFS-Based Topological Sort**

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Perform a standard **Depth-First Search** from every unvisited vertex.
2. When DFS finishes exploring a vertex (all descendants visited — *post-order*), **push it onto a stack**.
3. At the end, **pop** all vertices from the stack to obtain the topological order.
4. Equivalent logic: record the *finish time* of each vertex; sorting by decreasing finish time gives a topological order.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Units / Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | In-degree of vertex $v$ | $\text{in-deg}(v) = \sum_{u \in V} \mathbb{1}[(u,v) \in E]$ | Integer $\geq 0$ |
| 2 | Out-degree of vertex $v$ | $\text{out-deg}(v) = \sum_{w \in V} \mathbb{1}[(v,w) \in E]$ | Integer $\geq 0$ |
| 3 | Sum of all in-degrees | $\sum_{v \in V} \text{in-deg}(v) = \vert E \vert$ | Edge count |
| 4 | Sum of all out-degrees | $\sum_{v \in V} \text{out-deg}(v) = \vert E \vert$ | Edge count |
| 5 | Time Complexity (Both algorithms) | $T(n, m) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Linear |
| 6 | Space Complexity | $S(n) = O(\vert V \vert)$ | Linear |
| 7 | Cycle-Detection Condition | Output length $< \vert V \vert \Rightarrow$ Cycle exists | Boolean |
| 8 | Max # of Topological Orders | Upper bound $\vert V \vert !$ (worst-case DAG) | Combinatorial |
| 9 | Min # of Topological Orders | 1 (only when DAG is a total order chain) | Combinatorial |
| 10 | # of Sources in any DAG | $\geq 1$ (always) | Lower bound |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the output of Kahn's algorithm. The order is built by *removing* source vertices one-by-one. Changing the order in which you dequeue equal-priority sources produces a *different valid* topological order — both are correct.

### 2.5 Real-World EDA Utility (Engineering Perspective)

In commercial EDA tools such as **Synopsys Design Compiler**, **Cadence Genus**, and **Siemens EDA (formerly Mentor) Calibre**, topological ordering underlies:

- **Levelized computation:** Gates are grouped into *logic levels* by iteratively removing all current in-degree-zero cells. This determines the *critical path length* and the minimum number of clock cycles.
- **Forward & backward STA traversal:** Arrival times propagate forward through the topological order; required arrival times propagate backward through the *reverse* topological order.
- **Cycle-breaking in combinational loops:** Before timing analysis, the engine performs a topological sort; any unvisited cells at the end indicate a combinational loop, which is reported as a fatal netlist error.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Kahn's Algorithm on a Sample DAG

> Consider the DAG $G = (V, E)$ with:
> $$V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}, \quad E = \{(A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (C,D), (D,E), (C,F), (F,E)\}$$
> This represents a typical gate-level netlist of a small combinational sub-circuit.

#### **Step 1 — Compute In-Degree of Every Vertex**

| Vertex | Incoming Edges | In-Degree |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | (none) | **0** ← source |
| B | (A, B) | 1 |
| C | (A, C) | 1 |
| D | (B, D), (C, D) | 2 |
| E | (D, E), (F, E) | 2 |
| F | (C, F) | 1 |

#### **Step 2 — Initialize Queue with Sources**

$$\text{Queue} = [\,A\,], \quad \text{Output} = [\,]$$

#### **Step 3 — Iterative Dequeue and Decrement**

**Iteration 1:** Dequeue $A$. Append to output. Update neighbors $B$ and $C$:
$$\text{in-deg}(B): 1 \to 0, \quad \text{in-deg}(C): 1 \to 0$$
$$\text{Queue} = [\,B, C\,], \quad \text{Output} = [\,A\,]$$

**Iteration 2:** Dequeue $B$. Append. Update $D$:
$$\text{in-deg}(D): 2 \to 1$$
$$\text{Queue} = [\,C\,], \quad \text{Output} = [\,A, B\,]$$

**Iteration 3:** Dequeue $C$. Append. Update $D$ and $F$:
$$\text{in-deg}(D): 1 \to 0, \quad \text{in-deg}(F): 1 \to 0$$
$$\text{Queue} = [\,D, F\,], \quad \text{Output} = [\,A, B, C\,]$$

**Iteration 4:** Dequeue $D$. Append. Update $E$:
$$\text{in-deg}(E): 2 \to 1$$
$$\text{Queue} = [\,F\,], \quad \text{Output} = [\,A, B, C, D\,]$$

**Iteration 5:** Dequeue $F$. Append. Update $E$:
$$\text{in-deg}(E): 1 \to 0$$
$$\text{Queue} = [\,E\,], \quad \text{Output} = [\,A, B, C, D, F\,]$$

**Iteration 6:** Dequeue $E$. Append.
$$\text{Queue} = [\,], \quad \text{Output} = [\,A, B, C, D, F, E\,]$$

#### **Step 4 — Validate the Result**

$$\vert \text{Output} \vert = 6 = \vert V \vert \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No cycle. Valid topological order.}$$

> **Final Topological Order (one of several):** $A \to B \to C \to D \to F \to E$

#### **Verification (Every Edge Respects the Order):**

| Edge | From Position | To Position | Valid? |
|---|---|---|---|
| (A, B) | 1 | 2 | ✓ |
| (A, C) | 1 | 3 | ✓ |
| (B, D) | 2 | 4 | ✓ |
| (C, D) | 3 | 4 | ✓ |
| (D, E) | 4 | 6 | ✓ |
| (C, F) | 3 | 5 | ✓ |
| (F, E) | 5 | 6 | ✓ |

All 7 edges satisfy $\text{pos}(u) < \text{pos}(v)$. **Topological sort verified.** ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DFS-Based Topological Sort

Using the **same DAG** as above, we apply the DFS variant and demonstrate the *finish-time stack*.

**Adjacency Representation (for DFS):**
$$A \to \{B, C\}, \quad B \to \{D\}, \quad C \to \{D, F\}, \quad D \to \{E\}, \quad F \to \{E\}, \quad E \to \{\}$$

#### **DFS Traversal Trace**

| Step | Action | Stack (Bottom → Top) | Output (Pop Order) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | visit(A), recurse to B | [A] | — |
| 2 | visit(B), recurse to D | [A, B] | — |
| 3 | visit(D), recurse to E | [A, B, D] | — |
| 4 | visit(E) — no neighbors, **push E** | [A, B, D] | E |
| 5 | backtrack, **push D** | [A, B] | E, D |
| 6 | backtrack, **push B** | [A] | E, D, B |
| 7 | backtrack to A, recurse to C | [A] | E, D, B |
| 8 | visit(C), recurse to D (already visited) | [A, C] | E, D, B |
| 9 | recurse to F | [A, C] | E, D, B |
| 10 | visit(F), recurse to E (already visited) | [A, C, F] | E, D, B |
| 11 | backtrack, **push F** | [A, C] | E, D, B, F |
| 12 | backtrack, **push C** | [A] | E, D, B, F, C |
| 13 | backtrack, **push A** | [ ] | E, D, B, F, C, A |

#### **Final Output (Pop Stack from Top):**

$$\text{Topological Order} = A \to C \to F \to B \to D \to E$$

> **Observation:** This is a *different but equally valid* topological ordering of the same DAG. KTU expects either to receive full credit provided every edge constraint is satisfied.

---

### 3.3 Cycle-Detection Counter-Example

> **Question:** What happens if the edge $(E, A)$ is added to the above graph?

The new graph now contains the cycle $A \to B \to D \to E \to A$. Let's run Kahn's algorithm:

- **Initial sources:** Only $A$ (in-degree of $A$ is now 1, not 0).
- **Queue** $= [\,]$ — empty from the start!
- The algorithm terminates with $\text{Output} = [\,]$.
- Since $\vert \text{Output} \vert = 0 < 6 = \vert V \vert$, a **cycle is detected** ✓.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication in EDA:** A netlist with a combinational loop will cause the timing engine to fail during topological sort. The EDA tool aborts analysis and reports a *combinational loop error* to the designer — this is precisely the same cycle-detection mechanism.

---

### 3.4 Complexity Derivation

Let $n = \vert V \vert$ and $m = \vert E \vert$.

**For Kahn's Algorithm:**

$$T(n, m) = T_{\text{in-deg}} + T_{\text{enqueue}} + T_{\text{dequeue}} + T_{\text{edge-relax}}$$

Each step:
- In-degree computation: visits each edge once → $O(m)$.
- Each vertex enqueued and dequeued at most once → $O(n)$.
- Each edge relaxed (decremented) exactly once → $O(m)$.

$$T(n, m) = O(n) + O(n) + O(m) = O(n + m)$$

**For DFS-Based Algorithm:**

- Each vertex visited once → $O(n)$.
- Each edge examined twice (forward + back) → $O(m)$.
- Push/pop stack operations → $O(n)$.

$$T(n, m) = O(n + m)$$

Both algorithms achieve **optimal linear time** because every edge and vertex must be examined at least once.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Directed Acyclic Graph (Sample DAG for Topological Sort)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[A] --> B1[B]
    A1 --> C1[C]
    B1 --> D1[D]
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[E]
    C1 --> F1[F]
    F1 --> E1
```

> **Figure 4.1:** A sample DAG with 6 vertices and 7 edges. The valid topological orderings include: `A B C D F E`, `A C B D F E`, `A C F B D E`, etc.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Kahn's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input Directed Graph G] --> IDG[Compute In-Degree of Every Vertex]
    IDG --> INIT[Initialize Queue Q with all In-Degree Zero Vertices]
    INIT --> CHK{Q is empty?}
    CHK -- No --> DEQ[Dequeue Vertex u from Q]
    DEQ --> APP[Append u to Output List]
    APP --> REL[For each outgoing edge u to v: Decrement in-degree of v]
    REL --> CHK2{in-degree of v becomes zero?}
    CHK2 -- Yes --> ENQ[Enqueue v into Q]
    CHK2 -- No --> CHK
    ENQ --> CHK
    CHK -- Yes --> VER[Output Length equals V count?]
    VER -- Yes --> OK[Valid Topological Order Produced]
    VER -- No --> CYCLE[Cycle Detected in Graph]
```

> **Figure 4.2:** Functional flow of Kahn's BFS-based topological sorting algorithm. The cycle-detection branch is highlighted as a critical safety net in EDA netlist analysis.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DFS-Based Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Input Directed Graph G] --> MARK[Mark all Vertices as Unvisited]
    MARK --> LOOP{All Vertices Visited?}
    LOOP -- No --> PICK[Pick an Unvisited Vertex v]
    PICK --> DFS[Invoke DFS from v]
    DFS --> REC{More Adjacent Unvisited?}
    REC -- Yes --> GOREC[Recurse to Adjacent Vertex]
    GOREC --> REC
    REC -- No --> FIN[Push v onto Finish Stack]
    FIN --> LOOP
    LOOP -- Yes --> POP[Pop All Vertices from Stack in Order]
    POP --> RESULT[Resulting Sequence is a Topological Order]
```

> **Figure 4.3:** Functional flow of the DFS-based topological sort. The recursion unwinding corresponds to *post-order* finishing of vertices.

---

### 4.4 Cycle-Detection Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    A2[A] --> B2[B]
    B2 --> D2[D]
    D2 --> E2[E]
    E2 -.->|Back edge forming cycle| A2
    A2 --> C2[C]
    C2 --> D2
    C2 --> F2[F]
    F2 --> E2
```

> **Figure 4.4:** The same DAG as in Figure 4.1, with an additional back-edge $(E \to A)$ shown as a dashed line. This creates the cycle $A \to B \to D \to E \to A$, which will cause Kahn's algorithm to terminate with an empty output.

---

### 4.5 EDA Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NET[Gate-Level Netlist] --> PARSE[Parse into Directed Graph]
    PARSE --> TOPSORT[Apply Topological Sort]
    TOPSORT --> CYC{Cycle Detected?}
    CYC -- Yes --> ERR[Report Combinational Loop Error to Designer]
    CYC -- No --> LEVEL[Compute Logic Levels per Vertex]
    LEVEL --> STA[Perform Static Timing Analysis Forward and Backward]
    STA --> OUT[Generate Timing Reports slack hold setup]
```

> **Figure 4.5:** The role of topological sort inside a typical EDA static-timing-analysis flow. Without a valid topological order, neither forward nor backward propagation of arrival / required times is possible.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define Topological Sort. State the necessary and sufficient condition for a directed graph to have a topological ordering.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> A *topological sort* of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is a linear ordering $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n$ of its vertices such that for every directed edge $(v_i, v_j) \in E$, we have $i < j$.

> **Necessary and Sufficient Condition (2 Marks):**
> A directed graph admits a topological ordering **if and only if** it is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** — i.e., it contains **no directed cycles**.
> - *Necessity:* If a topological ordering exists, the graph cannot contain a cycle (else a vertex would have to appear before and after itself).
> - *Sufficiency:* Every finite DAG has at least one source vertex (in-degree 0). Removing it iteratively yields a topological order.

---

#### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between Kahn's algorithm and the DFS-based approach for topological sorting. Mention the time complexity of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Kahn's Algorithm (BFS) | DFS-Based Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Underlying Traversal** | Breadth-First (queue-based) | Depth-First (stack / recursion) |
| **Data Structure** | Queue of in-degree-zero vertices | Recursion stack / adjacency list |
| **Selection Criterion** | Picks *any* current source vertex | Picks a vertex and explores *deepest descendants first* |
| **Cycle Detection** | Output size $< \vert V \vert$ at termination | A back-edge encountered during DFS |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| **Natural Use Case** | Level-order logic evaluation in STA | Finish-time-based dependency analysis |

Both algorithms are **asymptotically optimal** because they are bounded below by the requirement to scan every vertex and edge.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Kahn's Algorithm & Cycle Detection** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For the directed graph $G = (V, E)$ shown in **Figure 5.1**:
$$V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}, \quad E = \{(1,2), (1,3), (2,4), (3,4), (4,5), (5,6), (6,3), (3,7)\}$$

> **Note:** Vertex 7 has no outgoing edges. Vertex 3 has an incoming edge from 6, and 6 has an incoming edge from 5, and 5 from 4, and 4 from both 2 and 3 — forming a possible cycle $3 \to 7$ is a sink, but $3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 3$ is a **cycle**.

**Apply Kahn's algorithm step-by-step. Show the in-degree table, the queue state at each iteration, and the final output. Comment on the result.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the role of topological sorting in **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** of a VLSI design? Explain with a suitable block diagram how the order of gate evaluation affects slack computation. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Construct the In-Degree Table (2 Marks)**

| Vertex | Incoming Edges | In-Degree |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | — | **0** |
| 2 | (1, 2) | 1 |
| 3 | (1, 3), (6, 3) | 2 |
| 4 | (2, 4), (3, 4) | 2 |
| 5 | (4, 5) | 1 |
| 6 | (5, 6) | 1 |
| 7 | (3, 7) | 1 |

**Step 2: Initialize Queue with Sources (0.5 Mark)**

$$\text{Queue} = [\,1\,], \quad \text{Output} = [\,]$$

**Step 3: Iterations (3.5 Marks)**

| Iteration | Dequeue | Output After | Edges Relaxed | In-Degree Updates | Queue After |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | [1] | (1,2), (1,3) | in-deg(2):1→0; in-deg(3):2→1 | [2] |
| 2 | 2 | [1, 2] | (2, 4) | in-deg(4):2→1 | [ ] |
| 3 | — | — | — | Queue empty, STOP | [ ] |

**Step 4: Final Output and Cycle Conclusion (1 Mark)**

$$\text{Output} = [\,1, 2\,], \quad \vert \text{Output} \vert = 2 < 7 = \vert V \vert$$

$$\therefore \text{Cycle Detected: } 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 3$$

The remaining unvisited vertices $\{3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$ form a directed cycle, so no valid topological order exists for this graph.

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> **Static Timing Analysis (STA) and Topological Sort (7 Marks):**

In Static Timing Analysis, the timing engine computes the **arrival time (AT)** at every cell output and the **required arrival time (RAT)** at every cell input, and finally reports the **slack** at each timing endpoint:
$$\text{slack}(n) = \text{RAT}(n) - \text{AT}(n)$$

**Forward Propagation (1.5 Marks):** Arrival times must be propagated *from primary inputs (PIs) to primary outputs (POs)* along the combinational paths. This requires the cells to be processed in a **forward topological order** — each gate evaluated only after all its fan-in cones have been evaluated. Without a topological order, the arrival-time computation would either miss paths or double-count gates.

**Backward Propagation (1.5 Marks):** Required arrival times are propagated *from POs to PIs* in the **reverse topological order** of the same graph.

**Combinational Loop Detection (1.5 Marks):** If the netlist contains a combinational loop (e.g., a latch missing in a feedback path), the topological sort will fail — the STA tool reports a *combinational loop* and aborts. This is the same cycle-detection mechanism as in Part (a).

**Levelized Evaluation (1 Mark):** Topological sort naturally produces *logic levels*. The critical-path delay equals the *longest path* in the levelized DAG.

**Slack Computation (1.5 Marks):**
$$\text{slack} = T_{\text{period}} - T_{\text{CP}} - T_{\text{setup}} - T_{\text{skew}}$$
where $T_{\text{CP}}$ (critical-path delay) is computed using topological traversal. A *negative slack* indicates a timing violation, which must be fixed by optimization, buffering, or pipeline insertion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **(Lose 1 mark)** Forgetting to show the *in-degree table* explicitly — KTU mandates step-by-step working.
> 2. **(Lose 1 mark)** Stopping the algorithm when the queue empties but failing to compare $\vert \text{Output} \vert$ with $\vert V \vert$ and conclude *cycle detected*.
> 3. **(Lose 2 marks)** In Part (b), confusing *forward* topological order (AT propagation) with *backward* order (RAT propagation). Be explicit about the direction of propagation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — DFS Approach & EDA Applications** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** For the DAG with vertices $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and edges $E = \{(A, C), (A, D), (B, D), (B, E), (C, F), (D, F), (E, F)\}$, apply the **DFS-based topological sort** algorithm. Draw the recursion tree, show the finish-stack, and produce the final ordering. Verify that your result is a valid topological order. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how topological sorting is used in **logic-level computation and gate-level netlist evaluation** in EDA. Show how the topological order determines the *level* of each gate and the *critical path* of the circuit. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Build the Adjacency List (1 Mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\to \{C, D\} \\
B &\to \{D, E\} \\
C &\to \{F\} \\
D &\to \{F\} \\
E &\to \{F\} \\
F &\to \{\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Run DFS, Record Finish Times (4 Marks)**

| DFS Call | Action | Stack (Bottom → Top) | Output (Pop order) |
|---|---|---|---|
| visit(A) | Push A | [A] | — |
| → visit(C) | Push C | [A, C] | — |
| → visit(F) | Push F | [A, C, F] | — |
| ← backtrack | **Pop F** (no unvisited neighbors) | [A, C] | F |
| ← backtrack | **Pop C** | [A] | F, C |
| → visit(D) | Push D | [A, D] | — |
| → visit(F) | already visited, no recursion | [A, D] | F, C |
| ← backtrack | **Pop D** | [A] | F, C, D |
| ← backtrack to A | **Pop A** | [ ] | F, C, D, A |
| visit(B) | Push B | [B] | — |
| → visit(D) | already visited | [B] | F, C, D, A |
| → visit(E) | Push E | [B, E] | — |
| → visit(F) | already visited | [B, E] | F, C, D, A |
| ← backtrack | **Pop E** | [B] | F, C, D, A, E |
| ← backtrack | **Pop B** | [ ] | F, C, D, A, E, B |

**Step 3: Pop Stack to Get Order (1 Mark)**

$$\text{Topological Order} = B \to E \to A \to D \to C \to F$$

**Step 4: Verify All Edges (1 Mark)**

| Edge | Position of $u$ | Position of $v$ | Valid? |
|---|---|---|---|
| (A, C) | 3 | 5 | ✓ |
| (A, D) | 3 | 4 | ✓ |
| (B, D) | 1 | 4 | ✓ |
| (B, E) | 1 | 2 | ✓ |
| (C, F) | 5 | 6 | ✓ |
| (D, F) | 4 | 6 | ✓ |
| (E, F) | 2 | 6 | ✓ |

All 7 edges satisfy $\text{pos}(u) < \text{pos}(v)$. **Topological sort verified.** ✓

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> **Logic-Level Computation in EDA (7 Marks):**

**Gate-Level Netlist as a DAG (2 Marks):** A flattened gate-level netlist of a purely combinational circuit is naturally a DAG. Each cell is a vertex; each wire (from cell output to cell input) is a directed edge. Sequential elements (flip-flops) act as *boundaries* between clock cycles — between two FFs, the combinational cone is a DAG.

**Levelization (2 Marks):** A *logic level* is assigned to each gate as follows:
$$\text{level}(v) = 1 + \max\{\text{level}(u) : (u, v) \in E\}$$
Primary inputs (PIs) are at level 0. Topological sort provides a *natural order* in which to compute levels — process each vertex only after all its predecessors have been assigned levels. The **critical path** is:
$$L_{\text{CP}} = \max_{v \in \text{POs}} \text{level}(v)$$

**Critical Path Identification (1.5 Marks):** The longest combinational path (in number of gate delays) equals the maximum level. Topological order ensures this maximum is found correctly because the entire fan-in cone of every cell is processed before the cell itself.

**Use in EDA Tools (1.5 Marks):** Commercial EDA tools (Synopsys DC, Cadence Genus) compute *logic levels* using topological sort. Optimization passes (buffering, gate sizing, retiming) are guided by the critical path identified via this levelized structure. **Static timing engines** also rely on this ordering to propagate delays in the correct causal direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **(Lose 1 mark)** Not labelling which vertex is at which *DFS discovery time* versus *finish time* — both are needed in the stack trace.
> 2. **(Lose 1 mark)** Forgetting to mark already-visited neighbors (e.g., $F$ being visited from both $C$ and $D$) — this is what *prevents infinite recursion* and proves the graph is acyclic.
> 3. **(Lose 1 mark)** In Part (b), not distinguishing *combinational* levels from *sequential* boundaries.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition (Must Memorize):** A *topological sort* is a linear ordering of vertices of a DAG such that every directed edge $(u, v)$ satisfies $u$ appearing before $v$ in the order.
- **Existence Theorem:** A topological ordering exists **iff** the graph is a **DAG** (no directed cycles).
- **Source Vertex:** A vertex with in-degree $= 0$. Every DAG has at least one source.
- **Kahn's Algorithm (BFS):** Repeatedly remove a source, decrement neighbor in-degrees, append source to output. *Cycle detected* if output has fewer than $\vert V \vert$ vertices.
- **DFS Approach:** Push vertex onto a *finish stack* on DFS post-order; pop stack to get topological order.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — optimal for both algorithms.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ for the in-degree array, queue, and output list.
- **Cycle-Detection Trigger:** In Kahn's — empty queue before processing all vertices. In DFS — encountering a *back-edge* to an ancestor in the recursion stack.
- **Multiple Valid Orders:** A DAG can have exponentially many valid topological orderings.
- **EDA Uses (Critical for PEAET741):**
  1. Gate-level evaluation order in logic simulation.
  2. Forward / backward propagation in Static Timing Analysis.
  3. Logic level computation for critical-path delay estimation.
  4. Combinational loop detection in netlists.
  5. Scheduling of synthesis, placement, and routing passes.
- **Verification Rule (Always Apply):** Every edge $(u, v)$ must have $\text{pos}(u) < \text{pos}(v)$ — this is the KTU board's *gold-standard* check.
- **Comparison Quick-Points:**
  - Kahn's → iterative, queue-based, naturally *levelized*.
  - DFS → recursive, finish-time-based, naturally gives *reverse post-order*.
- **Pitfalls to Avoid:**
  - Do not confuse *in-degree* with *out-degree*.
  - Do not output the order as the order vertices *enter* the queue — output them as they *leave*.
  - Always show the in-degree table; KTU deducts marks if intermediate steps are missing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shortest Path Algorithms - Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm and Floyd Warshall Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shortest Path Algorithms - Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm and Floyd Warshall Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Shortest Path Algorithms — Formal Definition

In the context of **Electronic Design Automation (EDA)**, shortest path algorithms are fundamental graph-theoretic procedures used to compute the minimum cost (or minimum delay, minimum wirelength, minimum impedance) path between nodes in a circuit graph, netlist, or routing fabric. A **weighted directed/undirected graph** $G(V, E, W)$ consists of a finite set of vertices $V$, edges $E$, and weights $W: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{\geq 0}$. The objective is to find a path $P = \{v_0, v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$ between any two vertices $v_s$ and $v_t$ such that the cumulative path weight $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} w(v_i, v_{i+1})$ is minimized.

Two canonical algorithms form the cornerstone of this topic in the **KTU 2024 EDA syllabus**:

- **Dijkstra's Algorithm (1959)**: A *single-source, single-destination (or all-destinations)* shortest path algorithm for graphs with non-negative edge weights. It uses a greedy strategy combined with priority queue relaxation.
- **Floyd–Warshall Algorithm (1962)**: An *all-pairs shortest path* algorithm based on dynamic programming that handles positive, zero, and negative edge weights (but not negative cycles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In EDA, Dijkstra is heavily used in **timing-driven routing**, **signal integrity analysis**, and **Steiner tree approximation**, whereas Floyd–Warshall is used in **all-pairs timing analysis**, **transistor-level delay matrices**, and **switch-level fault propagation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### The Kerala Road Trip Analogy (Dijkstra's)

Imagine you are at the KTU Campus in **Thiruvananthapuram** and want to find the shortest driving time to **Kozhikode**. You have a map (the graph) with cities as *nodes* and highways as *edges* (with travel times as *weights*).

**Dijkstra's algorithm** works like this: You don't try every possible route blindly. Instead, you maintain a "smart city list":
- Start at Trivandrum (distance = 0).
- Repeatedly pick the **unvisited city with the currently smallest known travel time** (greedy choice).
- From that city, "relax" — i.e., update — the travel times of its neighboring cities if a shorter route is discovered through the current city.

This mirrors how EDA routers iteratively settle the closest un-routed net in global routing.

### The "Comparing All Bus Stops" Analogy (Floyd–Warshall)

Now imagine you have a **Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) timetable matrix** where the entry $(i, j)$ is the direct bus time from stop $i$ to stop $j$. Floyd–Warshall says: *"For every possible intermediate stop $k$, check if going from $i$ to $k$ and then from $k$ to $j$ is faster than the currently known direct route."* It tries every intermediate stop, systematically improving the matrix until the **absolute shortest time** between every pair of stops is known.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions to Memorize:**
> - **Greedy Algorithm**: Builds solution step-by-step, always picking locally optimal choice (Dijkstra).
> - **Dynamic Programming**: Solves sub-problems, stores results, and combines them (Floyd–Warshall).
> - **Relaxation**: Updating the shortest-path estimate if a better path is found.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | EDA Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Infinity $\infty$ | $2^{31} - 1$ or $\infty$ | Initial sentinel for unreachable nodes |
| Edge weight $w(u,v)$ | Non-negative real | Wire delay, capacitance, impedance |
| Path length $d[v]$ | Real number | Cumulative cost from source |
| Time Complexity (Dijkstra, with min-heap) | $O((V + E) \log V)$ | Used in large VLSI netlists |
| Time Complexity (Floyd–Warshall) | $O(V^3)$ | Used in moderate-size all-pairs analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Constants for KTU Exams:** The vertex set cardinality is denoted **$\vert V \vert = n$**, and edge set cardinality is **$\vert E \vert = m$**. These appear in nearly every complexity question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Operational Breakdown

Dijkstra's algorithm solves the **Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP)** problem on a graph $G = (V, E, W)$ where all edge weights $w(u, v) \geq 0$.

### Logical Steps (Why & How)

1. **Initialization:** Assign distance $d[s] = 0$ for the source vertex $s$, and $d[v] = \infty$ for all other vertices $v \in V \setminus \{s\}$. Mark all vertices as *unvisited*. Maintain a priority queue $Q$ keyed by current $d[v]$.

2. **Greedy Selection (The "Why"):** Extract the unvisited vertex $u$ with the **minimum tentative distance** $d[u]$. Because all edge weights are non-negative, once $u$ is extracted, $d[u]$ is **final** — no future path can improve it (this is the core correctness argument).

3. **Relaxation (The "How"):** For each neighbor $v$ of $u$, compute the alternative path cost $d[u] + w(u, v)$. If this is less than $d[v]$, update $d[v]$ and record the predecessor $\pi[v] = u$.

4. **Termination:** Repeat steps 2–3 until $Q$ is empty (or the target vertex is extracted). The final $d[v]$ values are the shortest distances.

### Pseudocode Logic (Mathematical Representation)

$$
D_0[v] = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } v = s \\ \infty & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

For iteration $k$, select:

$$
u_k = \arg\min_{v \in V \setminus S_{k-1}} D_{k-1}[v]
$$

where $S_{k-1}$ is the set of *settled* (visited) vertices. Then update:

$$
D_k[v] = \min\{D_{k-1}[v],\; D_{k-1}[u_k] + w(u_k, v)\} \quad \forall v \in V \setminus S_k
$$

## 2.2 Floyd–Warshall Algorithm — Operational Breakdown

Floyd–Warshall solves the **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP)** problem. It considers all possible intermediate vertices when computing the shortest path between any two vertices.

### Logical Steps (Why & How)

1. **Initialization:** Construct a distance matrix $D^{(0)}$ where $D^{(0)}[i][j] = w(i, j)$ if edge $(i, j)$ exists, $0$ if $i = j$, and $\infty$ otherwise.

2. **Recursive Improvement:** For each intermediate vertex $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, construct $D^{(k)}$ from $D^{(k-1)}$ by considering whether the shortest path from $i$ to $j$ passes through $k$.

3. **Recursive Relation (Core Equation):**

$$
D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\left\{ D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \right\}
$$

4. **Termination:** After $k = n$, the matrix $D^{(n)}$ contains the all-pairs shortest path distances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Dynamic Programming?** The optimal substructure is: *"The shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using intermediate vertices from $\{1, \ldots, k\}$ either does not use $k$, or uses $k$ as the final intermediate hop, splitting into two sub-paths."* This naturally lends itself to DP.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application / Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Dijkstra Relaxation** | $d[v] = \min\{d[v], d[u] + w(u, v)\}$ | Single-pair, single-source |
| **Settled Set Condition** | $u = \arg\min_{v \notin S} d[v]$ | Greedy extraction from priority queue |
| **Floyd–Warshall Recurrence** | $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\{D^{(k-1)}[i][j], D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\}$ | All-pairs DP |
| **Initial Distance Matrix** | $D^{(0)}[i][j] = w(i,j)$ if $(i,j) \in E$, else $\infty$ | Matrix $D^{(0)}$ setup |
| **Diagonal Entries** | $D^{(k)}[i][i] = 0$ | Zero self-distance always |
| **Dijkstra Complexity (Binary Heap)** | $O((V + E) \log V)$ | Most common KTU answer |
| **Dijkstra Complexity (Array)** | $O(V^2)$ | Dense graph variant |
| **Floyd–Warshall Complexity** | $O(V^3)$ | Triple nested loop |
| **Floyd–Warshall Space** | $O(V^2)$ | In-place matrix optimization |
| **Negative Cycle Detection** | $\exists\, i: D[i][i] < 0$ | Diagonal becomes negative |
| **Predecessor (Path Reconstruction)** | $\pi[v] = u$ where $u$ last relaxed $v$ | Traces shortest path tree |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:**
> - **Dijkstra** FAILS on negative edge weights (greedy extraction assumes finality).
> - **Floyd–Warshall** handles negative weights but FAILS on negative cycles (returns incorrect/$- \infty$ values).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI Global Routing**: Dijkstra finds shortest Manhattan routes between pins on a routing grid.
- **Static Timing Analysis (STA)**: Floyd–Warshall computes delay matrices in transistor networks.
- **FPGA Architecture Mapping**: Shortest path guides logic block placement to minimize interconnect delay.
- **Network-on-Chip (NoC) Routing**: Dijkstra variants (e.g., turn-model routing) rely on shortest-path computations.
- **PCB Design**: Floyd–Warshall helps compute via-cost matrices for layer assignment.
- **Fault Diagnosis in Analog Circuits**: All-pairs signal-flow delays identify critical fault paths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Dijkstra's Algorithm

**Problem:** Consider the directed weighted graph below representing an EDA routing fabric. Find the shortest path from source vertex **A** to all other vertices.

**Edge List (representing wire delay in nanoseconds):**
- $(A, B) = 4$, $(A, C) = 1$, $(C, B) = 2$, $(C, D) = 5$, $(B, D) = 1$, $(B, E) = 6$, $(D, E) = 3$

### Step-by-Step Execution

**Initialization:**

$$
d[A] = 0,\quad d[B] = \infty,\quad d[C] = \infty,\quad d[D] = \infty,\quad d[E] = \infty
$$

Settled set $S = \{\}$. Priority queue $Q = \{A(0), B(\infty), C(\infty), D(\infty), E(\infty)\}$.

---

**Iteration 1:** Extract $u = A$ (minimum $d[A] = 0$). Settle $S = \{A\}$.

Relax neighbors of $A$:
- To $B$: $\min\{\infty,\; 0 + 4\} = 4$, so $d[B] = 4$, $\pi[B] = A$.
- To $C$: $\min\{\infty,\; 0 + 1\} = 1$, so $d[C] = 1$, $\pi[C] = A$.

Updated $Q = \{C(1), B(4), D(\infty), E(\infty)\}$.

---

**Iteration 2:** Extract $u = C$ (minimum $d[C] = 1$). Settle $S = \{A, C\}$.

Relax neighbors of $C$:
- To $B$: $\min\{4,\; 1 + 2\} = \min\{4, 3\} = 3$, so $d[B] = 3$, $\pi[B] = C$.
- To $D$: $\min\{\infty,\; 1 + 5\} = 6$, so $d[D] = 6$, $\pi[D] = C$.

Updated $Q = \{B(3), D(6), E(\infty)\}$.

---

**Iteration 3:** Extract $u = B$ (minimum $d[B] = 3$). Settle $S = \{A, C, B\}$.

Relax neighbors of $B$:
- To $D$: $\min\{6,\; 3 + 1\} = \min\{6, 4\} = 4$, so $d[D] = 4$, $\pi[D] = B$.
- To $E$: $\min\{\infty,\; 3 + 6\} = 9$, so $d[E] = 9$, $\pi[E] = B$.

Updated $Q = \{D(4), E(9)\}$.

---

**Iteration 4:** Extract $u = D$ (minimum $d[D] = 4$). Settle $S = \{A, C, B, D\}$.

Relax neighbors of $D$:
- To $E$: $\min\{9,\; 4 + 3\} = \min\{9, 7\} = 7$, so $d[E] = 7$, $\pi[E] = D$.

Updated $Q = \{E(7)\}$.

---

**Iteration 5:** Extract $u = E$. Settle $S = \{A, C, B, D, E\}$. Algorithm terminates.

### Final Result Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
d[A] &= 0 \\
d[B] &= 3 \quad \text{(path: } A \to C \to B \text{)} \\
d[C] &= 1 \quad \text{(path: } A \to C \text{)} \\
d[D] &= 4 \quad \text{(path: } A \to C \to B \to D \text{)} \\
d[E] &= 7 \quad \text{(path: } A \to C \to B \to D \to E \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Floyd–Warshall Algorithm

**Problem:** Compute the all-pairs shortest path for the same graph using Floyd–Warshall. Use vertices labeled $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ (we may relabel for matrix convenience).

**Relabel for matrix indexing:** Let A = 1, B = 2, C = 3, D = 4, E = 5.

### Step 1: Construct Initial Matrix $D^{(0)}$

Using $D^{(0)}[i][j] = w(i, j)$, with $\infty$ for missing edges and 0 on the diagonal:

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 1 & \infty & \infty \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 6 \\
\infty & 2 & 0 & 5 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0 & 3 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Recurrence for $k = 1$ (intermediate vertex = A = 1)

Check: Does any path through vertex 1 improve? Only edges going out of 1 are $(1,2)=4$ and $(1,3)=1$. New paths: $D[2][3]$ via 1: $D[2][1] + D[1][3] = \infty + 1 = \infty$ (no improvement). All other rows are unchanged.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 1 & \infty & \infty \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 6 \\
\infty & 2 & 0 & 5 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0 & 3 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Recurrence for $k = 2$ (intermediate vertex = B = 2)

Check pairs $(i, j)$ that can be improved via vertex 2:
- $D[1][4]$: $\min\{\infty,\; D[1][2] + D[2][4]\} = \min\{\infty,\; 4 + 1\} = 5$.
- $D[1][5]$: $\min\{\infty,\; D[1][2] + D[2][5]\} = \min\{\infty,\; 4 + 6\} = 10$.
- $D[3][4]$: $\min\{5,\; D[3][2] + D[2][4]\} = \min\{5,\; 2 + 1\} = 3$.
- $D[3][5]$: $\min\{\infty,\; D[3][2] + D[2][5]\} = \min\{\infty,\; 2 + 6\} = 8$.

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 1 & 5 & 10 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 6 \\
\infty & 2 & 0 & 3 & 8 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0 & 3 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Recurrence for $k = 3$ (intermediate vertex = C = 3)

Out-edges from 3 are $(3,2)=2$ and $(3,4)=5$. New improvements:
- $D[1][4]$: $\min\{5,\; D[1][3] + D[3][4]\} = \min\{5,\; 1 + 5\} = 5$ (no change).
- $D[1][2]$: $\min\{4,\; D[1][3] + D[3][2]\} = \min\{4,\; 1 + 2\} = 3$. **(Improved!)**
- $D[2][4]$: $\min\{1,\; D[2][3] + D[3][4]\} = \min\{1,\; \infty + 5\} = 1$ (no change).

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 1 & 5 & 10 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 6 \\
\infty & 2 & 0 & 3 & 8 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0 & 3 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5: Apply Recurrence for $k = 4$ (intermediate vertex = D = 4)

Out-edges from 4 are $(4,5)=3$. New improvements:
- $D[1][5]$: $\min\{10,\; D[1][4] + D[4][5]\} = \min\{10,\; 5 + 3\} = 8$. **(Improved!)**
- $D[2][5]$: $\min\{6,\; D[2][4] + D[4][5]\} = \min\{6,\; 1 + 3\} = 4$. **(Improved!)**
- $D[3][5]$: $\min\{8,\; D[3][4] + D[4][5]\} = \min\{8,\; 3 + 3\} = 6$. **(Improved!)**

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 1 & 5 & 8 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 4 \\
\infty & 2 & 0 & 3 & 6 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0 & 3 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6: Apply Recurrence for $k = 5$ (intermediate vertex = E = 5)

Vertex 5 has no outgoing edges. No improvements possible.

$$
D^{(5)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 1 & 5 & 8 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 4 \\
\infty & 2 & 0 & 3 & 6 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0 & 3 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 7: Verification (Compare with Dijkstra Results)

Reading from row 1 (vertex A):
$$
d[A,B] = 3,\quad d[A,C] = 1,\quad d[A,D] = 5,\quad d[A,E] = 8
$$

The Dijkstra result gave $d[A,D] = 4$ and $d[A,E] = 7$. **Discrepancy!** Let's re-check — in our initial matrix we used $(A,B)=4$, but Dijkstra discovered a better path $A \to C \to B$ with cost $1+2=3$. The matrix above already captures this (entry $[1][2] = 3$). So Dijkstra's $d[A,B]=3$ matches. But $d[A,D]=5$ from matrix vs $d[A,D]=4$ from Dijkstra. The path $A \to C \to B \to D$ has cost $1 + 2 + 1 = 4$, but in our matrix encoding, $(C, B) = 2$ was added at $k=2$ (using vertex 2 as intermediate) — this means **after** $k=2$, the entry $[3][2] = 2$, and at $k=3$ we set $[1][2] = 3$. So at $k=4$, we'd compute $[1][4] = \min\{5, [1][2]+[2][4]\} = \min\{5, 3+1\} = 4$.

The matrix should reflect $D[1][4] = 4$ after re-examining step 4. Let me correct:

**Corrected $D^{(4)}$:**

After $k=4$, also check improvements via newly shortened paths through vertex 2:
- $D[1][4]$: $\min\{5,\; D[1][2] + D[2][4]\} = \min\{5,\; 3 + 1\} = 4$. **(Updated!)**

$$
D^{(4)}_{\text{corrected}} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 1 & 4 & 7 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 4 \\
\infty & 2 & 0 & 3 & 6 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0 & 3 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Now $d[A,B]=3$, $d[A,C]=1$, $d[A,D]=4$, $d[A,E]=7$ — **all match Dijkstra's result.** ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Floyd–Warshall requires careful **transitive** update at each $k$. Many students miss paths that use multiple intermediate vertices of index $\leq k$. Always re-check at each iteration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Input Graph G and Source s]) --> initA[Initialize dist all vertices = Infinity]
    initA --> initA2[Set dist s = 0]
    initA2 --> initA3[Build Priority Queue Q with all vertices]
    initA3 --> checkA{Is Q empty?}
    checkA -- Yes --> endA([End: Output shortest distances])
    checkA -- No --> extractA[Extract vertex u with minimum dist from Q]
    extractA --> relaxLoop[For each neighbor v of u]
    relaxLoop --> relaxCheck{dist u + w u v less than dist v?}
    relaxCheck -- Yes --> updateA[Update dist v = dist u + w u v]
    relaxCheck -- No --> relaxLoop
    updateA --> nextA[Mark u as visited]
    nextA --> checkA
```

## 4.2 Floyd–Warshall Algorithm — Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start: Input distance matrix D of order n x n]) --> initB[Initialize D superscript 0 with edge weights]
    initB --> loopKB[For k = 1 to n do]
    loopKB --> loopIB[For i = 1 to n do]
    loopIB --> loopJB[For j = 1 to n do]
    loopJB --> relaxB{D superscript k-1 i k + D superscript k-1 k j less than D superscript k-1 i j?}
    relaxB -- Yes --> updateB[Update D superscript k i j = sum via k]
    relaxB -- No --> loopJB
    updateB --> loopJB
    loopJB -- End j loop --> loopIB
    loopIB -- End i loop --> loopKB
    loopKB -- End k loop --> negCheckB{Any diagonal D i i less than 0?}
    negCheckB -- Yes --> warnB([Negative cycle detected])
    negCheckB -- No --> endB([End: D superscript n is all-pairs shortest path])
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIJK["Dijkstra's Paradigm"]
        d1[Single Source s] --> d2[Greedy Selection via Priority Queue]
        d2 --> d3[Relax Outgoing Edges]
        d3 --> d4[Settle Vertex]
        d4 --> d2
    end

    subgraph FLOYD["Floyd Warshall Paradigm"]
        f1[Initialize Matrix D superscript 0] --> f2[Loop over k intermediate vertices]
        f2 --> f3[For each pair update via k]
        f3 --> f4[Store improved matrix D superscript k]
        f4 --> f2
    end

    inputG[Weighted Graph G] --> DIJK
    inputG --> FLOYD
    DIJK --> outD[Single Source Shortest Tree]
    FLOYD --> outF[All Pairs Distance Matrix]
```

## 4.4 Application Mapping in EDA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eda[Electronic Design Automation] --> route[Global Routing]
    eda --> sta[Static Timing Analysis]
    eda --> pcb[PCB Layer Assignment]

    route --> use1[Dijkstra finds shortest Manhattan path per net]
    sta --> use2[Floyd Warshall computes delay matrix for all gate pairs]
    pcb --> use3[Floyd Warshall computes via cost matrix across layers]

    use1 --> out1[Minimized wirelength and congestion]
    use2 --> out2[Critical path identification]
    use3 --> out3[Optimal layer transition]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define shortest path problem. Differentiate between single-source and all-pairs shortest path problems.**

**Model Answer:**
The shortest path problem involves finding a path between two vertices (or sets of vertices) in a weighted graph such that the sum of the edge weights along the path is minimized.

- **Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP):** Computes shortest paths from one designated source vertex to all other vertices. Example algorithms: Dijkstra's, Bellman-Ford.
- **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP):** Computes shortest paths between **every** pair of vertices. Example algorithms: Floyd–Warshall, Johnson's.

SSSP runs in $O((V+E)\log V)$ for Dijkstra; APSP runs in $O(V^3)$ for Floyd–Warshall.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why does Dijkstra's algorithm fail on graphs with negative edge weights? Justify briefly.**

**Model Answer:**
Dijkstra's algorithm uses a **greedy strategy**: once a vertex $u$ is extracted from the priority queue with the minimum tentative distance, it is marked as **settled**, assuming no future shorter path can be found through $u$.

This assumption is valid **only if all edge weights are non-negative**. With a negative edge weight $w(a, b) < 0$, a later path $s \to \cdots \to a \to b$ could yield a smaller distance than the value $d[b]$ at the time $b$ was settled. This violates the greedy finality property, leading to incorrect results. Bellman-Ford or Floyd–Warshall must be used instead.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]` (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Dijkstra's algorithm with its time complexity. State any two applications in EDA.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the directed graph given below, find the shortest path from vertex **1** to all other vertices using Dijkstra's algorithm. Show all iterations clearly.

**Edges and weights:**
$(1, 2) = 7$, $(1, 3) = 9$, $(1, 6) = 14$, $(2, 3) = 10$, $(2, 4) = 15$, $(3, 4) = 11$, $(3, 6) = 2$, $(4, 5) = 6$, $(5, 6) = 9$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]` (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Floyd–Warshall algorithm with its recurrence relation. How does it detect negative cycles?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the Floyd–Warshall algorithm to compute the all-pairs shortest path matrix for the graph with vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and the following weighted directed edges:

$(1, 2) = 5$, $(1, 3) = 9$, $(1, 4) = 1$, $(2, 4) = 2$, $(3, 2) = 3$, $(4, 3) = 4$. Show matrices $D^{(0)}$, $D^{(1)}$, $D^{(2)}$, $D^{(3)}$, and $D^{(4)}$.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Question A — Solution

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation [7 Marks]:**

Dijkstra's algorithm (1959) solves the **Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP)** problem for a graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize $d[s] = 0$, $d[v] = \infty$ for all other $v$.
2. Insert all vertices into a min-priority queue $Q$ keyed by $d[v]$.
3. While $Q$ is non-empty:
   - Extract vertex $u$ with minimum $d[u]$ from $Q$.
   - For each neighbor $v$ of $u$, perform relaxation: if $d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]$, then $d[v] = d[u] + w(u, v)$ and update $Q$.

**Time Complexity:**
- Using binary/min-heap: $O((V + E) \log V)$
- Using simple array (dense graphs): $O(V^2)$

**Two EDA Applications:**
1. **Global Routing in VLSI:** Finds shortest Manhattan-distance paths between pin pairs on the routing grid, minimizing wirelength and congestion.
2. **Static Timing Analysis (Short Paths):** Computes minimum arrival times at every gate output from primary inputs.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

Initialize: $d[1]=0$, $d[2]=d[3]=d[4]=d[5]=d[6]=\infty$. Queue: $\{1(0), 2(\infty), 3(\infty), 4(\infty), 5(\infty), 6(\infty)\}$.

| Iter | Extract | Settled $d$ | Relaxations | Queue State (dist only) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 (0) | $d[1]=0$ | $d[2]=7$, $d[3]=9$, $d[6]=14$ | $\{2(7), 3(9), 4(\infty), 5(\infty), 6(14)\}$ |
| 2 | 2 (7) | $d[2]=7$ | $d[4]=\min\{\infty, 7+15\}=22$ | $\{3(9), 4(22), 5(\infty), 6(14)\}$ |
| 3 | 3 (9) | $d[3]=9$ | $d[4]=\min\{22, 9+11\}=20$, $d[6]=\min\{14, 9+2\}=11$ | $\{4(20), 5(\infty), 6(11)\}$ |
| 4 | 6 (11) | $d[6]=11$ | $d[5]=\min\{\infty, 11+9\}=20$ | $\{4(20), 5(20)\}$ |
| 5 | 4 (20) | $d[4]=20$ | $d[5]=\min\{20, 20+6\}=20$ (no change) | $\{5(20)\}$ |
| 6 | 5 (20) | $d[5]=20$ | — | $\{\}$ |

**Final distances from vertex 1:**
$$
d[1]=0,\; d[2]=7,\; d[3]=9,\; d[4]=20,\; d[5]=20,\; d[6]=11
$$

**Shortest paths:**
- $1 \to 2$: cost 7
- $1 \to 3$: cost 9
- $1 \to 6$: cost 11 (via $1 \to 3 \to 6$)
- $1 \to 4$: cost 20 (via $1 \to 3 \to 4$)
- $1 \to 5$: cost 20 (via $1 \to 3 \to 6 \to 5$)

---

### Question B — Solution

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation [7 Marks]:**

The **Floyd–Warshall algorithm** (1962) solves the **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP)** problem using dynamic programming.

**Initialization:** Build matrix $D^{(0)}$ where $D^{(0)}[i][j] = w(i,j)$ if $(i,j) \in E$, $0$ if $i = j$, else $\infty$.

**Recurrence Relation:**

$$
D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\left\{ D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \right\}
$$

This says: the shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using intermediate vertices only from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ either does not pass through $k$ (first term), or uses $k$ as the last intermediate hop (second term).

**Time Complexity:** $O(V^3)$ — three nested loops of size $n$. **Space:** $O(V^2)$ (can be optimized in-place).

**Negative Cycle Detection:** After computing $D^{(n)}$, if any diagonal entry $D[i][i] < 0$, a negative cycle exists reachable from vertex $i$. This is because in a normal graph $D[i][i] = 0$, but a negative cycle reduces the self-distance below zero.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Initial Matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 9 & 1 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
\infty & 3 & 0 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & 4 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 1$ (intermediate vertex = 1):**
Outgoing from 1: $(1,2)=5$, $(1,3)=9$, $(1,4)=1$.
- $D[3][4]$: $\min\{\infty,\; \infty + 1\} = \infty$
- $D[4][2]$: $\min\{\infty,\; \infty + 5\} = \infty$

No improvements.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 9 & 1 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
\infty & 3 & 0 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & 4 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 2$ (intermediate vertex = 2):**
Outgoing from 2: $(2,4)=2$.
- $D[1][4]$: $\min\{1,\; 5 + 2\} = 1$ (no change)
- $D[3][4]$: $\min\{\infty,\; 3 + 2\} = 5$ **(Updated!)**

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 9 & 1 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
\infty & 3 & 0 & 5 \\
\infty & \infty & 4 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 3$ (intermediate vertex = 3):**
Outgoing from 3: $(3,2)=3$.
- $D[1][2]$: $\min\{5,\; 9 + 3\} = 5$ (no change)
- $D[4][2]$: $\min\{\infty,\; 4 + 3\} = 7$ **(Updated!)**
- $D[4][4]$: $\min\{0,\; 4 + \infty\} = 0$ (no change)

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 9 & 1 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
\infty & 3 & 0 & 5 \\
\infty & 7 & 4 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 4$ (intermediate vertex = 4):**
Outgoing from 4: $(4,3)=4$.
- $D[1][3]$: $\min\{9,\; 1 + 4\} = 5$ **(Updated!)**
- $D[2][3]$: $\min\{\infty,\; 2 + 4\} = 6$ **(Updated!)**
- $D[3][3]$: $\min\{0,\; 5 + 4\} = 0$ (no change)

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 5 & 1 \\
\infty & 0 & 6 & 2 \\
\infty & 3 & 0 & 5 \\
\infty & 7 & 4 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final All-Pairs Shortest Paths:**
- $d[1,2]=5$, $d[1,3]=5$, $d[1,4]=1$
- $d[2,3]=6$, $d[2,4]=2$
- $d[3,2]=3$, $d[3,4]=5$
- $d[4,2]=7$, $d[4,3]=4$

No negative cycle (all diagonals = 0). ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update predecessors** $\pi[v]$ during Dijkstra relaxation → path reconstruction becomes impossible (lose 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Stuck infinity values** in Floyd–Warshall: Students often write $\infty + \infty = \infty$ but forget to check this — proper handling needs explicit conditional checks.
> 3. **Mixing $k$ as vertex vs $k$ as iteration index** in Floyd–Warshall: Always clarify that $k$ denotes *vertex $k$* as an allowed intermediate.
> 4. **Not settling** in Dijkstra: Failing to mark extracted vertex as visited allows re-extraction → incorrect results.
> 5. **Wrong initial matrix**: Confusing row-major $(i, j)$ vs column-major $(j, i)$ indexing in matrix $D$.
> 6. **Omitting iteration tables**: In numerical questions, KTU examiners deduct 2–3 marks if step-by-step tables are missing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Summary

- **Dijkstra's Algorithm**:
  - Solves **SSSP** (single-source).
  - Uses **greedy** approach with a **priority queue**.
  - Requires **non-negative** edge weights.
  - Complexity: $O((V + E) \log V)$ with binary heap; $O(V^2)$ with array.
  - **Fails** on negative edge weights.
  - Produces a **shortest-path tree** rooted at source.
  - EDA use: global routing, net delay estimation.

- **Floyd–Warshall Algorithm**:
  - Solves **APSP** (all-pairs).
  - Uses **dynamic programming** via triple nested loop.
  - Handles **negative** weights (but not negative cycles).
  - Recurrence: $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\{D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\}$.
  - Complexity: $O(V^3)$ time, $O(V^2)$ space.
  - **Negative cycle check**: any diagonal entry $D[i][i] < 0$ after $k = n$.
  - EDA use: STA delay matrix, all-pairs signal propagation.

### Memory Aid for KTU Viva

- **D**ijkstra = **D**estination from **D**eparture (one source).
- **F**loyd–**W**arshall = **F**ull **W**orld shortest paths (all pairs).
- **Greedy** = Dijkstra's hammer; **DP** = Floyd's scalpel.

### Key Comparisons Table

| Aspect | Dijkstra | Floyd–Warshall |
|---|---|---|
| Problem Type | SSSP | APSSP |
| Strategy | Greedy | Dynamic Programming |
| Edge Weights | Non-negative only | Any (no negative cycles) |
| Complexity | $O((V+E)\log V)$ | $O(V^3)$ |
| Data Structure | Priority Queue | Distance Matrix |
| Path Reconstruction | Predecessor array $\pi[]$ | Predecessor matrix |
| Negative Cycle Handling | Not supported | Detected via diagonal |

### Final KTU Exam Tips

1. Always show **iteration tables** for Dijkstra numericals — examiners reward stepwise clarity.
2. For Floyd–Warshall, label each matrix $D^{(k)}$ clearly and **underline changed entries**.
3. Memorize the **recurrence relation** word-for-word — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 question.
4. Be ready to discuss **Bellman-Ford** as a comparison (handles negative weights, but slower at $O(VE)$).
5. In EDA context, mention **wire delay** or **congestion** as the weight interpretation for full marks on application sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Automation - VLSI Design Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Automation - VLSI Design Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VLSI Design Flow** is the *systematic, sequential sequence of engineering steps* used to transform an abstract **Integrated Circuit (IC)** specification into a *physically manufacturable silicon layout*. In the context of KTU's EDA framework, it is the *automation pipeline* that bridges *behavioural intent* and *fabrication-ready GDSII layout* through disciplined abstraction levels, formal verification, and tool-driven transformations.

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEAET741 – Electronic Design Automation** defines the VLSI Design Flow as the **end-to-end trajectory** covering:

1. **Specification & Feasibility**
2. **Architectural / High-Level Design**
3. **RTL (Register Transfer Level) Design**
4. **Functional / Dynamic Simulation**
5. **Logic Synthesis**
6. **Gate-Level Netlist Generation**
7. **Floorplanning & Partitioning**
8. **Placement**
9. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)**
10. **Routing**
11. **Physical Verification (DRC, LVS, ERC)**
12. **Timing Sign-off & Tape-out (GDSII)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Construction" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of designing a chip like building a skyscraper:**
>
> - **Specification** = The client saying "I need a 50-floor office tower with 200 parking slots."
> - **Architecture** = The structural engineer deciding where steel, glass, and concrete go.
> - **RTL Design** = Drawing the *blueprint* of each floor.
> - **Simulation** = Checking on a computer whether the blueprint will withstand storms and earthquakes.
> - **Logic Synthesis** = Converting blueprints into *executable construction plans* (lists of doors, beams, pipes).
> - **Floorplanning** = Laying out the construction site — *where* does the lobby go, where are the elevators?
> - **Placement** = Setting each brick and beam in its exact spot.
> - **Routing** = Connecting all the *electrical wires and water pipes* between elements.
> - **Verification (DRC/LVS)** = The municipal inspector checking *whether your building obeys city rules*.
> - **Tape-out** = Sending the final approved plan to the *factory (fab)* for mass construction.

Just as a skyscraper cannot skip from "client wish" to "concrete pour," a chip cannot jump from "specification" to "silicon wafer." Each step *must* be completed and verified before the next.

## 1.3 Why VLSI Design Flow is Critical in EDA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The complexity of modern chips exceeds **>10 billion transistors** (e.g., Apple M-Series, NVIDIA H100). Without a *structured, automated design flow*, manual design is mathematically impossible. Each step in the flow is a **specialized EDA tool** solving an **NP-hard optimization problem** (placement, routing, scheduling).

### 1.3.1 The Two Principal Design Philosophies

| Design Style | Full Form | Use Case | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ASIC** | Application-Specific Integrated Circuit | High-volume consumer products (smartphones, GPUs) | **High NRE, low unit cost** |
| **FPGA** | Field-Programmable Gate Array | Prototyping, low-volume, aerospace | **No NRE, higher unit cost** |
| **SoC** | System-on-Chip | Mixed-signal + digital + memory + CPU on one die | Very high complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost** is a one-time mask cost (often **$1M – $50M+** for advanced nodes like 5nm/3nm) paid only during ASIC design. This single fact drives the entire **design flow discipline** — *one mistake means re-spinning the masks*.

## 1.4 The Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn) — The Foundational Abstraction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Gajski-Kuhn Y-chart** (popularized by Daniel Gajski, 1983) is the *canonical reference* in KTU 2024 EDA. It shows that VLSI design moves through **three axes** of abstraction — *Behavioural, Structural,* and *Geometric* — across concentric rings representing design *domains* (System, Algorithm, RTL, Logic, Circuit, Layout).

![Y-Chart Visual Concept]

```
        BEHAVIOURAL AXIS  (what it does)
                ▲
                │
                │
   LAYOUT ◄─────┼─────► STRUCTURAL AXIS
   (geometry)    │       (interconnection)
                │
                ▼
        GEOMETRIC AXIS
        (how it looks physically)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Each ring of the Y-chart corresponds to a level of design abstraction** — and the *design flow* is essentially a *clockwise journey* from the *outer behavioural ring* to the *inner geometric ring*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Full VLSI Design Flow — Structured Logic Steps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme organizes the design flow into **two macro-phases**: **(A) Front-End Design** (behavioural/logical) and **(B) Back-End Design** (physical). Each step in the flow is a **distinct EDA tool with discrete inputs and outputs**.

### 2.1.1 Front-End Design (Logical Domain)

| Step | Input | Output | EDA Tool Category |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Specification** | Marketing / market need | *Functional spec document* | Document/Excel |
| **2. Architecture Design** | Spec doc | *Block diagram, data flow* | UML / MATLAB / C |
| **3. RTL Coding** | Architecture | *HDL code (Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog)* | VCS, QuestaSim |
| **4. Functional Simulation** | HDL code + Testbench | *Pass/Fail waveforms* | ModelSim, VCS |
| **5. Logic Synthesis** | RTL + Constraints (.sdc) | *Gate-level netlist (.v)* | Design Compiler, Genus |
| **6. Formal Verification** | RTL vs Netlist | *Equivalence check report* | Formality, Conformal |
| **7. Gate-Level Sim (GLS)** | Netlist + SDF | *Timing-aware simulation* | VCS, Xcelium |

### 2.1.2 Back-End Design (Physical Domain)

| Step | Input | Output | EDA Tool Category |
|---|---|---|---|
| **8. Floorplanning** | Netlist + .sdc | *Die size, macro placement* | Innovus, ICC2 |
| **9. Placement** | Floorplan + netlist | *Standard cell positions* | Innovus, ICC2 |
| **10. CTS** | Placed netlist | *Clock tree netlist* | Innovus, Clock Concurrent |
| **11. Routing** | Placed+CTS netlist | *Routed DEF* | Innovus, ICC2, Calibre |
| **12. Physical Verification** | GDSII | *DRC, LVS, ERC clean report* | Calibre, PVS, IC Validator |
| **13. Static Timing Analysis (STA)** | Netlist + SPEF | *Setup/Hold slack report* | PrimeTime, Tempus |
| **14. Power Analysis** | VCD + netlist | *Power report (avg/peak/leakage)* | PrimePower, Voltus |
| **15. Tape-out / GDSII** | Clean DB | *Fractured mask data* | Synopsys, Cadence |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> Each EDA step exists to *reduce one specific risk*. KTU expects students to articulate the **risk-reward** of each step.

- **Simulation** reduces *functional risk* (does it do the right thing?).
- **Synthesis** reduces *portability risk* (works across target libraries).
- **Floorplanning** reduces *timing congestion risk* (long wires = slow signals).
- **CTS** reduces *clock skew risk* (all flip-flops must see the edge simultaneously).
- **Routing** reduces *electromigration + IR drop risk* (wires have physical resistance).
- **DRC** reduces *fabrication risk* (does it obey foundry rules?).
- **LVS** reduces *connectivity risk* (does the layout *match* the schematic?).
- **STA** reduces *timing risk* (do all signals arrive before their deadline?).

## 2.3 The Y-Chart in Detail — Gajski's Domain Rings

| Ring (Outer→Inner) | Domain | Description | File/Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R6 — System** | Behavioural | Chip architecture, I/O planning | Spec doc |
| **R5 — Algorithm** | Behavioural | Algorithm partitioning | C/C++/MATLAB |
| **R4 — RTL** | Structural | Data flow between registers | Verilog/VHDL |
| **R3 — Logic** | Structural | Boolean equations, FSMs | Gate netlist |
| **R2 — Circuit** | Geometric | Transistor sizing, W/L ratios | SPICE |
| **R1 — Layout** | Geometric | Geometric polygons on silicon | GDSII, DEF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Movement along the Y-axis (outer → inner) = Increasing detail + Increasing cost of error.**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These are the *core analytical formulas* a KTU 2024 student must memorize for VLSI Design Flow questions. They govern timing, area, and power — the **three pillars** of design quality.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + T_{skew}$ | **Clock period constraint** (setup) | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $T_{hold} \leq T_{cq} + T_{comb} - T_{skew}$ | **Hold-time constraint** | s |
| 3 | $Slack_{setup} = T_{clk} - (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + T_{skew})$ | **Setup slack** (must be ≥ 0) | s |
| 4 | $Slack_{hold} = (T_{cq} + T_{comb}) - (T_{hold} + T_{skew})$ | **Hold slack** (must be ≥ 0) | s |
| 5 | $T_{comb} = N \cdot \tau_{inv}$ | **Combinational delay** (N gates, τ = FO4 delay) | s |
| 6 | $A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_{cell,i} + A_{macro} + A_{routing}$ | **Total silicon area** | µm² |
| 7 | $P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$ | **Total power** | W (watts) |
| 8 | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | **Dynamic power** (switching) | W |
| 9 | $P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | **Static leakage power** | W |
| 10 | $T_{gate} = \frac{k \cdot L^{2}}{\mu \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{t})^{\alpha}}$ | **Gate delay** (alpha-power law) | s |
| 11 | $FO4 = \frac{C_{inv} \cdot (V_{DD}/2)}{I_{on}}$ | **Fan-Out of 4 inverter delay** | s |
| 12 | $T_{skew} = \max(T_{clk \to FF_i}) - \min(T_{clk \to FF_j})$ | **Clock skew** | s |
| 13 | $Utilization = \frac{A_{used}}{A_{core}}$ | **Core utilization ratio** | unitless (0–1) |
| 14 | $F_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}}$ | **Maximum operating frequency** | Hz |
| 15 | $R_{wire} = R_{sq} \cdot \frac{L}{W} \cdot N_{vias}$ | **Wire resistance** | Ω |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Formula:** $P_{dynamic} \propto V_{DD}^{2}$ — this *quadratic* relationship is why modern chips aggressively scale voltage (e.g., from **1.2V → 0.75V**) to save power. This is a **favourite KTU numerical question**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the VLSI Design Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The VLSI design flow is the *backbone of the multi-billion dollar semiconductor industry*. Mastering it allows engineers to design chips for **AI accelerators (NVIDIA), mobile SoCs (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series), networking (Broadcom), automotive (Tesla FSD), and aerospace (Xilinx radiation-hardened FPGAs)**. Without EDA automation, none of these would be economically viable.

## 2.6 ASIC vs FPGA Flow — Key Differences

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 frequently asks comparison questions** between ASIC and FPGA flows. The fundamental difference is *where* the design is "frozen."

| Aspect | **ASIC Flow** | **FPGA Flow** |
|---|---|---|
| Final output | GDSII (sent to fab) | Bitstream (sent to FPGA) |
| Synthesis target | Standard-cell library (.lib) | FPGA primitives (LUT, FF, BRAM, DSP) |
| Place & Route | Innovus/ICC2 | Vivado, Quartus |
| Power | **Low** (custom cells) | **High** (programmable switches) |
| Performance | **High** (dedicated transistors) | **Medium** (LUT delays) |
| NRE Cost | **Very High** (millions $) | **Zero** (no masks) |
| Time-to-market | **6–18 months** | **Weeks** |
| Volume economy | Best for >100k units | Best for <10k units |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section builds the **three most important KTU numerical derivations** from the VLSI Design Flow. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — **no skipped transitions**.

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Setup & Hold Timing Equations from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a simple **flip-flop → combinational logic → flip-flop** pipeline. We derive the **clock period** and **hold constraints** that guarantee correct data capture.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Setup-Time Constraint

Let us define the variables rigorously:
- $T_{clk}$ = Clock period (the time between two rising edges of the same clock)
- $T_{cq}$ = Clock-to-Q delay of the launching flip-flop (time from clock edge to data output)
- $T_{comb}$ = Worst-case propagation delay through the combinational logic between two FFs
- $T_{su}$ = Setup time of the capturing flip-flop (data must be stable $T_{su}$ *before* the edge)
- $T_{skew}$ = Clock skew between the launching and capturing flip-flops

**Step 1 — Launch event:** The launching FF $FF_1$ releases new data at time $t = T_{cq}$ after its clock edge.

**Step 2 — Propagation:** The data travels through the combinational logic, taking $T_{comb}$ to arrive at $D_2$.

**Step 3 — Arrival time at $D_2$:** 
$$T_{arrival} = T_{cq} + T_{comb}$$

**Step 4 — Required time at $D_2$:** Data must be stable at the capturing FF $FF_2$ *at least* $T_{su}$ *before* its clock edge arrives. With a clock skew of $T_{skew}$ (delaying $FF_2$'s edge by $T_{skew}$), the required arrival time is:
$$T_{required} = T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su}$$

**Step 5 — Setup constraint** (arrival ≤ required):
$$T_{cq} + T_{comb} \leq T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for minimum clock period**:
$$T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - T_{skew}$$

**Step 7 — Conservative form (skew increases required period)**:
$$\boxed{T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + \vert T_{skew} \vert}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Hold-Time Constraint

The hold constraint ensures data is *not* overwritten too quickly. It is checked on the *same* clock edge (no $T_{clk}$ involved).

**Step 1 — Data must remain stable at $D_2$ for** $T_{hold}$ *after* the capturing clock edge.

**Step 2 — Data launched at $t = T_{cq}$, propagated over $T_{comb}$**:
$$T_{arrival}^{hold} = T_{cq} + T_{comb}$$

**Step 3 — Earliest capture edge at $FF_2$** (with skew $T_{skew}$ arriving early):
$$T_{capture}^{hold} = T_{skew} + T_{hold}$$

**Step 4 — Hold constraint** (data must arrive *after* the hold window):
$$T_{cq} + T_{comb} \geq T_{skew} + T_{hold}$$

**Step 5 — Rearranged (hold slack form)**:
$$\boxed{Slack_{hold} = (T_{cq} + T_{comb}) - (T_{hold} + T_{skew}) \geq 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Hold constraints are *independent of clock period* — they must be satisfied even when the clock is stopped. This is why hold violations can occur with *excessively fast* combinational paths.

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Dynamic Power vs Voltage — Why $V_{DD}$ Scaling is the Lever

### 3.2.1 Starting from the Energy of a Charging Capacitor

When a CMOS gate switches, it charges/discharges a load capacitance $C_L$ through the supply $V_{DD}$.

**Step 1 — Energy stored on a capacitor** charged from 0 to $V_{DD}$:
$$E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Energy dissipated in the PMOS pull-up network**:
$$E_{PMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated in the NMOS pull-down network**:
$$E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Total energy per switching event**:
$$E_{total} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — If the gate switches with activity factor $\alpha$** (probability of switching per clock cycle) at clock frequency $f$:

Number of transitions per second = $\alpha \cdot f$

**Step 6 — Dynamic power**:
$$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f}$$

### 3.2.2 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** $\alpha = 0.15$, $C_L = 50$ fF, $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $f = 1$ GHz.

**Step 7 — Compute**:
$$P = 0.15 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.0)^{2} \times 10^{9}$$

$$P = 0.15 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 7.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = 7.5 \text{ µW per gate}}$$

**Step 8 — Voltage scaling check:** If $V_{DD}$ is reduced to **0.7 V**:
$$P_{new} = 0.15 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (0.7)^{2} \times 10^{9}$$
$$P_{new} = 0.15 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.49 = 3.675 \text{ µW}$$

**Power reduction: ~51%** for a 30% voltage drop → **quadratic savings**.

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Wire Resistance & RC Delay in Routing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Routing is a **back-end EDA step**, but its *delay impact* is calculated using fundamental RC equations. KTU tests this in back-end flow questions.

### 3.3.1 Distributed RC Wire Model

A long wire of length $L$ is modeled as a **distributed RC ladder** of $N$ identical segments. Each segment has resistance $\Delta R = R_{sq} \cdot L/(N \cdot W)$ and capacitance $\Delta C = C_{sq} \cdot L \cdot W + C_{fringe}$.

**Step 1 — Elmore delay** for a distributed RC line driving an open circuit (no load):
$$T_{delay} = 0.5 \cdot R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire} = 0.5 \cdot \left(\frac{R_{sq} \cdot L}{W}\right) \cdot (C_{sq} \cdot L \cdot W + C_{fringe} \cdot L)$$

**Step 2 — Simplifying** (assuming $C_{fringe}$ is small):
$$T_{delay} \approx 0.5 \cdot R_{sq} \cdot C_{sq} \cdot L^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Key insight** (the **quadratic length dependence**):
$$\boxed{T_{wire} \propto L^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Implication for EDA tools:** The **placement** step *minimizes* $L$ for high-fanout nets. The **CTS** step ensures clock nets are *shielded* and *balanced* to keep $L$ short and uniform. The **routing** step assigns *upper metal layers* (thicker, lower R) to long routes.

### 3.3.2 KTU-Style Numerical: Wire Delay Estimation

**Given:** $R_{sq} = 0.08$ Ω/sq, $C_{sq} = 0.15$ fF/µm², $W = 0.1$ µm, $L = 100$ µm.

**Step 5 — Compute $R_{wire}$**:
$$R_{wire} = R_{sq} \cdot \frac{L}{W} = 0.08 \times \frac{100}{0.1} = 80 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $C_{wire}$**:
$$C_{wire} = C_{sq} \cdot L \cdot W = 0.15 \times 10^{-15} \times 100 \times 0.1 = 1.5 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Elmore delay**:
$$T_{delay} = 0.5 \times R_{wire} \times C_{wire} = 0.5 \times 80 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$T_{delay} = 60 \text{ ps}$$

$$\boxed{T_{wire} = 60 \text{ picoseconds}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Flow Steps to EDA Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **KTU 2024 cognitive-level application** of the design flow — connecting *what step* uses *which algorithm*.

| Flow Step | Core Algorithmic Problem | Algorithm Used | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synthesis | Boolean mapping to cell library | Technology mapping + DAG covering | Polynomial |
| Floorplanning | Macro placement optimization | Simulated annealing, slicing trees | NP-hard |
| Placement | Standard cell legalization | Min-cut, force-directed, quadratic | NP-hard |
| CTS | Skew minimization, balanced tree | H-tree, fishbone, deferred merge | Heuristic |
| Routing | Wire assignment on grid | Maze (Lee's), A*, multi-commodity flow | NP-hard |
| DRC | Geometric rule checking | Polygon intersection, scanline | O(n log n) |
| LVS | Graph isomorphism | Subgraph matching (Ullmann) | NP-complete |
| STA | Path-based delay propagation | PERT, topological sort | Polynomial |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **"VLSI Design Flow."** List any **four** major steps involved in the back-end (physical) design of an ASIC.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VLSI Design Flow** is the *structured sequence of design and verification steps* that transforms an abstract integrated circuit specification into a *physically realizable layout* (GDSII) ready for fabrication. It integrates *behavioural modelling, logical synthesis, physical implementation,* and *sign-off verification* through EDA tools.

**Four major back-end (physical) design steps:**
1. **Floorplanning** — placement of hard macros, I/O pads, and core rows.
2. **Placement** — assignment of standard cells to specific geometric locations.
3. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** — building a balanced clock distribution network.
4. **Routing** — assignment of metal interconnect wires between cells.

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between **DRC** and **LVS** checks in the VLSI physical verification flow. State one tool used for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **DRC (Design Rule Check)** | **LVS (Layout vs Schematic)** |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Verify *geometry* obeys foundry rules | Verify *connectivity* matches schematic |
| Checks | Min width, spacing, enclosure, density | Device count, pin matching, net matching |
| Output | DRC clean report (errors per layer) | LVS clean report (matched/unmatched) |
| Tool example | **Synopsys IC Validator, Mentor Calibre** | **Cadence PVS, Mentor Calibre** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q3.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of the **Gajski-Kuhn Y-chart**, explain the **three axes** and **four concentric domains** of VLSI design abstraction. Discuss how a design *traverses* the Y-chart from specification to layout. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Draw a complete **block diagram of the ASIC design flow** and explain the functions of **Floorplanning, Placement, CTS, Routing, and Physical Verification.** State one EDA tool for each step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart Explanation:**

**The Three Axes:**
- **Behavioural axis (Y-axis):** Describes *what* the design does — algorithms, specifications, and functional behaviour.
- **Structural axis (X-axis):** Describes *how* the design is constructed from interconnected components (gates, blocks).
