- **Geometric axis (Z-axis):** Describes the *physical shape, position,* and *interconnection* of components on silicon.

**The Four Concentric Domains (per KTU 2024 simplified model):**

1. **System level (outermost):** High-level architecture, I/O planning, chip partitioning.
2. **Algorithmic/RTL level:** Data flow description, register-transfer operations.
3. **Logic/Gate level:** Boolean expressions, FSMs, optimized gate netlists.
4. **Circuit/Layout level (innermost):** Transistor sizing, RC extraction, geometric polygons.

**Design Traversal:** A design begins at the *outer behavioural ring* (system specification) and moves *inward* through *synthesis* (behavioural → structural) and *physical design* (structural → geometric). Each inward step introduces *more detail* and *more cost of error*.

**(b) ASIC Design Flow Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above) and tool mapping:**

| Step | Function | EDA Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Floorplanning | Define die area, place macros, allocate power/clock regions | **Cadence Innovus** |
| Placement | Position standard cells to minimize wirelength and congestion | **Synopsys ICC2** |
| CTS | Build balanced H-tree to minimize clock skew | **Cadence Clock Concurrent Opt.** |
| Routing | Global + detailed metal interconnect assignment | **Cadence Innovus / Calibre sign-off** |
| Physical Verification | DRC, LVS, ERC checks against foundry rules | **Mentor Calibre** |

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q4.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Compare the **ASIC design flow** with the **FPGA design flow** in terms of steps, output, NRE cost, and target applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L = 100$ fF operates at $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, clock frequency $f = 500$ MHz, with switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$. Calculate:
>   - (i) The **dynamic power consumption** of the gate.
>   - (ii) The **new dynamic power** if $V_{DD}$ is scaled down to **0.85 V** (assume $C_L$, $\alpha$, $f$ unchanged).
>   - (iii) Comment on the **percentage power saving** obtained. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ASIC vs FPGA Design Flow Comparison** (refer to Section 2.6 table in this guide for the complete matrix).

| Parameter | ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|
| Output | GDSII → foundry | Bitstream → FPGA device |
| Synthesis target | Standard-cell library | LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, DSPs |
| NRE | High (mask cost > $1M) | Zero |
| Per-unit cost | Low (for high volume) | Higher (FPGA silicon cost) |
| Time-to-market | 6–18 months | Weeks |
| Best for | High-volume (>100k units) | Prototyping, low-volume |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $C_L = 100$ fF = $100 \times 10^{-15}$ F, $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $f = 500$ MHz = $500 \times 10^{6}$ Hz, $\alpha = 0.2$.

**(i) Original dynamic power:**
$$P_1 = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$
$$P_1 = 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.2)^{2} \times 500 \times 10^{6}$$
$$P_1 = 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 500 \times 10^{6}$$
$$P_1 = 0.2 \times 1.44 \times 500 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$P_1 = 144 \times 10^{-7} = 1.44 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{P_1 = 14.4 \text{ µW}}$$

**(ii) New dynamic power at $V_{DD} = 0.85$ V:**
$$P_2 = 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times (0.85)^{2} \times 500 \times 10^{6}$$
$$P_2 = 0.2 \times 0.7225 \times 500 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$P_2 = 0.2 \times 0.7225 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$P_2 = 7.225 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{P_2 = 7.225 \text{ µW}}$$

**(iii) Percentage power saving:**
$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1} \times 100$$
$$= \frac{14.4 - 7.225}{14.4} \times 100$$
$$= \frac{7.175}{14.4} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{\approx 49.83\% \text{ power saving}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** Voltage scaling from 1.2 V → 0.85 V (a ~29% reduction) yields nearly **50% dynamic power savings** because $P_{dynamic} \propto V_{DD}^{2}$. This is the *engineering rationale* behind **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** in modern SoCs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout
> 1. **Forgetting to square $V_{DD}$:** Many students write $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD} f$ — this is **WRONG** and loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Always convert fF → F (multiply by $10^{-15}$) and MHz → Hz (multiply by $10^{6}$). The result is in **Watts**.
> 3. **Confusing DRC vs LVS:** DRC is *geometry*, LVS is *connectivity*. Mixing them loses 2–3 marks in comparison questions.
> 4. **Y-chart axes:** The three axes are **Behavioural, Structural, Geometric** — *not* "front-end, back-end, system" (a common student error).
> 5. **Synthesis ≠ Place & Route:** Synthesis produces a *netlist* (logical), P&R produces *DEF/GDSII* (physical). Conflating them is a frequent mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the KTU 2024 EDA exam on "Design Automation — VLSI Design Flow":**

- ✅ The VLSI design flow is **two macro-phases**: *front-end (logical)* and *back-end (physical)*.
- ✅ The **Y-chart (Gajski-Kuhn)** has three axes: *Behavioural, Structural, Geometric* and concentric domains from *System → Algorithm → RTL → Logic → Circuit → Layout*.
- ✅ **Front-end outputs:** RTL code → Netlist (`.v`) → SDF for timing.
- ✅ **Back-end outputs:** DEF (placed/routed) → SPEF (parasitics) → GDSII (tapeout).
- ✅ **Logic Synthesis** = translation + mapping + optimization, producing a **gate-level netlist** from RTL.
- ✅ **Floorplanning** decides *die size, aspect ratio, macro placement, and pin assignment*.
- ✅ **Placement** minimizes **wirelength** and **congestion** using algorithms like *min-cut* and *simulated annealing*.
- ✅ **CTS (Clock Tree Synthesis)** minimizes **clock skew** using *H-tree* and *fishbone* topologies.
- ✅ **Routing** has two phases: *Global routing* (path planning) and *Detailed routing* (actual wire assignment).
- ✅ **DRC** checks *geometry* against foundry rules; **LVS** checks *connectivity* against the schematic.
- ✅ **STA (Static Timing Analysis)** verifies setup/hold slacks across all paths; uses PERT-based propagation.
- ✅ The **setup constraint** is $T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + T_{skew}$.
- ✅ The **hold constraint** is $(T_{cq} + T_{comb}) \geq T_{hold} + T_{skew}$ (independent of $T_{clk}$).
- ✅ **Dynamic power** $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ — **quadratic in $V_{DD}$**.
- ✅ **Static power** $P_{static} = I_{leak} V_{DD}$ — increases exponentially with temperature.
- ✅ **ASIC flow** ends in GDSII → foundry; **FPGA flow** ends in a bitstream → device.
- ✅ **EDA tool leaders:** Synopsys (DC, ICC2, PrimeTime), Cadence (Genus, Innovus, Conformal), Mentor/Siemens (Calibre, ModelSim).
- ✅ **NRE cost** for advanced ASICs (5nm/3nm) can exceed **$50M** — emphasizing the *cost discipline* of the design flow.
- ✅ **Tapeout** is the *final irreversible step* — all prior verification *must* be clean.
- ✅ **GDSII** is the *industry-standard* database format for mask-making (replaced earlier CIF and EDIF formats).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VLSI Design Styles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** VLSI Design Styles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**VLSI Design Styles** refer to the systematic methodologies and structural approaches adopted to translate a system-level specification (behavioral/functional) into a physical integrated circuit (IC) layout. Each design style represents a unique trade-off axis balancing **performance**, **silicon area (density)**, **power dissipation**, **Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) cost**, **per-unit fabrication cost**, **time-to-market**, and **design flexibility**.

In the KTU 2024 EDA context, VLSI design styles are classified along a spectrum ranging from **fully manual layout (Full Custom)** to **fully programmable silicon (FPGA)**, with several intermediate semi-custom and structured approaches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The module specifically evaluates your understanding of design classification, the design hierarchy (cell, block, chip), the choice of design style based on application constraints, and the corresponding EDA tool chain required for each style. Mastering the comparative trade-offs is **essential** for 14-mark Part B questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Construction" Metaphor

Imagine you want to build a house. You have several options:

| Real-World Analogy | VLSI Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hiring an architect who designs every brick, pipe, and wire from scratch (slow, expensive, unique) | **Full Custom Design** |
| Buying pre-made room modules (kitchen, bedroom) and assembling them on your plot | **Standard Cell Design** |
| Buying a pre-fabricated frame with empty slots, and only customizing the top-floor connections (wiring) | **Gate Array / Sea of Gates** |
| Buying a fully pre-built house where you only rearrange furniture and change software | **FPGA / Programmable Logic** |

The "expense" of architecture maps to **NRE cost (mask cost)**, "construction time" maps to **time-to-market**, "uniqueness" maps to **performance/area optimization**, and "rearranging furniture" maps to **programming/configuration flexibility**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Snapshot:**
> - **NRE Cost:** One-time cost paid to the foundry for fabricating the photolithographic masks. Can range from **$0** (FPGA) to **>$1 Million** (Full Custom at 7nm/5nm).
> - **Time-to-Market:** The wall-clock duration from specification to working silicon. Critical for consumer electronics.
> - **Yield:** The percentage of fabricated chips that function correctly on a wafer. Higher density ⇒ Lower yield typically.
> - **Channel Length ($\lambda$):** The KTU-standard scalable design rule parameter. Actual feature dimensions are integer multiples of $\lambda$.

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Design Philosophies

Every VLSI design style belongs to one of these three parent philosophies:

1. **Custom Design (Bottom-Up, Geometry-Driven):** Designer controls every polygon on every mask layer. Maximum performance, minimum area, maximum effort.
2. **Semi-Custom Design (Cell-Based, Compromise):** Pre-characterized logic cells are placed and routed automatically by EDA tools. Balanced trade-off.
3. **Programmable Design (Top-Down, Configuration-Driven):** The silicon is generic and fixed; only an on-chip configuration bitstream is changed. Minimum effort, minimum performance per watt.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "which design style is best?", there is **no universal answer**. The answer must reference the *application* (e.g., "FPGA is best for prototyping and low-volume production", "Full Custom is best for high-volume ASICs like smartphone SoCs").
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of VLSI Design Styles

Below is the exhaustive list of design styles, ranked by customization level (most custom → most programmable).

### Style 1: Full Custom Design
- **Concept:** Every transistor, every interconnect, and every layer is handcrafted by a layout engineer.
- **Used In:** Microprocessor cores, analog/RF blocks, memory sense amplifiers, ultra-high-performance CPUs/GPUs.
- **Tools:** Custom layout editors (Cadence Virtuoso, Synopsys Custom Compiler), manual DRC/LVS.
- **Advantages:** Highest performance, smallest area, lowest power.
- **Disadvantages:** Very high NRE cost, long design cycle, error-prone.

### Style 2: Standard Cell Design (Cell-Based Design — CBD)
- **Concept:** A library of pre-designed, pre-characterized logic gates (NAND, NOR, DFF, MUX, full adder, etc.) at fixed height is provided. EDA tools (place-and-route) automatically arrange these cells and route them.
- **Used In:** Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs), SoC design, almost all modern digital chips.
- **Key Concept — "Row-Based Placement":** Cells share a common **power rail (VDD/GND)** at the top and bottom, with internal routing channels between rows.
- **Advantages:** Good balance of performance/area/effort, scalable, predictable timing.
- **Disadvantages:** Still requires mask fabrication (NRE cost), longer than FPGA prototyping.

### Style 3: Gate Array Design (Mask-Programmable)
- **Concept:** The foundry pre-fabricates a "master slice" containing a regular array of uncommitted NAND/NOR gates (or transistors) on the wafer. The customer provides only the **metal interconnect masks** to personalize the logic.
- **Sub-types:** Channeled Gate Array (dedicated routing channels) and **Channel-less / Sea-of-Gates** (routing over the cell area).
- **Used In:** Mid-volume ASICs, legacy designs, prototyping.
- **Advantages:** Lower NRE than Full Custom (only metal masks needed), faster turnaround.
- **Disadvantages:** Fixed transistor array ⇒ area wastage; performance limited by fixed base.

### Style 4: Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)
- **Concept:** The silicon contains an array of **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)**, **programmable interconnects**, and **I/O blocks**. Logic is defined by an SRAM/Anti-fuse/Flash bitstream loaded at power-up or one-time.
- **Used In:** Prototyping, low-volume production, aerospace, military, AI inference accelerators (now high-end FPGAs rival ASICs in throughput).
- **Advantages:** Zero NRE cost, infinite reprogrammability, fastest time-to-market.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher per-unit cost, lower clock speed (typ. 100–500 MHz), 10–20× larger area than equivalent ASIC, higher dynamic power.

### Style 5: Programmable Logic Devices (PLD / CPLD)
- **Concept:** Simpler than FPGA, uses AND/OR plane logic with macrocells. Typically used for glue logic and simple state machines.
- **Used In:** Interface bridging, boot logic, simple control.

### Style 6: Structured ASIC (e.g., eASIC, HardCopy)
- **Concept:** Hybrid — a pre-fabricated base (like Gate Array) with one or two custom via layers to personalize routing.
- **Used In:** Cost-sensitive volume production (between FPGA and Standard Cell ASIC).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step in the Design Hierarchy

When implementing any VLSI chip, the design flows through a strict hierarchy:

1. **System Specification** → What must the chip do? Throughput, latency, power budget, cost ceiling.
2. **Behavioral Description** → Written in HDL (Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog) or high-level C/C++/SystemC.
3. **RTL Design** → The HDL is synthesized into a Register-Transfer Level netlist.
4. **Logic Synthesis** → Maps RTL to gates from a standard cell library.
5. **Floorplanning** → Macro placement, power grid design, I/O pad assignment.
6. **Placement** → Cell-by-cell positioning on the die.
7. **Routing** → Metal layers connect the placed cells.
8. **Physical Verification** → DRC (Design Rule Check), LVS (Layout vs. Schematic), ERC, Antenna checks.
9. **Sign-off** → Static Timing Analysis (STA), Power analysis, IR drop, Electromigration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this hierarchy matter for choosing a design style?**
> - In **FPGA design**, the designer stops at Step 3 (synthesis) → bitstream generation. The rest is "baked into the FPGA silicon."
> - In **Standard Cell ASIC**, the designer (or EDA tool) goes all the way to Step 9 — masks are then sent to a foundry (TSMC, Samsung, Intel Foundry).
> - In **Full Custom**, even the standard cells themselves are redesigned at the polygon level.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY FORMULAS — Memorize for ESE & Internal Assessments**

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Dynamic Power (Switching Power)** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity (0 to 1), $C_L$ = load capacitance (F), $V_{DD}$ = supply (V), $f$ = clock freq (Hz) |
| **Static Power (Leakage)** | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | $I_{leak}$ = subthreshold + gate leakage current (A) |
| **Total Power** | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static} + P_{short\_ckt}$ | Sum of all dissipation mechanisms |
| **Gate Delay (RC Model)** | $t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ | First-order Elmore delay approximation |
| **Intrinsic Delay** | $t_{int} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_{int}$ | Delay due to gate's own parasitic capacitance |
| **Propagation Delay (Inverter)** | $t_{PHL} = \frac{C_L}{K_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$ | High-to-Low transition (NMOS drives) |
| **Energy per Transition** | $E = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Energy drawn from supply per 0→1→0 cycle |
| **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_{pd}$ | Quality metric (Joules); lower is better |
| **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_{pd}$ | Stricter metric penalizing slow designs |
| **Total NRE Cost** | $Cost_{NRE} = N_{masks} \cdot Cost_{mask} + Engineering$ | Independent of volume |
| **Per-Unit Cost** | $Cost_{unit} = \frac{Cost_{NRE}}{Volume} + Cost_{fab}$ | Decreases with volume |
| **Design Rule Scaling (Lambda)** | $L_{min} = 2\lambda$, $W_{min} = 2\lambda$ | KTU scalable design rules (Mead-Conway) |
| **Channel Density** | $\rho_{ch} = \frac{\text{Routing Tracks}}{\text{Channel Height}}$ | Measures routing congestion in gate arrays |
| **Rent's Rule (Wire Length Estimation)** | $T = k \cdot N^p$ | $T$ = external terminals, $N$ = blocks, $k, p$ = empirical constants ($0.5 < p < 0.75$) |
| **Yield (Seeds' Model)** | $Y = e^{-\sqrt{A \cdot D}}$ | $A$ = chip area, $D$ = defect density |
| **Silicon Area (Sum of Cells)** | $A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{cell,i} + A_{interconnect}$ | Cells + routing overhead |
| **Clock Frequency Bound** | $f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{clk-to-Q} + t_{logic} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}}$ | Determines achievable throughput |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smartphone SoCs (Apple A18, Snapdragon):** Use a mix — **Full Custom** for CPU/GPU cores, **Standard Cell** for peripherals, and embedded **FPGA-like eFPGA** blocks in some industrial chips.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** FPGAs dominate (radiation-hardened FPGAs from Xilinx/Microchip) due to in-field reprogrammability and zero NRE.
- **AI Accelerators (Google TPU, NVIDIA):** **Full Custom + Standard Cell** hybrid for maximum TOPS/Watt.
- **IoT/Edge Devices:** Often use **Structured ASIC** or **CPLD** to balance cost and flexibility.
- **Education/Prototyping:** FPGA boards (ZedBoard, DE10-Nano) are the de-facto standard.

## 2.5 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Full Custom | Standard Cell | Gate Array | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NRE Cost** | Very High | High | Medium | Zero (no masks) |
| **Per-Unit Cost** | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| **Time-to-Market** | Longest | Medium | Short | Shortest |
| **Performance (Speed)** | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |
| **Density (Area)** | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |
| **Power Efficiency** | Best | Good | Medium | Worst |
| **Flexibility** | None (fixed) | None (fixed) | None (fixed) | Infinite |
| **EDA Tool Complexity** | Maximum | High | Medium | High (synthesis focus) |
| **Typical Application** | CPU cores, analog | ASICs, SoCs | Legacy ASICs | Prototyping, low-volume |
| **Design Effort (Engineer-Hours)** | 100% | 30–50% | 20–30% | 10–20% |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the exhaustive, step-by-step mathematical and logical derivations a KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark Part B question. Every algebraic transition is shown.

## 3.1 Derivation: Dynamic Power Dissipation in CMOS

**Given:** A CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L = 50 \text{ fF}$ at $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$, with clock frequency $f = 500 \text{ MHz}$ and switching activity $\alpha = 0.15$.

**Find:** The dynamic power dissipated and energy per switching event.

### Step 1: Energy Stored in the Capacitor During Charging (0 → 1)

When the output node transitions from logic 0 to logic 1, the PMOS transistor connects the load capacitance to $V_{DD}$. The energy drawn from the supply is:

$$E_{drawn} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV = C_L \cdot \frac{V_{DD}^2}{2}$$

**Reasoning:** The voltage across the capacitor rises from 0 to $V_{DD}$, and the current flowing charges the capacitor. Integrating $V \cdot I$ over the charge time yields the result.

### Step 2: Energy Stored in the Capacitor at Logic 1

At the end of charging, the capacitor holds energy:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Reasoning:** Standard formula for energy stored in a capacitor $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.

### Step 3: Energy Dissipated in the PMOS Transistor During Charging

The difference between drawn and stored energy is dissipated as heat in the PMOS:

$$E_{dissipated, PMOS} = E_{drawn} - E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Reasoning:** Half the energy is always lost to heat, regardless of transistor size — a fundamental CMOS property.

### Step 4: Energy During Discharging (1 → 0)

When the output switches from 1 to 0, the NMOS discharges the capacitor to ground. The stored energy $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated as heat in the NMOS. No energy is returned to the supply (unlike an inductor).

$$E_{dissipated, NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 5: Total Energy per Full Switching Cycle (0→1→0)

$$E_{cycle} = E_{dissipated, PMOS} + E_{dissipated, NMOS} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Conclusion:** The classic CMOS result — every complete 0→1→0 transition dissipates $C_L V_{DD}^2$ joules of energy.

### Step 6: Power (Energy × Frequency)

Multiplying by the number of switching events per second, $\alpha \cdot f$:

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot E_{cycle} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

### Step 7: Numerical Substitution

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn} &= 0.15 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.2)^2 \times 500 \times 10^6 \\
&= 0.15 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 5 \times 10^8 \\
&= 0.15 \times 50 \times 1.44 \times 5 \times 10^{-15+8} \\
&= 0.15 \times 360 \times 10^{-7} \\
&= 54 \times 10^{-7} \text{ W} \\
&= 5.4 \text{ \mu W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $P_{dyn} = 5.4 \text{ \mu W}$

### Step 8: Energy per Transition

$$E_{cycle} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.2)^2 = 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 = 72 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 72 \text{ fJ}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost Trade-Off Analysis (NRE vs. Volume)

**Given:** 
- Full Custom ASIC: NRE = ₹5,00,00,000 (₹5 Crore), Per-unit fab cost = ₹100
- FPGA: NRE = ₹0, Per-unit cost (FPGA chip + PCB) = ₹8,000
- Standard Cell ASIC: NRE = ₹50,00,000 (₹50 Lakh), Per-unit fab cost = ₹300
- Gate Array: NRE = ₹20,00,000 (₹20 Lakh), Per-unit fab cost = ₹500

**Find:** The break-even volume where each option becomes cheaper.

### Step 1: Formulate the Total Cost Equation

$$C_{total}(V) = C_{NRE} + V \cdot C_{unit}$$

Where $V$ = production volume, $C_{NRE}$ = non-recurring engineering cost, $C_{unit}$ = per-unit cost.

### Step 2: Break-Even Between FPGA and Standard Cell ASIC

Set the total costs equal:

$$\begin{aligned}
0 + V \cdot 8000 &= 50,00,000 + V \cdot 300 \\
8000V - 300V &= 50,00,000 \\
7700V &= 50,00,000 \\
V &= \frac{50,00,000}{7700} \approx 649.35
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** For $V \geq 650$ units, Standard Cell ASIC is cheaper than FPGA. Below 650 units, FPGA wins.

### Step 3: Break-Even Between Standard Cell and Full Custom

$$\begin{aligned}
50,00,000 + 300V &= 5,00,00,000 + 100V \\
200V &= 4,50,00,000 \\
V &= 22,500
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** For $V \geq 22,500$ units, Full Custom ASIC becomes cost-optimal.

### Step 4: Break-Even Between Gate Array and Standard Cell

$$\begin{aligned}
20,00,000 + 500V &= 50,00,000 + 300V \\
200V &= 30,00,000 \\
V &= 15,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Gate Array beats Standard Cell between 650 and 15,000 units — its "sweet spot."

### Step 5: Volume-Based Decision Matrix (Summary)

| Volume Range | Optimal Choice |
|---|---|
| 1 – 649 units | **FPGA** |
| 650 – 14,999 units | **Gate Array** |
| 15,000 – 22,499 units | **Standard Cell** |
| $\geq 22,500$ units | **Full Custom** |

## 3.3 Derivation: Rent's Rule for Wire Length Estimation

**Given:** A VLSI chip is partitioned into $N = 64$ functional blocks. Empirical constants: $k = 4.0$, $p = 0.6$.

**Find:** The number of external I/O terminals $T$.

### Step 1: Apply Rent's Rule

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= k \cdot N^p \\
T &= 4.0 \cdot (64)^{0.6} \\
T &= 4.0 \cdot (2^6)^{0.6} \\
T &= 4.0 \cdot 2^{3.6} \\
T &= 4.0 \cdot 12.126 \\
T &= 48.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Rounding:** $T \approx 49$ terminals.

**Engineering Interpretation:** A chip with 64 internal blocks requires approximately 49 external I/O pins, providing insight into package selection and routing complexity.

## 3.4 Derivation: RC Delay in Interconnect (Elmore Approximation)

**Given:** A distributed interconnect modeled as $n = 4$ identical RC segments, each with $R = 50 \text{ }\Omega$ and $C = 20 \text{ fF}$. Driver resistance $R_d = 100 \text{ }\Omega$.

**Find:** The Elmore delay from input to the end of the line.

### Step 1: Elmore Delay Formula

The Elmore delay for a chain of $n$ RC segments is:

$$t_d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{i,\to source} \cdot C_i$$

Where $R_{i,\to source}$ is the total resistance from the source to node $i$.

### Step 2: Compute Resistance to Each Capacitor Node

For a uniform ladder with $R_d$ driver and segments $R, C$ each:

- To $C_1$: $R_{to,C_1} = R_d = 100 \text{ }\Omega$
- To $C_2$: $R_{to,C_2} = R_d + R = 100 + 50 = 150 \text{ }\Omega$
- To $C_3$: $R_{to,C_3} = R_d + 2R = 100 + 100 = 200 \text{ }\Omega$
- To $C_4$: $R_{to,C_4} = R_d + 3R = 100 + 150 = 250 \text{ }\Omega$

### Step 3: Sum the Contributions

$$\begin{aligned}
t_d &= R_{to,C_1} \cdot C + R_{to,C_2} \cdot C + R_{to,C_3} \cdot C + R_{to,C_4} \cdot C \\
&= (100 + 150 + 200 + 250) \times 20 \times 10^{-15} \\
&= 700 \times 20 \times 10^{-15} \\
&= 14,000 \times 10^{-15} \text{ s} \\
&= 14 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The signal takes approximately 14 ps to propagate to the end of this 4-segment interconnect — a key metric in STA sign-off.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Style vs. Application

| Application Domain | Optimal Design Style | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Smartphone SoC (millions of units) | **Full Custom + Standard Cell** | Lowest per-unit cost at extreme volumes |
| 5G Baseband Modem | **Full Custom** | Strict RF performance, low power |
| Aerospace Flight Computer | **FPGA (Rad-Hard)** | Field reconfigurability, reliability |
| IoT Sensor Node | **Standard Cell ASIC or Structured ASIC** | Low power, low cost at moderate volume |
| ASIC Prototyping | **FPGA** | Fast iteration, zero NRE |
| Educational Lab Kit | **FPGA** | Reprogrammability for student experiments |
| Legacy Industrial Controller | **Gate Array** | Mature, stable, medium volume |
| AI Training Accelerator (TPU-class) | **Full Custom** | Maximum TOPS/Watt |
| USB-to-UART Bridge Chip | **CPLD / Structured ASIC** | Simple glue logic, low cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use Mermaid syntax with strict adherence to the Alpha Rule for node IDs and clean uppercase alphanumeric labels inside double quotes.

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of VLSI Design Styles

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["VLSI DESIGN STYLES"] --> nodeB["FULL CUSTOM DESIGN"]
    rootA --> nodeC["SEMI CUSTOM DESIGN"]
    rootA --> nodeD["PROGRAMMABLE DESIGN"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["TRANSISTOR LEVEL LAYOUT"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["CELL LEVEL LAYOUT"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["APPLICATION CPU GPU ANALOG RF"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["STANDARD CELL ASIC"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["GATE ARRAY"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["SEA OF GATES"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["STRUCTURED ASIC"]

    nodeC1 --> nodeC1A["ROW BASED PLACEMENT"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1B["PRE DESIGNED CELL LIBRARY"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1C["AUTOMATIC P AND R"]

    nodeC2 --> nodeC2A["CHANNELED"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2B["CHANNEL LESS"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2C["METAL CUSTOMIZATION ONLY"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["FPGA"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["CPLD"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["PLD"]

    nodeD1 --> nodeD1A["SRAM BASED"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1B["FLASH BASED"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1C["ANTI FUSE BASED"]

    nodeD1 --> nodeD1X["CLB SWITCH MATRIX IOB DSP BRAM"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2A["AND OR PLANE MACROCELLS"]
```

## 4.2 VLSI Design Flow — Common to All Styles (with Style-Specific Branches)

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["SYSTEM SPECIFICATION"] --> stepB["BEHAVIORAL DESCRIPTION HDL"]
    stepB --> stepC["RTL SYNTHESIS"]
    stepC --> stepD{"DESIGN STYLE CHOSEN"}

    stepD --> stepE1["FPGA PATH BITSTREAM GENERATION"]
    stepD --> stepE2["SEMI CUSTOM PATH LOGIC SYNTHESIS"]
    stepD --> stepE3["FULL CUSTOM PATH CUSTOM LAYOUT"]

    stepE1 --> stepF1["DOWNLOAD TO FPGA BOARD"]
    stepF1 --> stepF1X["TEST AND VALIDATE"]

    stepE2 --> stepF2["STANDARD CELL PLACEMENT"]
    stepF2 --> stepF2B["ROUTING"]
    stepF2B --> stepF2C["DRC LVS STA"]
    stepF2C --> stepF2D["MASK FABRICATION AT FOUNDRY"]
    stepF2D --> stepF2E["PACKAGING AND TEST"]

    stepE3 --> stepF3["TRANSISTOR SIZING"]
    stepF3 --> stepF3B["HAND LAYOUT POLYGON EDITING"]
    stepF3B --> stepF3C["PARASITIC EXTRACTION"]
    stepF3C --> stepF3D["POST LAYOUT SIMULATION"]
    stepF3D --> stepF3E["MASK TAPEOUT"]
    stepF3E --> stepF3F["FOUNDRY FABRICATION"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level FPGA Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    blkA["CONFIGURABLE LOGIC BLOCK CLB"] --> blkA1["LOOKUP TABLE LUT"]
    blkA --> blkA2["FLIP FLOP FF"]
    blkA --> blkA3["MULTIPLEXER MUX"]

    blkB["PROGRAMMABLE INTERCONNECT"] --> blkB1["HORIZONTAL SWITCH MATRIX"]
    blkB --> blkB2["VERTICAL SWITCH MATRIX"]
    blkB --> blkB3["CONNECTION BOXES"]

    blkC["I O BLOCK IOB"] --> blkC1["INPUT BUFFER"]
    blkC --> blkC2["OUTPUT BUFFER"]
    blkC --> blkC3["TRI STATE CONTROL"]

    blkD["EMBEDDED HARDWARE"] --> blkD1["BLOCK RAM BRAM"]
    blkD --> blkD2["DSP SLICES"]
    blkD --> blkD3["PLL CLOCK MANAGER"]
    blkD --> blkD4["TRANSCEIVERS"]

    blkE["CONFIGURATION MEMORY SRAM"] --> blkE1["BITSTREAM LOADED AT POWER UP"]

    blkA <--> blkB
    blkC <--> blkB
    blkD <--> blkB
    blkE1 -.CONFIGURES.-> blkA
    blkE1 -.CONFIGURES.-> blkB
    blkE1 -.CONFIGURES.-> blkC
```

## 4.4 Standard Cell ASIC — Physical Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    row1["ROW 1 VDD GND RAILS"] --> cell1A["CELL NAND2"]
    row1 --> cell1B["CELL DFF"]
    row1 --> cell1C["CELL MUX2"]

    row2["ROW 2 VDD GND RAILS"] --> cell2A["CELL INV"]
    row2 --> cell2B["CELL XOR"]
    row2 --> cell2C["CELL BUF"]

    channel1["ROUTING CHANNEL METAL 1 AND METAL 2"] -.WIRES.-> cell1A
    channel1 -.WIRES.-> cell1B
    channel1 -.WIRES.-> cell2A
    channel1 -.WIRES.-> cell2B

    ioRing["I O PAD RING"] --- core["CORE AREA STANDARD CELLS"]
    cornerPad["CORNER PAD"] --- ioRing
    powerPad["POWER PAD VDD VSS"] --- ioRing
```

## 4.5 Design Style Selection Decision Tree (Volume-Driven)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA{"WHAT IS THE PRODUCTION VOLUME"} --> startB["VOLUME LESS THAN 1000 UNITS"]
    startA --> startC["VOLUME 1000 TO 50000 UNITS"]
    startA --> startD["VOLUME GREATER THAN 50000 UNITS"]

    startA --> startE{"IS FIELD RECONFIGURABILITY REQUIRED"}

    startB --> choice1["FPGA IS OPTIMAL ZERO NRE FAST TTM"]
    startC --> choice2{"PERFORMANCE CRITICAL"}
    startC --> choice3["STANDARD CELL OR GATE ARRAY"]
    startD --> choice4["FULL CUSTOM OR OPTIMIZED STANDARD CELL"]

    startE --> yes1["YES CHOOSE FPGA"]
    startE --> no1["NO CHOOSE MASK BASED ASIC"]
```

## 4.6 Cost-vs-Volume Curve (Illustrative Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB
    axisX["PRODUCTION VOLUME UNITS"] --- axisY["TOTAL COST RUPEES"]

    curve1["FPGA COST LINEAR SLOPE HIGH"] --- axisX
    curve2["STANDARD CELL CURVE"] --- axisX
    curve3["FULL CUSTOM CURVE"] --- axisX
    curve4["BREAK EVEN POINT A"] --- axisX
    curve5["BREAK EVEN POINT B"] --- axisX

    region1["FPGA DOMINANT ZONE"] --- axisX
    region2["GATE ARRAY OR STD CELL ZONE"] --- axisX
    region3["FULL CUSTOM DOMINANT ZONE"] --- axisX
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Full Custom Design and Semi-Custom Design in VLSI.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Full Custom Design | Semi-Custom Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Every transistor and interconnect is hand-designed at the polygon level. | Pre-designed standard cells are placed and routed automatically by EDA tools. |
| **NRE Cost** | Very high (all mask layers custom) | Moderate to high (only interconnect masks custom) |
| **Design Time** | Longest (months to years) | Moderate (weeks to months) |
| **Performance** | Highest (every transistor sized optimally) | High (limited by cell library) |
| **Tools Used** | Cadence Virtuoso, manual DRC/LVS | Synopsys DC, Innovus, ICC2 |
| **Application** | CPU cores, analog/RF, memory | ASICs, SoCs, peripherals |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for NRE/perf difference, 1 mark for example)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "Sea of Gates" design style? How does it differ from a conventional channeled Gate Array?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Sea of Gates** is a variant of the **Gate Array** design style in which the pre-fabricated "master slice" consists of a dense, regular array of uncommitted **NAND or NOR cells** (or transistor pairs) covering the entire chip area, **without dedicated routing channels**.
- **Difference from Channeled Gate Array:** In a channeled gate array, fixed-width routing channels are reserved between rows of logic cells, limiting placement flexibility. In Sea of Gates, routing is performed **over the cell area** (using the unfabricated metal layers above unused transistors), yielding **higher cell density (up to 2×)** and greater placement freedom.
- **Trade-off:** More complex routing algorithms required; potentially longer wires and signal integrity issues.
- **Application:** Mid-volume ASICs where density matters more than extreme performance.

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for application/trade-off)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question (with both sub-parts)** from the choice of two.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Module 2, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the different **VLSI design styles** with suitable diagrams. Compare their **NRE cost**, **per-unit cost**, **performance**, and **time-to-market** in a tabular form. (7 marks)

**(b)** A design team is choosing between an **FPGA** and a **Standard Cell ASIC** for a new product. The FPGA option has zero NRE cost but a per-unit cost of ₹4,500. The Standard Cell ASIC has an NRE of ₹25,00,000 and a per-unit fabrication cost of ₹350. Determine the **break-even volume**. If the expected production volume is 20,000 units, which option is more economical, and by how much? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Model Answer:**

The major VLSI design styles are:

1. **Full Custom Design:** All mask layers are hand-designed. The designer manually places each transistor, sizes it for performance, and routes interconnects. Used in microprocessors, analog/RF blocks. Maximum performance, minimum area, maximum effort.

2. **Standard Cell Design (Semi-Custom):** Pre-designed and pre-characterized cells (NAND, NOR, DFF, MUX, Adder) from a cell library are placed in rows on the chip. EDA tools perform automatic placement and routing. Used in most digital ASICs and SoCs.

3. **Gate Array Design:** The foundry pre-fabricates a regular array of uncommitted logic cells (the "master slice"). The customer provides only the metal interconnect masks to personalize the logic. Lower NRE than full custom since only metal layers vary.

4. **Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA):** A fully fabricated IC containing an array of **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)**, programmable interconnects, and I/O blocks. Logic is defined by an SRAM/anti-fuse/flash configuration bitstream. No NRE cost; infinite reprogrammability.

5. **Programmable Logic Devices (PLD/CPLD):** Simpler programmable devices with AND-OR plane logic, used for glue logic and small state machines.

6. **Structured ASIC:** A hybrid between Gate Array and Standard Cell. Pre-fabricated base with one or two custom via layers for personalization.

**Comparison Table (to be drawn by student):**

| Parameter | Full Custom | Standard Cell | Gate Array | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NRE Cost | Very High | High | Medium | Zero |
| Per-Unit Cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| Performance | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |
| Time-to-Market | Longest | Medium | Short | Shortest |
| Density | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |

**(Marks distribution: 1 mark for each style listed correctly with example, 2 marks for the comparison table.)**

**Part (b) — Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the total cost equations.

$$C_{FPGA}(V) = 0 + 4500V$$

$$C_{ASIC}(V) = 25,00,000 + 350V$$

**Step 2:** Equate the two for break-even.

$$\begin{aligned}
4500V &= 25,00,000 + 350V \\
4500V - 350V &= 25,00,000 \\
4150V &= 25,00,000 \\
V_{break-even} &= \frac{25,00,000}{4150} \\
V_{break-even} &\approx 602.41
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The break-even volume is **603 units** (rounded up). For $V < 603$, FPGA is cheaper; for $V > 603$, Standard Cell ASIC is cheaper.

**Step 3:** Evaluate costs at $V = 20,000$ units.

$$C_{FPGA}(20000) = 4500 \times 20000 = 9,00,00,000 \text{ (₹9 Crore)}$$

$$C_{ASIC}(20000) = 25,00,000 + 350 \times 20000 = 25,00,000 + 70,00,000 = 95,00,000 \text{ (₹95 Lakh)}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate savings.

$$\text{Savings} = 9,00,00,000 - 95,00,000 = 8,05,00,000 \text{ (₹8.05 Crore)}$$

**Conclusion:** At 20,000 units, the **Standard Cell ASIC is more economical by ₹8.05 Crore** (total cost = ₹95 Lakh vs. FPGA's ₹9 Crore).

**(Marks distribution: 2 marks for setting up equations, 2 marks for solving break-even, 2 marks for cost comparison at 20,000 units, 1 mark for final conclusion.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Module 2, Alternative Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a modern FPGA**. Discuss the role of **CLBs**, **programmable interconnects**, **I/O blocks**, and **configuration memory** in implementing arbitrary digital logic. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for **dynamic power dissipation** in CMOS. A CMOS chip operates at $V_{DD} = 0.9 \text{ V}$, clock frequency $f = 1.2 \text{ GHz}$, with average switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$ and total load capacitance $C_L = 100 \text{ fF}$. Calculate the **dynamic power**. If $V_{DD}$ is reduced to 0.7 V, what is the percentage reduction in power? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Model Answer:**

A modern FPGA consists of four major building blocks:

1. **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs):** The basic logic unit. Each CLB contains one or more **Look-Up Tables (LUTs)** that implement any Boolean function of $N$ inputs, **flip-flops** for sequential logic, and **multiplexers** for signal steering. A 6-input LUT, for example, uses a 64-bit SRAM to store the truth table.

2. **Programmable Interconnects:** A hierarchical network of **horizontal and vertical wire segments** connected by **switch matrices** (controlled by SRAM bits). These allow any CLB output to be routed to any CLB input. Connection boxes interface the CLB pins to the routing channels.

3. **I/O Blocks (IOBs):** Located at the chip periphery. Each IOB supports multiple I/O standards (LVCMOS, LVDS, HSTL, etc.) with configurable drive strength, slew rate, and on-die termination.

4. **Embedded Hard IP:** Modern FPGAs include **Block RAM (BRAM)**, **DSP slices** (with hardware multipliers/accumulators), **PLLs/clock managers**, **high-speed transceivers** (SerDes), and sometimes **processor cores** (ARM Cortex hard cores in Xilinx Zynq, Intel Stratix).

5. **Configuration Memory:** An on-chip SRAM (or anti-fuse/flash) that stores the user's bitstream. At power-up, the bitstream is loaded, configuring all LUTs, switches, and IOBs.

**Role in Implementing Logic:** The LUTs implement combinational logic; flip-flops implement state; the interconnect network provides the "wiring"; and the IOBs connect to the outside world. Together, they emulate any digital circuit.

**(Marks distribution: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for CLB explanation, 2 marks for interconnect/IOB, 1 mark for configuration memory role.)**

**Part (b) — Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Derive Dynamic Power Expression**

Consider a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. In one full cycle (0→1→0):

**During charging (0→1):** The PMOS charges $C_L$ to $V_{DD}$. Energy drawn from the supply:

$$E_{drawn} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

Energy stored in $C_L$ at the end: $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$. Energy dissipated in PMOS: $E_{PMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.

**During discharging (1→0):** The NMOS discharges $C_L$. All stored energy ($\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$) is dissipated as heat in the NMOS.

**Total energy per cycle:** $E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2$.

**Power** = Energy per cycle × Number of cycles per second = $C_L V_{DD}^2 \cdot \alpha \cdot f$ (where $\alpha$ accounts for the fact that not every clock cycle causes a transition).

$$\boxed{P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}$$

**Step 2: Compute Power at $V_{DD} = 0.9$ V**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn,1} &= 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times (0.9)^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^9 \\
&= 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.81 \times 1.2 \times 10^9 \\
&= 0.2 \times 100 \times 0.81 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-15+9} \\
&= 19.44 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} \\
&= 19.44 \text{ \mu W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute Power at $V_{DD} = 0.7$ V**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn,2} &= 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times (0.7)^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^9 \\
&= 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.49 \times 1.2 \times 10^9 \\
&= 0.2 \times 100 \times 0.49 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 11.76 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} \\
&= 11.76 \text{ \mu W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Percentage Reduction**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Reduction} &= \frac{P_{dyn,1} - P_{dyn,2}}{P_{dyn,1}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{19.44 - 11.76}{19.44} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{7.68}{19.44} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 39.5\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Reducing $V_{DD}$ from 0.9 V to 0.7 V reduces dynamic power by approximately **39.5%** (theoretically should be $(1 - (0.7/0.9)^2) \times 100\% \approx 39.5\%$, which matches).

**(Marks distribution: 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for first calculation, 1 mark for second calculation, 1 mark for percentage result.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in VLSI Design Style Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Gate Array" with "Standard Cell"** — A gate array has a *pre-fabricated, fixed* transistor base, while a standard cell uses *pre-designed but separately fabricated* cells. Many students write them as identical.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the $\alpha$ factor in Power** — The switching activity is often omitted, leading to inflated power values. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 
> 3. **Writing "FPGA is always better" or "Full Custom is always better"** — Never declare a style as universally best. Always justify with **application + volume + performance** context.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the "Sea of Gates" mention** — Examiners specifically look for this sub-classification of gate arrays. A complete answer should distinguish channeled vs. channel-less.
> 
> 5. **In cost problems, forgetting to include NRE in the total** — Total cost = NRE + (Volume × Unit Cost). Many students only compute per-unit cost.
> 
> 6. **Missing the $V_{DD}^2$ dependence in power** — The quadratic dependence on supply voltage is a favorite KTU exam question. State it explicitly.
> 
> 7. **Not drawing the FPGA block diagram when asked** — A question asking "explain FPGA architecture with diagram" without a labeled diagram will lose 2–3 marks even if the text is correct.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for VLSI Design Styles (Module 2):**

- ☐ **Six major design styles:** Full Custom, Standard Cell, Gate Array (Channeled & Sea-of-Gates), FPGA, CPLD, Structured ASIC.
- ☐ **NRE cost order (high → low):** Full Custom > Standard Cell > Gate Array > Structured ASIC > FPGA (zero).
- ☐ **Per-unit cost order (high → low) for high volumes:** FPGA > Structured ASIC > Gate Array > Standard Cell > Full Custom.
- ☐ **Time-to-market (long → short):** Full Custom > Standard Cell > Gate Array > FPGA.
- ☐ **Dynamic Power Formula:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — memorize the **$V_{DD}^2$** dependence.
- ☐ **Static Power:** $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — dominates in modern nanometer nodes.
- ☐ **CMOS energy per cycle:** $E = C_L V_{DD}^2$ (half stored, half dissipated per transition).
- ☐ **FPGA building blocks:** CLB (LUT + FF), Programmable Interconnect, IOB, BRAM, DSP, Configuration SRAM.
- ☐ **Rent's Rule:** $T = kN^p$ — predicts I/O pin count from block count.
- ☐ **Elmore delay:** $t_d = \sum R_{i,\to source} \cdot C_i$ — used in interconnect delay estimation.
- ☐ **Break-even volume** is found by setting $C_{NRE1} + V \cdot c_1 = C_{NRE2} + V \cdot c_2$ and solving for $V$.
- ☐ **Lambda ($\lambda$) design rules:** $L_{min} = 2\lambda$, $W_{min} = 2\lambda$ — KTU exam standard.
- ☐ **Seeds' yield model:** $Y = e^{-\sqrt{AD}}$ — area defects reduce yield.
- ☐ **Choice of design style depends on:** Volume, Performance target, Power budget, NRE budget, Time-to-market, Field reconfigurability.
- ☐ **Sea of Gates = Gate Array without dedicated routing channels** (mention for full marks).
- ☐ **Structured ASIC = Hybrid (pre-fab base + 1–2 custom via layers)** — between FPGA and Standard Cell.
- ☐ **CLB = Configurable Logic Block** in FPGA (not "Central Logic Block" — common spelling error).
- ☐ **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** is the figure of merit for energy efficiency; **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** is stricter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partitioning Algorithms - Kernighan-Lin Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Partitioning Algorithms - Kernighan-Lin Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Kernighan-Lin Algorithm)**
> The **Kernighan-Lin (KL) Algorithm** is an iterative, graph-bisection heuristic proposed by *B. W. Kernighan and S. Lin (1970)*. It solves the **minimum-cut bipartitioning problem**: given a weighted undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, the objective is to partition the vertex set $V$ into two disjoint subsets $A$ and $B$ such that the number of edges (or total edge weight) crossing the cut, $\text{cut}(A, B) = \sum_{u \in A, v \in B} w(u,v)$, is minimized, subject to a balance constraint $|A| \approx |B| \approx \frac{n}{2}$.

The KL algorithm is the **foundational building block** of nearly every modern partitioning tool used in VLSI physical design automation, including hMetis, PaToH, and MLPart.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Classroom Reorganization" Analogy**
> Imagine a school principal has two classes (Class A and Class B), each of equal size. Every student has *friendships* (edges) with other students, both within their own class and across classes. The principal wants to **minimize cross-class friendships** (i.e., students who must travel to the other class to meet friends), while keeping both classes balanced.
>
> - Naive approach: randomly assign students. Result → many cross-class friendships (bad).
> - **KL approach**: intelligently identify which *single student-pair swap* produces the *biggest reduction* in cross-class friendships, perform that swap, lock both students, then repeat. The crucial insight: even a swap that *temporarily increases* cross-friendships may be acceptable if it sets up a much bigger improvement in a future swap.

This "accept temporarily-worse moves in the hope of escaping local optima" is the *secret sauce* that makes KL powerful and distinguishes it from greedy local-search heuristics.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

- **Gain $D(v)$:** *External cost minus internal cost* of a vertex. Positive $D(v) \Rightarrow$ moving $v$ reduces the cut.
- **Cutsize:** The number of edges crossing the partition — the **primary optimization metric**.
- **Balance Factor:** Constraint $|A| = |B| = n/2$ (assumed even $n$ in the classic version).
- **Locking:** Once a vertex is swapped, it is "locked" and cannot be moved again within the same *pass*.
- **Pass / Iteration:** One complete pass performs $n/2$ sequential swaps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KL algorithm is explicitly listed in Module 2 of the KTU 2024 EDA syllabus under *Partitioning Algorithms*. Examiners frequently test: (a) the gain computation, (b) the swap-pair selection rule, and (c) the cumulative-gain rollback logic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The Two-Phase Logic

The KL algorithm operates in **two nested loops**:

**Phase A — Inner Pass (Fixed Number of Swaps):**
1. Compute initial gains for every vertex.
2. For $i = 1, 2, \dots, n/2$:
   - **Select** the unlocked pair $(a_i, b_i)$ with $a_i \in A$, $b_i \in B$ that *maximizes* the pair-gain.
   - **Record** the cumulative gain $G_i = G_{i-1} + g_i$ where $G_0 = 0$.
   - **Lock** $a_i$ and $b_i$.
   - **Update** gains of unlocked neighbors of $a_i$ and $b_i$.
3. Find $k^* = \arg\max_{1 \le k \le n/2} G_k$.
4. **Commit** only the first $k^*$ swaps; *un-commit* the remaining (rollback logic).

**Phase B — Outer Loop:**
- Repeat Phase A until no pass produces a *positive* improvement in cut size.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Rollback Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The rollback is the *defining innovation* of KL. It allows the algorithm to **escape local minima**: it can perform swaps that *worsen* the current cut (e.g., $g_i < 0$) if those swaps are necessary to reach a *globally better* configuration $k$ steps later. After the pass, the algorithm keeps the prefix of swaps yielding the **best cumulative gain** observed at any prefix of the pass.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Cheat Sheet — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|
| $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ | **Gain** of vertex $v$ | $E(v)$ = sum of weights of edges from $v$ to opposite side; $I(v)$ = sum to same side |
| $g_i = D(a_i) + D(b_i) - 2 \cdot c(a_i, b_i)$ | **Pair-gain** for swapping $a_i \leftrightarrow b_i$ | $c(a,b)$ = edge weight between $a$ and $b$ (0 if no edge) |
| $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$ | **Cumulative gain** after $k$ swaps | Used to find best prefix |
| $\text{cut}(A,B) = \sum_{u \in A, v \in B} w(u,v)$ | **Cut size** — objective to minimize | Also written as $C(A,B)$ or $\phi$ |
| $\Delta \text{cut after one pass} = -G_{k^*}$ | Net cut-size change after a pass | $G_{k^*} > 0$ means improvement |
| $T(n) = O(n^3)$ | **Time complexity** of original KL | With Fiduccia-Mattheyes: $O(n \cdot p)$ where $p$ = pins |
| $S(n) = O(n^2)$ | **Space complexity** | Gain array, lock flag, history |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **VLSI physical design**, KL-based partitioning is used to:
- Decide which logic cells go into which die region in **multi-core chip floorplanning**.
- Pre-partition large netlists (>10M cells) for **distributed placement** in commercial EDA tools (Synopsys ICC2, Cadence Innovus).
- Solve **hypergraph partitioning** when extended (hMetis).
- Minimize **inter-block wirelength** in 3D-IC and chiplet designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pair-Gain Formula $g_i$

**Setting:** A vertex $a \in A$ and a vertex $b \in B$ are candidates for swapping. Let $c(a,b)$ denote the weight of the edge $(a,b)$ if it exists; otherwise $c(a,b) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Effect of moving $a$ from $A$ to $B$:**
The contribution of $a$ to the cut changes by:
- It loses $E(a)$ edges that were crossing the cut (now internal to $B$).
- It gains $I(a)$ edges that were internal to $A$ (now cross the cut).
- Net change in cut for $a$ alone: $+I(a) - E(a) = -D(a)$.

**Step 2 — Effect of moving $b$ from $B$ to $A$:**
By symmetry, net change for $b$ alone: $-D(b)$.

**Step 3 — Double-counting the edge $(a,b)$:**
If $c(a,b) > 0$ (an edge exists between $a$ and $b$), it has been counted **twice**:
- Once in $E(a)$ (it was a cross-edge of $a$),
- Once in $E(b)$ (it was a cross-edge of $b$).
After the swap, this edge becomes *internal* to the new $A$, so it is removed from the cut. The factor of 2 must therefore be subtracted.

**Step 4 — Total cut change after swapping $a$ and $b$:**

$$
\Delta \text{cut} = -D(a) - D(b) + 2 \cdot c(a,b)
$$

**Step 5 — Pair-gain defined as the reduction in cut:**

$$
g_i = -\Delta \text{cut} = D(a) + D(b) - 2 \cdot c(a,b)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If $a$ and $b$ are *not* directly connected ($c(a,b) = 0$), then the gain is simply $D(a) + D(b)$. If they *are* connected, the edge between them must be paid for twice in the cost, hence the $-2c(a,b)$ penalty.

### 3.2 Gain Update Formula After a Swap

When $a$ is moved from $A$ to $B$, every unlocked neighbor $x$ of $a$ changes its gain:

$$
D'(x) = D(x) + \Delta(x, a)
$$

where the gain correction is:

$$
\Delta(x, a) = \begin{cases} -2 \cdot w(x,a), & \text{if } x \text{ and } a \text{ are on the same (new) side} \\ +2 \cdot w(x,a), & \text{if } x \text{ and } a \text{ are on opposite (new) sides} \end{cases}
$$

This reduces the inner-loop cost from $O(n)$ per swap to $O(\text{degree of } a)$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (4-Vertex Graph)

Consider a graph with $|A| = |B| = 2$, where all edges have weight 1:
- $A = \{1, 2\}$, $B = \{3, 4\}$.
- Edges: $(1,2)$, $(1,3)$, $(2,3)$, $(3,4)$, $(2,4)$.
- Initial cut: $(1,3), (2,3), (2,4) \Rightarrow$ cut $= 3$.

**Initial gains:**

$$
D(1) = E(1) - I(1) = 1 - 1 = 0
$$

$$
D(2) = E(2) - I(2) = 2 - 1 = 1
$$

$$
D(3) = E(3) - I(3) = 2 - 1 = 1
$$

$$
D(4) = E(4) - I(4) = 1 - 1 = 0
$$

**Pair-gain candidates (swap a ∈ A, b ∈ B):**

$$
g(1,3) = 0 + 1 - 2 \cdot 1 = -1
$$

$$
g(1,4) = 0 + 0 - 0 = 0
$$

$$
g(2,3) = 1 + 1 - 2 \cdot 1 = 0
$$

$$
g(2,4) = 1 + 0 - 2 \cdot 0 = 1
$$

**Selection:** Best pair = $(2, 4)$ with $g_1 = 1$. After swapping, new $A = \{1, 4\}$, new $B = \{2, 3\}$. New cut: edges crossing are $(1,3), (2,4) \Rightarrow$ cut $= 2$ (improved!).

### 3.4 Complexity Analysis Derivation

- **Initial gain computation:** $O(|V| + |E|)$.
- **Per pass:** $n/2$ swaps. Each swap scans remaining unlocked vertices to find the max-gain pair: $O(n)$ per swap. Total: $O(n^2)$.
- **Gain updates:** $O(\text{degree})$ per swap; summed over a pass: $O(|E|)$.
- **Per pass total:** $O(n^2)$.
- **Number of passes:** Empirically $O(\log n)$ to $O(n)$, so worst-case $O(n^3)$ overall.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level KL Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read graph G V E] --> B[Generate initial bipartition A and B balanced]
    B --> C[Compute initial gain D v for every vertex]
    C --> D[Set all vertices unlocked]
    D --> E{i less than n by 2}
    E -- Yes --> F[Find max pair gain a in A b in B]
    F --> G[Record swap a b and cumulative gain G i]
    G --> H[Lock vertices a and b]
    H --> I[Update gains of unlocked neighbors]
    I --> J[i = i + 1]
    J --> E
    E -- No --> K[Find k star maximizing G k]
    K --> L{k star greater than 0}
    L -- Yes --> M[Commit first k star swaps]
    M --> N[Unlock all vertices for next pass]
    N --> O{Any improvement in this pass}
    O -- Yes --> C
    O -- No --> P[STOP: Return final partition]
    L -- No --> P
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a KL Pass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        P1[Graph G V E weights] --> P2[Initial Partitioner]
        P2 --> P3[Bipartition A B]
    end
    subgraph INIT[Initialization Stage]
        P3 --> P4[Gain Computer E v minus I v]
        P4 --> P5[Lock Table initialized to false]
    end
    subgraph INNER[Inner Swap Loop]
        P5 --> P6[Pair Selector max D a plus D b minus 2c]
        P6 --> P7[Swap Executor a moves to B b moves to A]
        P7 --> P8[Cumulative Gain Tracker G i]
        P8 --> P9[Gain Updater for unlocked neighbors]
        P9 --> P10[Lock Writer set lock a lock b true]
        P10 --> P6
    end
    subgraph COMMIT[Commit Stage]
        P8 --> P11[Best Prefix Finder argmax G k]
        P11 --> P12[Rollback to swap k star]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        P12 --> P13[Final bipartition A B minimized cut]
    end
```

### 4.3 Gain-Update Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[Vertex a is selected for swap] --> N2[For each unlocked neighbor x of a]
    N2 --> N3{Is x in same new side as a after swap}
    N3 -- Yes --> N4[D x = D x minus 2 w x a]
    N3 -- No --> N5[D x = D x plus 2 w x a]
    N4 --> N6[Continue scanning neighbors]
    N5 --> N6
    N6 --> N2
    N2 --> N7[All neighbors updated]
    N7 --> N8[Lock a and b]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each block corresponds to a *clear functional responsibility* in the KL algorithm. Examiners often award partial credit for correctly drawn flow diagrams showing the rollback mechanism at the commit stage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the gain $D(v)$ of a vertex with respect to a bipartition. Why is the pair-gain for swapping two vertices $a$ and $b$ given by $g = D(a) + D(b) - 2c(a,b)$?**

**Model Answer:**
The gain $D(v)$ of a vertex $v$ is defined as the difference between the *external cost* $E(v)$ (sum of weights of edges from $v$ to vertices in the opposite side) and the *internal cost* $I(v)$ (sum of weights of edges to vertices in the same side):

$$
D(v) = E(v) - I(v)
$$

A positive $D(v)$ indicates that moving $v$ to the opposite side will *decrease* the cut size. The pair-gain for swapping $a \in A$ and $b \in B$ is derived by summing the individual gains $-D(a) - D(b)$, which represents the cut reduction from each move considered independently. However, if the edge $(a,b)$ exists with weight $c(a,b)$, it has been counted *twice* in this sum (once in $E(a)$ and once in $E(b)$), and the swap will remove this edge from the cut. Therefore, the term $+2c(a,b)$ is added back to correct for this double-counting, yielding:

$$
g = D(a) + D(b) - 2c(a,b)
$$

If $c(a,b) = 0$ (no direct edge), the pair-gain reduces to $D(a) + D(b)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the "rollback to best prefix" strategy in the Kernighan-Lin algorithm? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
During a single pass, the KL algorithm performs $n/2$ sequential swaps and records the cumulative gain $G_k$ after each swap $k$. The "rollback to best prefix" strategy states that, at the end of the pass, the algorithm identifies the index $k^* = \arg\max_{1 \le k \le n/2} G_k$ that maximizes the cumulative gain, and **commits only the first $k^*$ swaps**, undoing (rolling back) the remaining ones.

This strategy is necessary because the greedy choice at each step is not globally optimal. A swap that *individually* has a low or even negative gain may be essential to *unlock* a much larger improvement in a later swap. Without rollback, the algorithm would be trapped in the local minimum of the *last* swap, missing the global best of the pass. Rollback allows the algorithm to escape local optima — the *key innovation* of KL over naive hill-climbing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> **(1) Gain formula errors:** A common mistake is writing $g = D(a) + D(b) - 2c(a,b)$ as $g = D(a) + D(b) + 2c(a,b)$. Remember: if $a$ and $b$ are connected, the edge is *removed from the cut*, so the *cut is reduced* by $2c(a,b)$, meaning the **gain is reduced** by $2c(a,b)$.
> **(2) Forgetting rollback:** Many students describe KL as a "greedy" algorithm. The hallmark of KL is the **best-prefix rollback**; omitting it in the answer costs 3–4 marks.
> **(3) Complexity:** Original KL is $O(n^3)$, NOT $O(n^2)$. Confusing the two is a frequent error.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step operation of the Kernighan-Lin bipartitioning algorithm. Include a clear description of gain computation, pair selection, locking, and the rollback mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A graph has $A = \{1, 2\}$, $B = \{3, 4\}$ with edges: $(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (2,4), (3,4)$, all of unit weight. Apply **one complete pass** of the Kernighan-Lin algorithm, computing the initial gains, the pair-gain at each step, the cumulative gains, and the final committed partition.

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Initialization:** Start with a balanced random bipartition $A, B$ of the vertex set $V$.
2. **Gain Computation:** For each vertex $v$, compute $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ where $E(v)$ is the sum of edge weights to vertices in the *opposite* partition, and $I(v)$ is the sum to vertices in the *same* partition.
3. **Pair Selection (Inner Loop):** For iteration $i = 1$ to $n/2$:
   - Examine all unlocked pairs $(a, b)$ with $a \in A$, $b \in B$.
   - Select the pair that maximizes $g_i = D(a) + D(b) - 2c(a,b)$.
   - This requires $O(n)$ scans per iteration.
4. **Swap and Lock:** Move $a$ to $B$ and $b$ to $A$. Mark both as *locked* so they are not considered again in this pass.
5. **Update Gains:** For every unlocked neighbor $x$ of $a$ or $b$, adjust $D(x)$ by $\pm 2w(x,a)$ or $\pm 2w(x,b)$ depending on whether $x$ and the moved vertex are now on the same or opposite sides.
6. **Record Cumulative Gain:** Track $G_i = G_{i-1} + g_i$ where $G_0 = 0$.
7. **Commit Best Prefix (Rollback):** After $n/2$ iterations, find $k^* = \arg\max G_k$. **Undo** all swaps after step $k^*$. The committed partition is the one at step $k^*$.
8. **Repeat Pass:** Restart until a pass produces no net cut improvement. The final partition is the local minimum discovered.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Initial Setup:** $A = \{1, 2\}$, $B = \{3, 4\}$, cut $= \{(1,3), (2,3), (2,4)\} \Rightarrow \text{cut} = 3$.

**Step 1 — Compute initial gains:**

$$
D(1) = E(1) - I(1) = 1 - 1 = 0
$$

$$
D(2) = E(2) - I(2) = 2 - 1 = 1
$$

$$
D(3) = E(3) - I(3) = 2 - 1 = 1
$$

$$
D(4) = E(4) - I(4) = 1 - 1 = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Find best pair (iteration 1):**

$$
g(1,3) = 0 + 1 - 2(1) = -1
$$

$$
g(1,4) = 0 + 0 - 0 = 0
$$

$$
g(2,3) = 1 + 1 - 2(1) = 0
$$

$$
g(2,4) = 1 + 0 - 0 = 1
$$

**Selected pair:** $(2, 4)$ with $g_1 = 1$, $G_1 = 1$. New partition: $A' = \{1, 4\}$, $B' = \{2, 3\}$. New cut: $(1,3), (2,4) \Rightarrow \text{cut} = 2$.

**Step 3 — Update remaining gains (vertex 1 and 3 still unlocked):**
Since 1 and 4 are now on the same side, and 4 was moved, $D(1)$ does not change (1 was already not connected to 4 in original cross-sense, but check neighbors of 2,3,4). After locking 2 and 4, the unlocked vertices are 1 and 3; they are directly connected, so on next iteration only pair (1,3) is possible with $g(1,3) = D(1) + D(3) - 2(1) = 0 + 1 - 2 = -1$, $G_2 = 1 + (-1) = 0$.

**Step 4 — Best prefix:** $k^* = 1$ (max $G_k$ is $G_1 = 1$).

**Step 5 — Commit:** Keep only swap 1. Final partition after one pass: $A = \{1, 4\}$, $B = \{2, 3\}$, cut $= 2$ (improved from 3).

**Second pass** would not yield further improvement; algorithm terminates.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the Kernighan-Lin algorithm with the **Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) algorithm**. Discuss the time complexity, move semantics, and the data structure used for bucket-based gain management in FM.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a graph with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges, derive the time complexity of one full pass of the Kernighan-Lin algorithm. Show how each phase (gain initialization, pair selection, gain update) contributes to the $O(n^2)$ per-pass bound.

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Aspect | Kernighan-Lin (KL) | Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Movement** | Swaps a *pair* of vertices simultaneously | Moves *one* vertex at a time |
| **Data structure** | Simple gain array, no buckets | Two **gain buckets** (one per side) indexed by gain value |
| **Selection rule** | Scan all unlocked pairs | Pick *max-gain* vertex from the higher side; move to the other side |
| **Locking** | Lock *both* vertices of the swapped pair | Lock only the *single* moved vertex |
| **Time per pass** | $O(n^2)$ — pair selection is quadratic | $O(n)$ — bucket lookup is $O(1)$ with proper maintenance |
| **Total complexity** | $O(n^3)$ overall | $O(n \cdot p)$ where $p$ = max pin count |
| **Rollback** | Yes — best prefix of pair-swaps | Yes — best prefix of single moves |
| **Hypergraph support** | Originally graph-only | Naturally extends to hypergraphs |
| **Vertex weight** | All vertices assumed unit weight | Supports *vertex weights* (cell area) |

**Conclusion:** FM is a *refinement* of KL that achieves $O(n)$ per pass by replacing pair-swap with single-vertex move and by using bucket-based priority queues. FM also generalizes more naturally to the hypergraph and weighted-vertex cases encountered in real VLSI netlists.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Phase 1 — Gain Initialization:**
For each vertex $v$, we compute $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ by summing edge weights to neighbors on the opposite side. Each edge contributes to two vertex gains, so total work is $O(m)$. Since $m \le \binom{n}{2} = O(n^2)$, this phase is $O(m) = O(n^2)$ in the worst case.

**Phase 2 — Pair Selection (executed $n/2$ times per pass):**
At each of the $n/2$ iterations, we scan *all* unlocked vertices on both sides to find the pair $(a, b)$ that maximizes $g = D(a) + D(b) - 2c(a,b)$. The number of unlocked vertices at iteration $i$ is $n - 2(i-1)$. Summing the scan work:

$$
T_{\text{select}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n/2} (n - 2i + 2) = \frac{n}{2} \cdot \frac{n+2}{2} \approx \frac{n^2}{4} = O(n^2)
$$

**Phase 3 — Gain Update (executed $n/2$ times per pass):**
After each swap, we update the gains of unlocked neighbors of the moved vertices. The total number of neighbor updates over a full pass equals $2m$ (each edge is touched at most twice — once from each endpoint), so:

$$
T_{\text{update}} = O(m) = O(n^2)
$$

**Phase 4 — Best Prefix Search:**
After the pass, scanning the cumulative-gain array of size $n/2$ to find $k^* = \arg\max G_k$ takes $O(n)$ time.

**Total per-pass time:**

$$
T_{\text{pass}} = T_{\text{init}} + T_{\text{select}} + T_{\text{update}} + T_{\text{prefix}} = O(n^2) + O(n^2) + O(n^2) + O(n) = O(n^2)
$$

Since the number of passes is empirically $O(n)$ in the worst case, the overall complexity is:

$$
T_{\text{KL total}} = O(n^3)
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Kernighan-Lin Algorithm**

- ☐ KL solves **bipartition (min-cut)** on a weighted undirected graph with a *balance constraint*.
- ☐ **Gain** of a vertex: $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ — memorize without confusion.
- ☐ **Pair-gain** for swapping $a$ and $b$: $g = D(a) + D(b) - 2c(a,b)$ — the $-2c(a,b)$ term accounts for the edge between them being removed from the cut.
- ☐ **Best-prefix rollback** is the *defining feature* that allows KL to escape local minima — do not call KL "greedy" in exams.
- ☐ **Locking** is per-pass: a vertex can move at most once per pass.
- ☐ **Time complexity:** $O(n^2)$ per pass, $O(n^3)$ overall in the original formulation.
- ☐ **Space complexity:** $O(n + m)$ for the gain array, lock flags, and cumulative-gain history.
- ☐ **KL is a *heuristic*** — it is not guaranteed to find the global optimum, but consistently produces high-quality local minima.
- ☐ **FM improvement:** $O(n \cdot p)$ per pass by using single-vertex moves and gain buckets; supports vertex weights and hypergraphs.
- ☐ **Engineering use:** VLSI netlist partitioning, multi-core floorplanning, 3D-IC and chiplet design pre-partitioning, distributed EDA tool backbones.
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Writing $g = D(a) + D(b) + 2c(a,b)$ — the sign of the $c(a,b)$ term is **negative**, not positive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fiduccia-Mattheyses Algorithm (Basic Algorithmic Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fiduccia-Mattheyses Algorithm (Basic Algorithmic Description Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) Algorithm** is a linear-time, iterative, hypergraph partitioning heuristic introduced in 1982 as a generalization and improvement of the classic Kernighan-Lin (KL) bipartitioning algorithm. Unlike KL, which operates on **vertex pairs (swaps)** on an unweighted graph, FM operates on a **weighted hypergraph** (suited to netlist/cell representation of VLSI circuits) and considers **single-cell moves** from one partition to the other.

Formally, for a hypergraph $H = (V, E)$ with vertex weights and edge weights, the FM algorithm:
- Attempts to minimize the **cutsize** (number of hyperedges crossing partitions).
- Computes a **gain** for every movable cell based on how moving it would change the cutsize.
- Performs a sequence of the *best feasible* moves, even if they temporarily worsen the cutsize, in order to escape local minima.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The treatment here is restricted to the *basic algorithmic description only*. The student must know the data structures (bucket list), the gain computation rule, the move/lock mechanism, the roll-back to best-so-far point, and the pass-termination logic. Mathematical proofs of optimality and advanced extensions (e.g., multi-way, constraint-aware FM) are out of scope for this module.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you have a **workplace** with 200 employees split into two buildings of equal size (Building A and Building B). Some employees collaborate frequently — they form **project teams** (nets). Your goal: rearrange the seating so that as few project teams as possible have members scattered across both buildings.

If you randomly move people, you may help some projects (by gathering them in one place) but accidentally split others. The FM approach is like a smart re-seater:

1. For each employee, compute a **"team-splitting score"** — how many of their projects would become fully together in one building (helping) vs. how many would become fully split (hurting) if they moved.
2. Pick the employee with the **highest positive score** who does not violate the building-capacity balance and move them.
3. **Lock** that seat — you cannot re-shuffle them back immediately.
4. Repeat, accepting moves that *temporarily worsen* the situation, because sometimes you have to take one step back to take two steps forward.
5. Keep a memory of the *best* arrangement seen so far; when the score starts dropping, roll back to that best state.
6. Start the entire process over (a new "pass") until no further improvement is possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from KL Algorithm:**
> - **KL** swaps *pairs* of vertices; **FM** moves *single* cells.
> - **KL** has $O(n^2 \log n)$ or $O(n^3)$ complexity; **FM** has **linear $O(P)$** complexity where $P$ is the number of pins (terminals) — this is what makes FM practical for VLSI circuits with millions of cells.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical KTU Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of cells | $n = \vert V \vert$ | **10 K – 10 M** | Movable vertices (gates/blocks) |
| Number of nets | $m = \vert E \vert$ | **10 K – 1 M** | Hyperedges (signal nets) |
| Number of pins | $P$ | $10^6$ typical | Total cell-to-net connections |
| Cell weight | $w(v)$ | **1** (unit) or area | Size of a cell |
| Net weight | $c(e)$ | **1** (unit) | Criticality of a net |
| Balance tolerance | $r$ | **0.0 – 0.1** | Allowed deviation of partition size |
| Number of buckets | $K$ | $\mathbf{2P_{max} + 1}$ | One per distinct possible gain value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Criticality Definition:** A net is *critical* if it has exactly **one** cell on one of the two partitions. Such a net is one cell away from becoming uncut (fully inside one partition).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The FM Algorithm — Operational Logic

The FM algorithm is built on four foundational pillars. Each is essential for KTU examination answers.

#### Pillar 1: Gain of a Cell
For a cell $v$ currently in partition $A$ being considered for a move to partition $B$:

$$g(v) = \text{FS}(v) - \text{TE}(v)$$

- $\text{FS}(v)$ — *From-Side gain*: number of critical nets at $v$ that will become *uncut* if $v$ moves to $B$.  
  Reason: If a net has $v$ as its only cell in $A$, removing $v$ leaves the net fully in $B$ → cutsize decreases.
- $\text{TE}(v)$ — *To-Side loss*: number of nets at $v$ that will become *newly critical* in $B$ if $v$ moves there.  
  Reason: If a net currently has all its cells in $B$ except for $v$, moving $v$ to $B$ makes the net "mostly in $B$" but now with $v$ alone in $B$ on the receiving side... actually: a net with exactly one cell in $B$ becomes newly critical (one cell in $B$) after the move.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** A positive $g(v)$ means the move *reduces* cutsize; negative $g(v)$ means the move *increases* cutsize. The algorithm is allowed to execute negative-gain moves as long as feasibility (balance) is preserved.

#### Pillar 2: Bucket-List Data Structure
Because gain values range from $-P_{max}$ to $+P_{max}$, a **bucket array** indexed by gain value stores all free (unlocked) cells. Each bucket is a doubly-linked list, supporting:
- Insertion / deletion in $O(1)$.
- Finding the max-gain cell in $O(K)$ (or $O(1)$ with a current-max pointer).

When a cell's gain changes after a move, it is **removed from one bucket and inserted into another in constant time**. This is the key to the linear-time complexity.

#### Pillar 3: Move-and-Lock Discipline
Each cell, once moved, is **locked** (marked *moved*) so that:
- It is not reconsidered in the current pass.
- Its neighbors' gains are **incrementally updated** (only the nets touching the moved cell need recomputation).
- This guarantees $O(P)$ work per pass, since each pin is touched a constant number of times.

#### Pillar 4: Roll-back to Best-So-Far
The partial cumulative gain after $k$ moves is:
$$G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$$
where $g_i$ is the gain of the $i$-th moved cell. Even though individual $g_i$ may be negative, $G_k$ may still be positive. FM finds $k^*$ such that $G_{k^*}$ is **maximum over all $k$** in the pass, and then *unrolls* all moves after $k^*$, fixing the state at the best configuration seen.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Gain of cell $v$** | $g(v) = \text{FS}(v) - \text{TE}(v)$ | Dimensionless integer |
| **Cumulative gain after $k$ moves** | $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$ | Used to find $k^*$ |
| **Cutsize (objective)** | $C = \sum_{e \in E_{cut}} c(e)$ | Number of cut nets (weighted) |
| **Balance constraint** | $\left\vert \frac{\vert A \vert}{n} - \frac{1}{2} \right\vert \leq r$ | $r$ = tolerance, $n$ = total cells |
| **Time complexity per pass** | $\mathbf{O(P)}$ | $P$ = total pin count |
| **Space complexity** | $\mathbf{O(n + m)}$ | Bucket list + net lists |
| **Number of gain buckets** | $K = 2 P_{max} + 1$ | $P_{max}$ = max pin count per net |
| **Maximum possible gain range** | $g(v) \in [-d(v), +d(v)]$ | $d(v)$ = cell degree |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Quick-Rule:** When asked "Why is FM linear?", the answer is: *each pin is examined a constant number of times per pass — once when the cell is locked, and once for each of its neighbors when updating their gains — yielding $O(P)$ work.*

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The FM algorithm is the **workhorse partitioning engine** inside nearly every EDA tool's design-flow. Specific applications include:
1. **Two-way circuit partitioning** at the floorplanning stage of ASIC design.
2. **Initial cut generation** for multi-way partitioners (Recursive FM).
3. **Kernighan-Lin style refinement** within multilevel frameworks (e.g., hMETIS, MLPart).
4. **FPGA placement** in tools like VPR (Versatile Place and Route).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gain Formula

**Statement to prove:** When a cell $v \in A$ is moved to partition $B$, the change in cutsize equals $g(v) = \text{FS}(v) - \text{TE}(v)$.

**Setup.** Let $e$ be a net incident on $v$. The cut contribution of $e$ depends on how many of its cells lie in $A$ and how many in $B$. Define:
- $\Phi_A(e) = $ number of cells of $e$ in $A$ (before move)
- $\Phi_B(e) = $ number of cells of $e$ in $B$ (before move)

The net $e$ is *cut* iff $\Phi_A(e) \geq 1$ AND $\Phi_B(e) \geq 1$.

**Case Analysis After Moving $v$ from $A$ to $B$:**

| Case | Before Move | After Move | Change in Cut Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. $e$ was uncut, all in $A$ | $\Phi_A = k, \Phi_B = 0$ | $\Phi_A = k-1, \Phi_B = 1$ | **Becomes cut** (change = $+1$) |
| 2. $e$ was uncut, all in $B$ | $\Phi_A = 0, \Phi_B = k$ | $\Phi_A = 0, \Phi_B = k+1$ | Remains uncut (change = $0$) |
| 3. $e$ was cut | $\Phi_A \geq 1, \Phi_B \geq 1$ | various | Depends on sub-case |
| 4. $e$ was critical in $A$ (only $v$ in $A$) | $\Phi_A = 1, \Phi_B = k$ | $\Phi_A = 0, \Phi_B = k+1$ | **Becomes uncut** (change = $-1$) |
| 5. $e$ was critical in $B$ (only 1 cell in $B$) | $\Phi_A = k, \Phi_B = 1$ | $\Phi_A = k-1, \Phi_B = 2$ | Remains cut (change = $0$) |
| 6. $e$ has exactly 2 cells in $B$ | $\Phi_A = k, \Phi_B = 2$ | $\Phi_A = k-1, \Phi_B = 3$ | Remains cut (change = $0$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Observation:** The only cases where the cut status *changes* are **Case 1** (uncut in $A$ becomes cut) and **Case 4** (critical in $A$ becomes uncut). Every other case leaves the cut status unchanged. This is precisely what the gain formula captures.

**Formal Derivation:**
