> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "Physical Design Optimization = placement". This is **wrong**. Placement is *one stage* of physical design; optimization is the *transformation engine* that improves placement, routing, and post-routing results. Marks are deducted if the distinction is blurred.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the Elmore delay model? Why is it preferred over SPICE for inner-loop optimization?**

**Model Answer:**
The Elmore delay model estimates the 50% propagation delay at a node in an RC tree as:

$$\tau_i = \sum_k R_{ik} \cdot C_k$$

It is preferred over SPICE because:
1. It is **closed-form and linear-time**, computed in $O(N)$ for an $N$-node tree.
2. It does **not require numerical integration**, making it ideal for the millions of incremental evaluations inside an optimization inner loop.
3. SPICE simulation would be **computationally infeasible** when called per move.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students forget to mention the **closed-form** property. Simply stating "it is faster" is too vague — always quantify with **O(N)** complexity or runtime.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **buffer insertion technique** for timing optimization on long interconnect wires. Derive the expression for the **optimal number of buffers** required on a wire of length $L$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Gate Sizing, Buffer Insertion, and Wire Sizing** in terms of delay reduction, area penalty, and applicability in modern sub-32 nm nodes.

---

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Problem Statement (1 mark):**
Long wires exhibit quadratic RC delay $T \propto R_w C_w L^2$, which dominates gate delay at deep sub-micron nodes. Buffer insertion breaks the wire into shorter RC segments.

**Step 2 — System Setup (2 marks):**
A wire of length $L$ with per-unit resistance $r$ and capacitance $c$ is driven by a buffer chain. Each buffer has intrinsic resistance $R_b$ and input capacitance $C_b$.

**Step 3 — Delay Expression (2 marks):**
Total delay after $n$ identical buffers:

$$T_{total} = n R_b C_b + \frac{R_b c L}{2} + \frac{r L C_b}{2} + \frac{r c L^2}{6n}$$

**Step 4 — Optimization (1 mark):**
Differentiate $T_{total}$ with respect to $n$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dT_{total}}{dn} = R_b C_b - \frac{r c L^2}{6n^2} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Final Result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{n_{opt} \approx L \sqrt{\frac{r \cdot c}{R_b \cdot C_b}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip:** The result scales as **$\sqrt{L}$**, not $L$. Hence doubling wire length needs only ~41% more buffers.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| Aspect | Gate Sizing | Buffer Insertion | Wire Sizing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delay Reduction | High (3×) | Very High (10×) | Medium (30%) |
| Area Penalty | Negative (cell grows) | Negative (extra cells) | Neutral |
| DRC Impact | Possible (cell width changes) | Low | Possible (spacing) |
| Sub-32 nm Suitability | Excellent | Excellent | Limited (manufacturing) |
| Tool Support | Native in all EDA | Native in all EDA | Partial / opt-in |
| Risk of Hold Violation | Medium | High | Low |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
For modern nodes, **buffer insertion** is the most effective delay recovery technique. Gate sizing is second-best for setup violations. Wire sizing is reserved for non-critical performance paths due to manufacturing complexity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Part b):** Students commonly confuse **setup vs hold** violations. Buffer insertion **helps setup** but **harms hold**. Always state this tradeoff explicitly to earn full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Logical Effort method** for gate delay estimation. Write the delay equation and define each term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **three power optimization techniques** used in physical design with their respective tradeoffs.

---

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
Logical Effort is a delay model that captures the intrinsic capability of a logic gate to drive a load. It decouples *process*, *gate topology*, and *load*.

**Step 2 — Delay Equation (2 marks):**
The delay of a gate in absolute time is:

$$d = \tau \cdot (g \cdot h + p)$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = process-dependent inverter delay (~ 3RC of an inverter in the technology).
- $g$ = **logical effort** of the gate (1 for inverter, 4/3 for NAND, etc.).
- $h$ = **electrical effort** = $C_{out} / C_{in}$ (fanout).
- $p$ = **parasitic delay** (intrinsic output capacitance effect).

**Step 3 — Branching Effort Extension (2 marks):**
For multi-fanout paths:

$$h = \frac{C_{on-path} + C_{off-path}}{C_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Optimization Principle (1 mark):**
Best performance is achieved when each stage has equal stage effort:

$$f_i = g_i h_i = f_{opt} \approx 4$$

This yields the **minimum path delay**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse $g$ and $h$. Remember: $g$ depends on the **gate itself**, $h$ depends on the **load attached**.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| # | Technique | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Multi-Vt Cell Swapping** | Replace LVT cells with HVT/SVT on non-critical paths | Reduces leakage by 10×, increases delay by 5–10% |
| 2 | **Clock Gating** | Disable clock to idle registers | Saves 20–40% dynamic power; needs RTL support |
| 3 | **Voltage Scaling** | Lower $V_{DD}$ on selected blocks | Power reduces as $V^2$, but delay increases sharply |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Power optimization in physical design is a **post-RTL, pre-sign-off** activity. The most effective strategy is **multi-Vt swapping**, followed by **clock gating** and finally **voltage scaling**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students write only "use low power" — KTU expects specific techniques with named mechanisms. Marks are awarded for naming the technique AND explaining its tradeoff.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU ESE Module 2 (Physical Design Optimizations)**

- [x] Physical Design Optimization = **post-placement transformations** to improve delay, area, power, congestion.
- [x] **Elmore Delay**: $\tau_i = \sum_k R_{ik} C_k$ — the **workhorse model** of inner-loop optimization.
- [x] **Logical Effort Delay**: $d = \tau(gh + p)$ — used for gate sizing decisions.
- [x] **Dynamic Power**: $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ — quadratic in voltage, linear in everything else.
- [x] **Optimal Buffer Count**: $n_{opt} \approx L \sqrt{rc / (R_b C_b)}$ — scales with $\sqrt{L}$.
- [x] **Wire Delay**: $T \approx 0.38 R_w C_w L^2$ — quadratic in length, hence buffer insertion is mandatory.
- [x] **Tapered Wire Width**: $w(x) = w_0 e^{\alpha x}$ — gives ~30% delay reduction over uniform.
- [x] **Cost Function**: $\Phi = w_t T + w_a A + w_p P + w_c U$ — weights must sum to 1.
- [x] **Setup vs Hold**: Buffer insertion fixes setup, hurts hold. Always mention tradeoff.
- [x] **Algorithm Family**: Greedy Hill Climbing + Simulated Annealing for placement/move acceptance.
- [x] **Optimization Stages**: Pre-routing (sizing, buffers) → Router → Post-routing (wire sizing, shielding).
- [x] **Industry Tools**: Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, Mentor Olympus — all use the equations above internally.
- [x] **Sign-off Outputs**: Optimized DEF, updated SPEF, power & timing reports.

### Numerical Constants to Memorize

| Constant | Value | Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta$ (velocity saturation index) | ≈ 1.3–1.5 | Alpha-power law delay model |
| Inverter Logical Effort $g_{inv}$ | **1** (by definition) | Logical effort base case |
| Tapering delay reduction | ≈ **30%** | Over uniform wire |
| Multi-Vt leakage ratio (LVT:HVT) | ≈ **10:1** | Power tradeoff estimate |
| Optimal stage effort $f_{opt}$ | ≈ **4** | Logical effort sizing rule |

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Valuation Tip:** Always include **units** in every numerical answer. Marks are deducted even for correct values if "ps" or "μm" is missing. Show **derivation steps**, not just final equations. KTU examiners allocate 2–3 marks per derivation step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compaction - Applications of Compaction Maximum Distance Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compaction - Applications of Compaction Maximum Distance Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Layout Compaction — Formal Academic Definition

**Layout Compaction** is a fundamental post-processing operation in the VLSI physical design cycle in which the geometric coordinates of every layout feature (rectangles, polygons, wires, cells, and vias) are translated along one or more coordinate axes so that the overall chip area is minimized, while **all design rules** and **electrical constraints** of the fabrication process continue to be satisfied.

Formally, compaction takes as input a *legal-but-redundant* layout (a layout that obeys every minimum spacing rule but contains empty whitespace) and produces an *optimal-or-near-optimal* equivalent layout by translating each feature as close as possible to its neighbours.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, compaction is categorized into:
- **1-D Compaction** (one-dimensional, axis-by-axis sweep)
- **2-D Compaction** (simultaneous X and Y compression)
- **Hierarchical Compaction** (compaction of cell libraries and macro blocks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme module explicitly emphasizes *constraint-graph based 1-D compaction*, *Maximum Distance Constraints (Upper Bounds)*, and *applications to mask generation*. Mastery of these three sub-topics is essential for Module 3 university examination questions.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "Packing a Suitcase"

Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a trip. You have:

| Suitcase Element | VLSI Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Clothes (objects) | Layout features (transistors, wires, contacts) |
| Folding clothes to remove air gaps | Compaction algorithm shifting features closer |
| Rule: no shirt on top of liquids (spacing) | Minimum design rule constraints |
| Rule: fragile items must be reached within a short time | **Maximum Distance Constraints** |
| Zipper boundary | Chip boundary / die area |

In the suitcase analogy:
- **Minimum Distance Constraints** ensure liquids do not spill on clothes (lower-bound spacing).
- **Maximum Distance Constraints** ensure that frequently-used items are not buried too deep — there is an *upper bound* on how far apart two related items may be. In VLSI, this corresponds to limits such as "the gate polysilicon must extend beyond the diffusion by *at least* $L_{poly}$ but *no more* than $L_{poly,max}$" or "a clock wire cannot exceed a maximum length before buffering."

## 1.3 Maximum Distance Constraints — Definition

A **Maximum Distance Constraint** (also called an **Upper-Bound Constraint** or **Maximum Separation Constraint**) restricts the *upper limit* on the distance between two layout features. In contrast to minimum spacing rules, these constraints **prevent features from being packed too far apart**.

Mathematical representation in horizontal compaction:

$$x_j - x_i \leq D_{ij}^{\max}$$

where $x_i, x_j$ are the right/left edges of features $i$ and $j$, and $D_{ij}^{\max}$ is the maximum permitted separation (in microns or lambda units).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** Minimum distance constraints are *one-sided lower bounds* (everything must be at least $d$ apart). Maximum distance constraints are *one-sided upper bounds* (nothing may be more than $D$ apart). A complete 1-D constraint graph contains *both* types simultaneously.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value | KTU Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum feature size $\lambda$ | $0.5\ \mu m$ to $7\ nm$ | $\lambda$ |
| Design rule unit (DPL) | $0.5\ \mu m$ | $\mu m$ |
| Poly-to-diffusion overlap | $0.5\ \lambda$ to $2\ \lambda$ | $L_{poly}$ |
| Maximum poly extension (antenna) | $5\ \mu m$ – $50\ \mu m$ | $D_{antenna}$ |
| Critical wire length (no buffer) | $100\ \mu m$ | $L_{crit}$ |
| Standard cell height | $12\ \lambda$ | $H_{cell}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Compaction Pipeline (Operational Breakdown)

The complete compaction flow consists of **six logical stages**, executed sequentially by the EDA tool:

1. **Layout Parsing & Feature Extraction**
   - The symbolic or geometric layout (GDS-II, CIF, or DEF) is read.
   - Each rectangle is converted into an *interval* on the X and Y axes, with explicit *left*, *right*, *top*, and *bottom* coordinate variables.

2. **Constraint Generation**
   - Design rules are translated into *constraint inequalities* between feature coordinates.
   - **Minimum** spacing rules yield: $x_j - x_i \geq d_{ij}^{\min}$.
   - **Maximum** spacing rules yield: $x_j - x_i \leq D_{ij}^{\max}$.

3. **Constraint Graph Construction**
   - Each layout feature is a *node* $v_i$ with coordinate $x_i$.
   - Each constraint $x_j - x_i \geq c$ becomes a *directed weighted edge* $i \rightarrow j$ of weight $c$.

4. **Constraint Solving (Longest-Path Computation)**
   - Find a feasible solution $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ that satisfies every edge.
   - For minimum constraints alone, this is the **longest path problem** in a DAG.
   - When maximum constraints are added, the problem becomes a **system of difference constraints with both bounds**.

5. **Coordinate Assignment & Iteration**
   - Assign concrete coordinates.
   - Re-check design rules (a critical step — design-rule violations may surface).
   - Iterate if necessary.

6. **Layout Re-emission**
   - Write back the compacted coordinates to the mask database.

## 2.2 Maximum Distance Constraints — Analytical Breakdown

Maximum distance constraints arise from *electrical*, *topological*, and *process* requirements:

### 2.2.1 Origin 1 — Electrical / Performance Constraints
- **Signal Integrity**: A clock distribution net must be shorter than $L_{clk,max}$ to avoid excessive skew.
- **RC Delay**: To keep the delay below $\tau_{max}$, the wire from driver to load is bounded: $x_{load} - x_{driver} \leq L_{crit}$.

### 2.2.2 Origin 2 — Process / Antenna Constraints
- The polysilicon gate cannot extend *unconnected* beyond the active area by more than $D_{antenna,max}$, else charge accumulation damages the gate oxide.

### 2.2.3 Origin 3 — Topological / Connectivity Constraints
- Two terminals of a via must be *close enough* to guarantee overlap during lithographic variation: $x_{via,top} - x_{via,bot} \leq \delta_{via}$.

### 2.2.4 Origin 4 — Yield / Lithographic Constraints
- Maximum line-end extension to avoid line-end shortening during OPC.

## 2.3 The Bounded-Coordinate Formulation

A complete 1-D horizontal compaction problem with both bound types is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x_i}\quad & x_{n} - x_{1} \\
\text{subject to}\quad & x_j - x_i \geq d_{ij}^{\min} \quad \forall\ (i,j) \in E_{\min} \\
& x_j - x_i \leq D_{ij}^{\max} \quad \forall\ (i,j) \in E_{\max} \\
& x_i^{\min} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{\max} \quad \forall\ i \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **Linear Programming (LP)** problem with a totally unimodular constraint matrix, solvable in **polynomial time**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Inequality | Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_j - x_i \geq d_{ij}^{\min}$ | Minimum spacing constraint | Design rule check |
| 2 | $x_j - x_i \leq D_{ij}^{\max}$ | **Maximum distance constraint** | Antenna, delay, line-end |
| 3 | $x_{n} - x_{1} = \sum_{e \in \text{critical path}} w_e$ | Final chip width | Area optimization |
| 4 | $\text{slack}(e) = x_j - x_i - w_e$ | Slack of edge $e = (i,j)$ | Tells how much an edge can stretch |
| 5 | $x_i = \max_{P \in \mathcal{P}_i}\ \sum_{e \in P} w_e$ | Final coordinate (LP longest-path) | DAG-based assignment |
| 6 | $D^{\max}_{\text{poly-diff}} = L_{poly,min} + \epsilon_{litho}$ | Maximum poly extension | Antenna rule |
| 7 | $L_{wire,max} = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_{max}}{r \cdot c}}$ | Max unbuffered wire length | RC delay bound |
| 8 | $T(V,E) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Topological sort cost | Pre-processing |
| 9 | $L(V,E) = O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | Longest path cost | Bellman-Ford |
| 10 | $W_{chip} = n \cdot \lambda \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i$ | Final compacted width | Final answer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Sheet Pitfall:** When writing $|x|$ or $|V|$ in tables, always use `\vert x\vert` or $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX. Inside a markdown table, the vertical bar `|` clashes with the column separator and breaks rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Maximum distance constraints and 1-D compaction together power:
- **Mask Data Preparation (MDP)** in foundries (TSMC, Samsung, Intel).
- **Standard Cell Library Compaction** in Synopsys IC Compiler, Cadence Innovus.
- **Lithography-Friendly Compaction** with inverse lithography technology (ILT) awareness.
- **3-D IC / TSV placement** where vertical stacking imposes *strict upper bounds* on layer-to-layer distances.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Design Rules to Constraint Graph

**Step 1.** Consider two layout rectangles $R_1$ and $R_2$ with horizontal edges $x_1$ (right edge of $R_1$) and $x_2$ (left edge of $R_2$).

**Step 2.** The minimum design rule states: they must be separated by at least $d$ units. Therefore:

$$x_2 - x_1 \geq d$$

**Step 3.** The maximum design rule states: due to antenna effect, they must NOT be separated by more than $D$ units. Therefore:

$$x_2 - x_1 \leq D$$

**Step 4.** Combining both into a single bounded interval:

$$d \leq x_2 - x_1 \leq D$$

**Step 5.** In the constraint graph $G = (V, E)$, we introduce two directed edges:
- Edge $1 \rightarrow 2$ with weight $d$ (lower bound).
- Edge $2 \rightarrow 1$ with weight $-D$ (the negation of the upper bound).

**Step 6.** The feasible coordinate vector $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ is any assignment that satisfies **all** edges simultaneously. Such a vector exists iff the constraint graph contains **no positive-weight cycle**.

## 3.2 Derivation — Longest Path in a DAG (Pure Minimum Constraints)

**Step 1.** Assume only minimum constraints exist. The problem is:

$$x_i \geq x_j + w_{j \rightarrow i} \quad \forall (j,i) \in E$$

**Step 2.** Perform topological sort on $G$, giving an ordering $v_{\sigma(1)}, v_{\sigma(2)}, \dots, v_{\sigma(n)}$.

**Step 3.** Initialize $x_{\sigma(1)} = x_{\sigma(1)}^{\min}$.

**Step 4.** For each subsequent vertex in topological order:

$$x_{\sigma(k)} = \max\Big(x_{\sigma(k)}^{\min},\ \max_{j:\ (j,\sigma(k)) \in E}\ \big(x_j + w_{j \rightarrow \sigma(k)}\big)\Big)$$

**Step 5.** This recurrence computes the longest-path distance from the source to every vertex, which is provably the **minimum-coordinate** solution (since any smaller coordinate would violate at least one minimum constraint).

## 3.3 Derivation — Critical Path and Final Chip Width

**Step 1.** After step 3.2, every edge $(j,i)$ has *slack*:

$$\text{slack}(j,i) = x_i - x_j - w_{j \rightarrow i} \geq 0$$

**Step 2.** Edges with $\text{slack}(j,i) = 0$ lie on a **critical path**.

**Step 3.** The total chip width is the longest path length from the leftmost (source) to the rightmost (sink) vertex:

$$W_{chip} = x_{n} - x_{1} = \sum_{e \in \text{critical path}} w_e$$

**Step 4.** Worked numerical example — Consider 4 features with these minimum constraints:
- $x_2 - x_1 \geq 2$
- $x_3 - x_2 \geq 3$
- $x_4 - x_3 \geq 2$
- $x_4 - x_1 \geq 10$

Topological order: $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$. Compute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 \\
x_2 &= \max(0,\ x_1 + 2) = 2 \\
x_3 &= \max(0,\ x_2 + 3) = 5 \\
x_4 &= \max(0,\ x_3 + 2,\ x_1 + 10) = \max(5,\ 10) = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Total chip width: $W_{chip} = x_4 - x_1 = 10$. Critical path: $x_1 \rightarrow x_4$ (edge weight $10$).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Minimum vs Maximum Distance Constraints

| Property | Minimum Distance Constraint | Maximum Distance Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| **Inequality type** | $\geq$ (lower bound) | $\leq$ (upper bound) |
| **Edge in graph** | $i \rightarrow j$, weight $+w$ | $i \rightarrow j$, weight $-w$ |
| **Graph effect** | Increases $x_j$ | Decreases $x_j$ |
| **Algorithm impact** | Adds to longest path | Subtracts from longest path |
| **Physical origin** | Lithography, DRC | Antenna, RC delay, line-end |
| **Detection** | Easy, edge slack = 0 | Difficult, requires pair-wise check |
| **Cycle condition** | Cycle implies infeasibility iff $\sum w > 0$ | Cycle implies infeasibility iff $\sum (-w) > 0$ i.e. $\sum w < 0$ |
| **Solver** | Bellman-Ford on lower bounds | Add virtual source with upper bounds, run longest-path in residual |
| **Slack definition** | $\text{slack} = x_j - x_i - w_{ij}^{\min}$ | $\text{slack} = D_{ij}^{\max} - (x_j - x_i)$ |

## 3.5 Derivation — Feasibility Check with Maximum Constraints

**Step 1.** Convert every max constraint $x_j - x_i \leq D$ into the lower-bound form $x_i - x_j \geq -D$.

**Step 2.** Add a dummy source node $s$ with edges $s \rightarrow v$ of weight $0$ for all $v$, and $v \rightarrow s$ of weight $-\infty$ (or cap at $L$).

**Step 3.** Run the **Bellman-Ford longest-path** algorithm.

**Step 4.** If the algorithm reports a *positive-weight cycle* in the augmented graph, the layout is **infeasible** — no coordinate assignment can satisfy all upper bounds.

**Step 5.** If no positive cycle exists, the resulting distances $d(s, v)$ are a valid minimum-coordinate solution.

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Minimum AND Maximum Together

Consider 3 features with both bound types:

$$x_2 - x_1 \geq 2 \quad (\text{min})$$
$$x_3 - x_2 \geq 3 \quad (\text{min})$$
$$x_3 - x_1 \leq 4 \quad (\text{max})$$

**Step 1.** Build the graph:
- Edge $1 \rightarrow 2$, weight $+2$.
- Edge $2 \rightarrow 3$, weight $+3$.
- Edge $1 \rightarrow 3$, weight $+4$ (max constraint rewritten as $x_3 - x_1 \geq -4$ is wrong; the correct rewrite for *upper* bound $x_3 - x_1 \leq 4$ gives the *equivalent lower bound*: $-(x_3 - x_1) \geq -4$, i.e., $x_1 - x_3 \geq -4$, i.e., edge $3 \rightarrow 1$ of weight $-4$).

**Step 2.** Compute longest paths:
- $x_1 = 0$
- $x_2 = x_1 + 2 = 2$
- $x_3 = \max(x_2 + 3,\ x_1 + \text{?}) = 5$ (only the lower-bound path applies)

**Step 3.** Check max constraint: $x_3 - x_1 = 5 \leq 4$? **Violated!**

**Step 4.** The graph contains a "negative-feasibility" cycle. Solution is infeasible — no coordinate assignment exists.

**Conclusion:** When both lower and upper bounds exist, *any inconsistency* between them makes the problem infeasible, signalling that the original symbolic layout violates an upper-bound design rule.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram — The Compaction Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Symbolic Layout CIF or GDS II] --> B[Feature Extractor]
    B --> C[Design Rule Database]
    C --> D[Constraint Generator]
    D --> E[Constraint Graph Lower bounds]
    D --> F[Constraint Graph Upper bounds]
    E --> G[Longest Path Solver Bellman Ford]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Feasible?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Coordinate Assigner]
    H -- No --> J[Report Violations Back Annotate]
    I --> K[Compacted Mask Layout]
    J --> B
    K --> L[MDP and OPC Stage]
    L --> M[Final Manufacturable Layout]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Constraint Generator** emits *two parallel streams* — one for minimum edges, one for maximum edges.
- Both streams feed into a unified solver, which performs feasibility detection.
- Infeasible layouts are returned for back-annotation (the user is told *which* upper bound was violated).

## 4.2 Diagram — Constraint Graph of a 4-Feature Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1((Feature 1)) -- x2 minus x1 >= 2 --> N2((Feature 2))
    N2 -- x3 minus x2 >= 3 --> N3((Feature 3))
    N3 -- x4 minus x3 >= 2 --> N4((Feature 4))
    N1 -- x4 minus x1 >= 10 upper bound preferred --> N4
    N3 -. x3 minus x1 less equal 6 maximum .-> N1
```

- **Solid arrows** represent minimum (lower-bound) constraints.
- **Dashed arrows** represent maximum (upper-bound) constraints.
- The arrow $N3 \rightarrow N1$ (dashed) enforces $x_3 - x_1 \leq 6$.

## 4.3 Diagram — Decision Flow When Maximum Constraints Are Active

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start 1 D Compaction]) --> P1[Parse layout and generate constraints]
    P1 --> P2{Separate lower and upper bounds}
    P2 -- Lower bounds --> Q1[Add as positive weight edges]
    P2 -- Upper bounds --> Q2[Rewrite and add as negative weight edges]
    Q1 --> R[Construct unified constraint graph]
    Q2 --> R
    R --> S[Add dummy source s]
    S --> T[Run Bellman Ford longest path]
    T --> U{Positive cycle detected?}
    U -- Yes --> V[Mark infeasible and report upper bound violations]
    U -- No --> W[Assign final coordinates x_i]
    W --> X[Emit compacted layout]
    X --> End([End])
    V --> End
```

## 4.4 Diagram — Hierarchical View of Maximum Constraint Sources

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Maximum Distance Constraints))
    Electrical
      RC delay bound
      Clock skew limit
      Antenna ratio
    Process
      Poly extension cap
      Line end shortening
      Via overlap margin
    Topological
      Connectivity window
      Critical path bounding
    Yield
      Lithography variation
      Defect tolerance

```

This mind-map visually summarizes the *engineering origins* of maximum distance constraints discussed in Section 2.2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mark-loss hotspot #1** — Students frequently write "$x_j - x_i = d$" (equality) when the constraint is *inequality*. Always use $\geq$ for minimum and $\leq$ for maximum.
> 2. **Mark-loss hotspot #2** — In maximum constraint problems, students forget to *negate the weight* when adding the edge to the constraint graph. The edge is $j \rightarrow i$ with weight $-D$, not $i \rightarrow j$ with weight $+D$.
> 3. **Mark-loss hotspot #3** — Failing to check feasibility (positive cycle). KTU 2024 papers explicitly award 2 marks for the feasibility verdict.
> 4. **Mark-loss hotspot #4** — Confusing *chip width* (longest path) with *number of edges*. Width is the *sum of edge weights on the critical path*, not the count.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Define "Layout Compaction" and list any three of its primary applications in the VLSI physical design flow.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Layout compaction is the post-layout process of translating geometric features of a chip design so as to **reduce the overall silicon area** while **maintaining design-rule correctness** and **electrical connectivity**.

Three primary applications:
1. **Chip Area Reduction** — directly cuts fabrication cost per die.
2. **Mask Data Generation** — produces the final manufacturable GDS-II used by the foundry.
3. **Design Rule Compliance Verification** — implicitly re-checks every spacing rule during the compression pass.

*(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for any three correctly stated applications.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between a Minimum Distance Constraint and a Maximum Distance Constraint in 1-D compaction. Provide one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Minimum Distance Constraint | Maximum Distance Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Inequality | $x_j - x_i \geq d^{\min}$ | $x_j - x_i \leq D^{\max}$ |
| Purpose | Prevent features from being too close | Prevent features from being too far |
| Example | Poly-to-active spacing $\geq 0.2\ \mu m$ | Maximum unbuffered wire length $\leq 100\ \mu m$ |

*(1.5 marks for the difference table, 1.5 marks for the example pair.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *Construct the constraint graph* for a layout consisting of **5 features** with the following horizontal minimum spacing rules:

- $x_2 - x_1 \geq 2$
- $x_3 - x_2 \geq 4$
- $x_4 - x_3 \geq 3$
- $x_5 - x_4 \geq 2$
- $x_5 - x_1 \geq 12$

Compute the **minimum-coordinate solution** and the **final chip width**. Identify the **critical path**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *Add a maximum distance constraint* $x_5 - x_1 \leq 11$ to the above problem. Show how the constraint graph changes and determine **whether the augmented problem is feasible**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Topological Order:**
Features can be ordered $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5$ since every constraint is forward. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Initialize Coordinates:**

$$x_1 = 0$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Propagate Minimum Constraints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2 &= x_1 + 2 = 2 \\
x_3 &= x_2 + 4 = 6 \\
x_4 &= x_3 + 3 = 9 \\
x_5 &= \max(x_4 + 2,\ x_1 + 12) = \max(11,\ 12) = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks — one for each line of computation.)**

**Step 4 — Final Chip Width:**

$$W_{chip} = x_5 - x_1 = 12 - 0 = 12$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Critical Path Identification:**
The edge $1 \rightarrow 5$ with weight $12$ has zero slack and dominates. The critical path is:

$$1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \quad (2+4+3+2 = 11) \quad \text{(not critical)}$$
$$1 \rightarrow 5 \quad (12) \quad \text{(critical)}$$

**(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Maximum Constraint Rewrite:**
$x_5 - x_1 \leq 11$ is equivalent to $x_1 - x_5 \geq -11$. Add a directed edge $5 \rightarrow 1$ of weight $-11$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Augmented Graph Cycles to Check:**

- Cycle $1 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 1$ has total weight $12 + (-11) = +1 > 0$. **Positive cycle detected.** **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Bellman-Ford Verdict:**
Bellman-Ford reports *positive cycle = infeasibility*. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**
The added maximum constraint $x_5 - x_1 \leq 11$ is **incompatible** with the existing minimum constraint $x_5 - x_1 \geq 12$. The two together force $11 \geq x_5 - x_1 \geq 12$, which is a contradiction. **(2 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat sketch of the **constraint graph**, explain how the **longest-path algorithm** (Bellman-Ford) computes the minimum-coordinate solution for a 1-D compaction problem containing only *minimum distance constraints*. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A layout has 4 features with the following *mixed* constraints:

- $x_2 - x_1 \geq 3$ (min)
- $x_3 - x_2 \geq 2$ (min)
- $x_4 - x_3 \geq 4$ (min)
- $x_4 - x_1 \leq 8$ (max)

Show the **augmented constraint graph**, check feasibility, and find the **final coordinates**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Graph Construction:** Each feature is a node; each minimum constraint $x_j - x_i \geq w$ becomes a directed edge $i \rightarrow j$ of weight $+w$. **(1 mark for sketch, 1 mark for description.)**

**Step 2 — Topological Sort:** Vertices are ordered such that all edges point forward. This is possible because the constraint graph (with only lower bounds) must be a DAG — otherwise no feasible solution exists. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Relaxation (Longest-Path) Recurrence:**
For each vertex $v$ in topological order, set:
$$x_v = \max_{(u,v) \in E}\ (x_u + w_{u \rightarrow v})$$
This relaxation guarantees every minimum constraint is satisfied *with equality whenever possible*. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Termination:** After $n - 1$ iterations (or one pass after topological sort), $x_v$ holds the *smallest feasible coordinate* for every feature. The final chip width is $x_{\max} - x_{\min}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Complexity:** $O(|V| + |E|)$ with topological sort + longest path, or $O(|V| \cdot |E|)$ with Bellman-Ford. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Translate Maximum to Lower Bound:**
$x_4 - x_1 \leq 8 \iff x_1 - x_4 \geq -8$. Add edge $4 \rightarrow 1$ of weight $-8$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Augmented Graph Edges:**
- $1 \rightarrow 2$, weight $+3$
- $2 \rightarrow 3$, weight $+2$
- $3 \rightarrow 4$, weight $+4$
- $4 \rightarrow 1$, weight $-8$

**(1 mark for listing)**

**Step 3 — Cycle Detection:**
Cycle $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1$ has total weight $3 + 2 + 4 + (-8) = +1 > 0$. **Positive cycle → INFEASIBLE.** **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Engineering Meaning:**
The minimum requirements force $x_4 - x_1 \geq 9$, but the maximum requirement forces $x_4 - x_1 \leq 8$. Contradiction. **No valid compaction exists.** **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Designer Action:**
The layout must be *re-routed* or *re-spaced* so that the natural spreading satisfies $x_4 - x_1 \leq 8$. Possible fix: relax $x_4 - x_3 \geq 4$ down to $\geq 3$. **(1 mark)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Compaction & Maximum Distance Constraints**

- **Compaction = area minimization + design-rule preservation**, not just shifting.
- **1-D compaction** sweeps one axis at a time; **2-D compaction** solves both simultaneously (NP-hard in general).
- **Constraint graph** $G = (V, E)$: nodes are features, edges are inequalities.
- **Minimum constraint** $x_j - x_i \geq w$ → directed edge $i \rightarrow j$ with weight $+w$.
- **Maximum constraint** $x_j - x_i \leq D$ → directed edge $j \rightarrow i$ with weight $-D$ (negation trick).
- **Feasibility test** = detect *positive-weight cycle* in the augmented graph (Bellman-Ford).
- **Final chip width** = longest-path length from source to sink = $\sum w_e$ over critical path.
- **Slack of edge** $e = (u,v)$ = $x_v - x_u - w_e \geq 0$; slack $= 0$ ⇒ edge lies on a critical path.
- **Applications** of compaction: area reduction, mask generation, DRC re-verification, cell-library compression, lithography-friendly output.
- **Maximum distance constraints** arise from: antenna effect, RC delay bound, clock skew, line-end shortening, and connectivity window.
- Always use `\vert` for absolute value in LaTeX tables to avoid breaking markdown.
- Always check for *both* lower and upper bound violations before finalizing the layout.
- **KTU 2024 hot keywords**: *longest path*, *critical path*, *Bellman-Ford*, *slack*, *positive cycle*, *feasibility*, *upper-bound constraint*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Placement - Wirelength Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Placement - Wirelength Estimation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Placement** is the stage in the VLSI physical design flow where every movable cell (standard cell, macro, or block) is assigned a unique legal coordinate `(x, y)` on the chip canvas such that there are no overlaps, and the design meets performance, area, and power targets. **Wirelength Estimation** is the *cost-evaluation* sub-problem of placement. It computes a fast, approximate total interconnection length for the current placement, **without performing detailed routing**, so that the placer's optimization engine can iteratively move cells to minimize the cost. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 3 — Placement):** > Wirelength estimation is the **objective function** that drives most modern placement algorithms. The faster, more accurate, and more *differentiable* the estimator, the better the placer can converge to a high-quality solution. Mathematically, the total estimated wirelength of a placement is: $$ W_{total} = \sum_{n \in N_{nets}} L(n) $$ where $N_{nets}$ is the set of all nets in the design and $L(n)$ is the estimated length of net $n$. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are **organizing furniture in a new house** before any electrician has wired the rooms. You cannot yet run cables, but you want the lamps, TV, and switchboards to be close enough that cables will be short and cheap. You cannot measure "cable length" directly because the cables are not yet installed. So you estimate it by: 1. Drawing an imaginary **rectangle** that encloses every appliance a cable will connect. 2. The *bigger* that rectangle, the *longer* the future cable. 3. You reshuffle furniture to **shrink** the rectangles. That rectangle-based estimate is the famous **Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL)** — the workhorse of VLSI placement. The placer does exactly the same: it minimizes the sum of bounding-box perimeters over millions of nets, and only after the layout is finalized does actual routing verify the prediction. ## 1.3 Why Wirelength Estimation Matters > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Motivation — Why we do NOT use real routing in placement:** > 1. **Full global routing can take hours for a single iteration** — placement needs *thousands* of iterations. > 2. Routing is **NP-hard** at every level; we need a **polynomial-time proxy** for length. > 3. The estimator must be **smooth and differentiable** so gradient-based solvers (e.g., quadratic, force-directed) can be applied. Wirelength estimation directly drives: - **Timing** (shorter wires → less delay) - **Congestion** (shorter wires → fewer routing tracks) - **Power** (wire capacitance ∝ length; dynamic power ∝ $C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$) - **Manufacturability** (long wires cause signal-integrity issues) - **Area** (routing area overhead) ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations The following metrics are universally referenced and form the backbone of the **Wirelength Estimation** module: | Metric | Acronym | Cost Type | Differentiability | |---|---|---|---| | Half-Perimeter Wire Length | **HPWL** | Linear, bounding-box | Not differentiable at corners | | Center-to-Center / Manhattan | **MST** / **CC** | Pairwise sum | Not differentiable | | Log-Sum-Exp | **LSE / Smoothed** | Smooth approximation | **Infinitely differentiable** | | Quadratic (Squared) | **Q** | Sum of squared distances | Smooth & differentiable | | Steiner Tree (rectilinear) | **RST** | Exact minimum tree | NP-hard | | Log-Sum-Exp (smooth) | **LSE** | Smooth approx to linear | Smooth & differentiable | **Bold terms** above are the standard notation students must use in KTU answer scripts. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Wirelength Estimation Models Wirelength estimation models are organized into three families based on the geometric object they construct per net: ### Family 1: **Bounding-Box Models** (Most Common in Industry) 1. **HPWL — Half-Perimeter Wire Length** 2. **BBox — Full Bounding Box length** 3. **Bounded-Skew / Bounded-Useful-Skew extensions** ### Family 2: **Graph Models** 1. **MST — Minimum Spanning Tree** (over net pins) 2. **Steiner Tree** (RST: Rectilinear Steiner Tree) 3. **Star / Hub model** (one star per net) 4. **Clique model** (complete graph) ### Family 3: **Probabilistic / Stochastic Models** 1. **Donath's hierarchical model** (used in Rent's-rule based estimation) 2. **LSE smooth approximation** (used in analytical placement) 3. **L₂ / Quadratic model** (used in quadratic placement) ## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation — Cell and Net Notation For a placement containing $n$ movable cells, each cell $i$ has center coordinate $(x_i, y_i)$. A **net** $e$ connects a set of pins $P_e$ belonging to a subset of cells. The estimated length of net $e$ is written as $L(e)$. - **Pin set of net $e$:** $P_e = \{ (x_p, y_p) \mid p \in \text{pins of } e \}$ - **Number of pins in net $e$:** $|P_e|$ (often denoted $k_e$ or $s(e)$) - **Total estimated wirelength:** $W = \sum_{e} L(e)$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master this table — questions on these formulas appear almost every semester.** | # | Estimator | Formula (per net $e$) | Properties | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **HPWL** | $L_{HPWL}(e) = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min})$ where $x_{min}=\min_{p\in P_e} x_p$, etc. | Tight upper bound on RST length. Additive. | KraftWerk, NTUplace, all commercial placers | | 2 | **BBox** | $L_{BB}(e) = 2 \cdot (x_{max}-x_{min} + y_{max}-y_{min})$ | Exactly twice HPWL | Simple estimation | | 3 | **Star (MST approx)** | $L_{star}(e) = \sum_{p \in P_e} d_1(p, c)$ where $c$ = Steiner point (often centroid) | Lower bound on MST | Fast estimation | | 4 | **MST** | $L_{MST}(e) = $ weight of minimum spanning tree on $P_e$ | NP-hard to compute optimal; solvable in $O(k \log k)$ for fixed $k$ | BonnPlace, Dragon | | 5 | **RST (Rectilinear Steiner Tree)** | $L_{RST}(e) = $ length of minimum rectilinear Steiner tree | NP-hard, exact algorithms exponential in $k$ | Final evaluation only | | 6 | **Quadratic** | $L_Q(e) = \frac{1}{s(e)} \sum_{p,q \in P_e, p [!IMPORTANT] > HPWL satisfies **three critical properties** that the KTU board examiner loves to test: 1. **Tight Upper Bound on RST:** $L_{HPWL}(e) \geq L_{RST}(e)$ always; equality when all pins of the net are aligned on the bounding box edges. 2. **Additivity:** $W_{HPWL} = \sum_e L_{HPWL}(e)$ — total cost decomposes per-net, enabling efficient incremental updates during cell moves. 3. **Incremental Update:** When one cell $i$ moves by $\Delta x$, only the nets touching cell $i$ need their $x_{min}/x_{max}$ re-evaluated — typically $O(1)$ amortized per move. **Smoothness problem (and the LSE cure):** HPWL is **not differentiable** at the pin-coordinates where $x_p = x_{min}$ or $x_p = x_{max}$. This breaks gradient-based placers. The **LSE approximation** replaces $\max$ and $\min$ with smooth log-sum-exp, recovering a smooth, differentiable cost. ## 2.5 Wirelength in Probabilistic / Rent's-Rule Framework For *random* placement (e.g., initial layout before optimization), the expected total wirelength can be derived from **Rent's Rule**: $$ T = k \cdot N^{p} $$ where: - $T$ = number of external pins (I/O) - $N$ = number of cells in the module - $k$ = Rent's constant - $p$ = Rent's exponent ($0 < p < 1$, typically $\sim 0.5$–$0.75$) **Donath's expected wirelength model** uses hierarchical partitioning and Rent's rule. The expected number of nets crossing a partition that splits the design into two halves of $N_1$ and $N_2$ cells is: $$ E[\text{nets cut}] = k \cdot N_1^p + k \cdot N_2^p - k \cdot N^p $$ Iterating this partitioning gives the expected total wirelength. The model predicts that $W \sim O(N)$ for $p < 1$, and that *better-organized* placements (higher $p$, fewer cuts per level) achieve shorter wires. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility Across Domains > [!NOTE] > **Where wirelength estimation shows up beyond EDA:** > - **PCB design** — board-level placement uses the same HPWL heuristics. > - **FPGA place-and-route** — VPR, the academic FPGA tool, uses bounding-box cost. > - **Network-on-Chip (NoC) mapping** — task-to-tile mapping minimizes Manhattan wire-length proxies. > - **Quantum layout synthesis** — modern quantum compilers minimize SWAP-wirelength using HPWL-style metrics. > - **Robotics / Logistics** — facility layout problems use identical bounding-box cost models. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section derives the three estimators most often asked in KTU examinations: **HPWL**, **Quadratic**, and **LSE smooth approximation**, plus the **lower bound on Steiner length**. ## 3.1 Derivation of HPWL from Rectilinear Steiner Tree **Claim.** For any net $e$ with pin set $P_e$, the half-perimeter of its axis-aligned bounding box is a *tight upper bound* on the rectilinear Steiner tree length: $L_{HPWL}(e) \geq L_{RST}(e)$. **Proof (step-by-step):** *Step 1 — Define the bounding box.* Let $$ x_{min} = \min_{p \in P_e} x_p, \quad x_{max} = \max_{p \in P_e} x_p $$ $$ y_{min} = \min_{p \in P_e} y_p, \quad y_{max} = \max_{p \in P_e} y_p $$ *Step 2 — Construct the perimeter of the bounding box.* The rectangle $R_e = [x_{min}, x_{max}] \times [y_{min}, y_{max}]$ has perimeter $$ P_{BB}(e) = 2 (x_{max} - x_{min}) + 2 (y_{max} - y_{min}) $$ *Step 3 — HPWL is half the perimeter.* $$ L_{HPWL}(e) = \frac{P_{BB}(e)}{2} = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min}) $$ *Step 4 — Show that any rectilinear Steiner tree $T_e$ connecting all pins must "visit" all four sides of $R_e$.* To connect a pin in the bottom-left corner to a pin in the top-right corner, the tree must have total horizontal extent $\geq (x_{max} - x_{min})$ and total vertical extent $\geq (y_{max} - y_{min})$. Since the tree uses rectilinear (Manhattan) edges, the total length $L(T_e) \geq (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min}) = L_{HPWL}(e)$. *Step 5 — Constructive lower-bound example (a 2-pin net).* For a 2-pin net with pins $A = (x_1, y_1)$ and $B = (x_2, y_2)$, the unique Steiner tree is the Manhattan path: $$ L_{RST} = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert = L_{HPWL} $$ So HPWL is **tight** for 2-pin nets. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the Quadratic Wirelength Objective **Setup.** We seek a differentiable objective for analytical placement. The simplest symmetric, smooth choice is the **sum of squared Euclidean (or Manhattan) distances over all connected pin pairs**, divided by net size to make a multi-pin net's contribution independent of its degree. **Step-by-step derivation:** *Step 1 — Define the pairwise squared distance.* For a 2-pin net $\{i, j\}$: $$ d_{ij}^2 = (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 $$ *Step 2 — Generalize to a net $e$ of size $s(e)$.* Use the average pairwise squared distance: $$ q(e) = \frac{1}{s(e)} \sum_{p 0$, this is differentiable; as $\gamma \to 0^+$, the approximation converges exactly to $\max$, and as $\gamma \to \infty$ it converges to the average (lossy). *Step 2 — Replace $\min$ with the smooth approximation.* $$ \min(x_1, \dots, x_k) = -\max(-x_1, \dots, -x_k) \approx -\gamma \log \left( \sum_{p=1}^k e^{-x_p / \gamma} \right) $$ *Step 3 — Smooth the horizontal span.* $$ \begin{aligned} \widetilde{x}_{span}(e) &= \gamma \log \left( \sum_{p \in P_e} e^{x_p/\gamma} \right) - \left[ -\gamma \log \left( \sum_{p \in P_e} e^{-x_p/\gamma} \right) \right] \\ &= \gamma \log \left( \sum_{p} e^{x_p/\gamma} \right) + \gamma \log \left( \sum_{p} e^{-x_p/\gamma} \right) \end{aligned} $$ *Step 4 — Same for the vertical span.* $$ \widetilde{y}_{span}(e) = \gamma \log \left( \sum_p e^{y_p/\gamma} \right) + \gamma \log \left( \sum_p e^{-y_p/\gamma} \right) $$ *Step 5 — Combine into smoothed HPWL.* $$ \boxed{\, L_{LSE}(e) \;=\; \gamma \left[ \log \sum_p e^{x_p/\gamma} + \log \sum_p e^{-x_p/\gamma} + \log \sum_p e^{y_p/\gamma} + \log \sum_p e^{-y_p/\gamma} \right] \,} $$ *Step 6 — Gradient with respect to cell $i$'s x-coordinate.* $$ \frac{\partial L_{LSE}}{\partial x_i} = \frac{e^{x_i/\gamma}}{\sum_p e^{x_p/\gamma}} - \frac{e^{-x_i/\gamma}}{\sum_p e^{-x_p/\gamma}} $$ This is well-defined, bounded in $[-1, 1]$, and drives gradient-based placers. ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Computing HPWL for a 4-pin Net > [!NOTE] > **Typical 3-mark KTU Part-A question:** "A net connects four pins at (0,0), (4,1), (2,3), (1,5). Compute HPWL." **Solution.** *Step 1.* Identify $x$-coordinates: $\{0, 4, 2, 1\}$. Identify $y$-coordinates: $\{0, 1, 3, 5\}$. *Step 2.* Compute extremes: $$ x_{min} = 0, \quad x_{max} = 4, \quad y_{min} = 0, \quad y_{max} = 5 $$ *Step 3.* Apply HPWL formula: $$ L_{HPWL} = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min}) = (4 - 0) + (5 - 0) = 4 + 5 = 9 $$ *Step 4.* Final answer: $\boxed{L_{HPWL} = 9 \text{ units}}$ For comparison, the **BBox** (full perimeter) is $2 \times 9 = 18$ units, and the **MST** (sum of three shortest Manhattan edges) would be $\sim 10$ units — HPWL is the *tightest* simple linear estimator. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Estimator Comparison | Estimator | Tightness vs. RST | Computational Cost | Differentiability | Used by | |---|---|---|---|---| | **HPWL** | Upper bound | $O(k)$ per net | No | All commercial | | **MST** | $\le 1.5 \times$ RST | $O(k \log k)$ | No | Dragon, BonnPlace | | **RST (exact)** | Tight (definition) | NP-hard | No | Final eval only | | **Quadratic $W_Q$** | Loose (over-weights long nets) | $O(n)$ per iter (CG) | Smooth (∞) | KraftWerk, RQL | | **LSE** | $\to$ HPWL as $\gamma\to 0$ | $O(k)$ per net | Smooth (∞) | mPL, NTUplace, ePlace | | **Star (centroid)** | Loose | $O(k)$ | No | Initial estimate | | **Steiner lower bound** | Lower bound on RST | $O(k)$ | No | Lower-bounding cost | ## 3.6 Derivation of Steiner-Tree Lower Bound (Quick) For a 2-pin net: $L_{RST} = d_1(A, B) = L_{HPWL}$. For a 3-pin net with pin centroid $c_e = (\bar{x}, \bar{y})$: $$ L_{SLB}(e) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{p \in P_e} d_1(p, c_e) $$ For larger nets, the lower bound $L_{SLB}(e) \leq L_{RST}(e)$ is derived by the fact that any rectilinear Steiner tree's total edge length is at least the sum of distances from the centroid (each edge "covers" at most 2 pins' contributions). This is a high-yield KTU question, so students are advised to memorize the centroid-based bound. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Placement-Flow Block Diagram (with Wirelength Estimation as Cost Engine) ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Parsed Netlist and Library] --> A1[Initial Random / Heuristic Placement] A1 --> A2[Current Cell Coordinates] A2 --> B1{Netlist Hypergraph} B1 --> B2[HPWL Estimator] B1 --> B3[Quadratic Estimator] B1 --> B4[LSE Smooth Estimator] B1 --> B5[Steiner / MST Estimator] B2 --> C1[Total Cost WL_old] B3 --> C1 B4 --> C1 B5 --> C1 A2 --> D1[Local Move Engine] D1 --> D2[Perturbed Coordinates] D2 --> B1 D2 --> C2[Total Cost WL_new] C1 --> E1[Cost Comparator] C2 --> E1 E1 --> F1{Better?} F1 -- Yes --> F2[Accept Move] F1 -- No --> F3[Reject Move] F2 --> G1[Convergence Check] F3 --> G1 G1 -- Not Converged --> D1 G1 -- Converged --> H1[Final Placed Layout] H1 --> H2[Detailed Routing] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **Netlist Hypergraph** is read in parallel by *four* estimators — the placer's configuration selects one (or blends several). - The cost comparator implements the **Simulated Annealing acceptance rule**: $P(\text{accept}) = \exp(-\Delta WL / T)$, where $T$ is the annealing "temperature" gradually lowered to zero. ## 4.2 Bounding-Box Diagram (HPWL Visual Intuition) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph netBB[Net e Bounding Box] direction LR P1((Pin A
x1y1)) --- P2((Pin B
x2y2)) P2 --- P3((Pin C
x3y3)) P3 --- P4((Pin D
x4y4)) end XMIN["xmin"]:::corner --> XMAX["xmax"]:::corner YMIN["ymin"]:::corner --> YMAX["ymax"]:::corner XMAX -.- YMAX XMIN -.- YMIN XMIN -.- YMAX XMAX -.- YMIN classDef corner fill:#FFD966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Concept:** The four corner cells of the bounding box completely determine the HPWL. Movement of a cell *inside* the box does not change HPWL — a key feature exploited by **local-rectangle** move operators in analytic placement. ## 4.3 Estimator Spectrum — Smoothness vs. Accuracy ```mermaid flowchart LR L2[Quadratic L2
Smooth, Loosest] --> STAR[Star / Centroid
Cheap Linear] STAR --> MST[MST
Moderate, k log k] MST --> LSE[LSE
Smooth Approx to HPWL] LSE --> HPWL[HPWL
Industry Default] HPWL --> RST[Rectilinear Steiner
Exact, NP-hard] classDef smooth fill:#B4C7E7,stroke:#1F3864,color:#000 classDef exact fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000 class L2,LSE smooth class RST exact ``` **Reading the diagram:** Movement from left → right increases accuracy but worsens computational cost and/or smoothness. Practical placers pick a point near **LSE / HPWL** for the best trade-off. ## 4.4 Incremental Update Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD M0[Cell i moves Delta x] --> M1{Scan nets touching i} M1 --> M2a[Net e1] M1 --> M2b[Net e2] M1 --> M2c[Net ek] M2a --> N1a[Re-eval xmin_xmax] M2b --> N1b[Re-eval xmin_xmax] M2c --> N1c[Re-eval xmin_xmax] N1a --> P1[Delta HPWL contribution e1] N1b --> P2[Delta HPWL contribution e2] N1c --> P3[Delta HPWL contribution ek] P1 --> S[Sum: net cost change] P2 --> S P3 --> S S --> ACC[Accept or Reject Move] ``` This is the *engine of simulated annealing and force-directed placers*. Only the **k nets touching cell i** (typically 4–8 in standard-cell designs) are re-evaluated per move, making the placement loop feasible for designs with millions of cells. ## 4.5 LSE Smoothing vs. HPWL — Visual of the Smoothness Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Pin p1 at x=2] --> HPWLOP[max op / min op] P2[Pin p2 at x=5] --> HPWLOP P3[Pin p3 at x=8] --> HPWLOP HPWLOP --> HOUT[xspan = max - min = 6
NOT Differentiable] P1 --> LSEOP[log sum exp / log sum exp -x] P2 --> LSEOP P3 --> LSEOP LSEOP --> LOUT[xspan_tilde = gamma log sum e x over gamma + gamma log sum e -x over gamma
INFINITELY DIFFERENTIABLE] ``` **Why this matters:** Gradient-based placers (mPL, NTUplace, ePlace) cannot compute $\partial \max / \partial x$. The LSE surrogate solves this elegantly. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL). [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **HPWL (Half-Perimeter Wire Length)** is a fast, bounding-box-based estimator for the total length of a net in a placement. For a net $e$ connecting a set of pins $P_e$ with coordinates $(x_p, y_p)$, the HPWL is defined as: > > $$L_{HPWL}(e) = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min})$$ > > where $x_{min} = \min_{p \in P_e} x_p$, $x_{max} = \max_{p \in P_e} x_p$, and similarly for $y_{min}, y_{max}$. > > HPWL is widely used in placement because it is **(i)** a tight upper bound on the rectilinear Steiner tree length, **(ii)** additive over nets, and **(iii)** admits efficient $O(1)$ amortized incremental updates when a single cell moves. Its drawback is **non-differentiability** at the points where a pin coordinate equals the bounding-box corner. --- ### Q2. List any three wirelength estimators used in placement and state one limitation of HPWL. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Model Answer (3 marks):** Three wirelength estimators used in placement are: 1. **HPWL** (Half-Perimeter Wire Length) — bounding-box based. 2. **Quadratic** — sum of squared distances, differentiable. 3. **LSE (Log-Sum-Exp)** — smooth approximation of HPWL. 4. **MST (Minimum Spanning Tree)** — graph-based. 5. **RST (Rectilinear Steiner Tree)** — exact but NP-hard. **Limitation of HPWL:** HPWL is **not differentiable** at the bounding-box corners, which prevents its direct use in gradient-based analytical placers. To overcome this, smooth approximations such as LSE are employed. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Format (per 2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, with an internal choice between Q-A and Q-B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) #### Q-A(a) Derive the HPWL formula for a 2-pin and 3-pin net. Show that HPWL is a tight upper bound on the rectilinear Steiner tree length. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — 2-pin net case.* For a net with pins $A = (x_1, y_1)$ and $B = (x_2, y_2)$: $$ L_{HPWL} = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert = d_1(A, B) = L_{RST} $$ For a 2-pin net, the unique rectilinear Steiner tree is the Manhattan path, and HPWL is *exact* (not just a bound). *Step 2 — 3-pin net case.* For a net with pins $A, B, C$, define the bounding box $R = [\min x, \max x] \times [\min y, \max y]$. Then: $$ L_{HPWL} = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min}) $$ This corresponds to a "rectilinear H" or "L" tree that visits the extreme pin-coordinates. *Step 3 — Upper-bound proof.* Any rectilinear Steiner tree $T$ connecting all pins must have: - Total horizontal extent ≥ $(x_{max} - x_{min})$. - Total vertical extent ≥ $(y_{max} - y_{min})$. Since rectilinear edges contribute exactly their length to the total, summing horizontally and vertically: $$ L(T) \geq (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min}) = L_{HPWL} $$ *Step 4 — Tightness.* Construct the L-shaped tree consisting of one horizontal segment spanning $x_{min} \to x_{max}$ at $y = y_{min}$ and one vertical segment spanning $y_{min} \to y_{max}$ at $x = x_{max}$. This tree's length is exactly $L_{HPWL}$, proving the bound is achieved when the pin-set allows it. Hence HPWL is a **tight** upper bound. $\blacksquare$ #### Q-A(b) Consider a placement with 5 nets. The pin coordinates are: - Net 1: (0,0), (4,2) - Net 2: (1,1), (1,5), (6,3) - Net 3: (2,7) - Net 4: (3,0), (5,4), (7,1), (0,6) - Net 5: (4,4), (4,8) Compute the total HPWL of the placement. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Model Solution:** For each net, identify $(x_{min}, x_{max}, y_{min}, y_{max})$ and apply $L_{HPWL} = (x_{max}-x_{min}) + (y_{max}-y_{min})$. *Net 1:* Pins (0,0) and (4,2). $x_{min}=0, x_{max}=4, y_{min}=0, y_{max}=2$. $$ L_{HPWL,1} = (4-0) + (2-0) = 4 + 2 = 6 $$ *Net 2:* Pins (1,1), (1,5), (6,3). $x_{min}=1, x_{max}=6, y_{min}=1, y_{max}=5$. $$ L_{HPWL,2} = (6-1) + (5-1) = 5 + 4 = 9 $$ *Net 3:* Single pin (2,7). A 1-pin net has zero wirelength: $$ L_{HPWL,3} = 0 $$ *Net 4:* Pins (3,0), (5,4), (7,1), (0,6). $x_{min}=0, x_{max}=7, y_{min}=0, y_{max}=6$. $$ L_{HPWL,4} = (7-0) + (6-0) = 7 + 6 = 13 $$ *Net 5:* Pins (4,4) and (4,8). $x_{min}=x_{max}=4, y_{min}=4, y_{max}=8$. $$ L_{HPWL,5} = (4-4) + (8-4) = 0 + 4 = 4 $$ *Total HPWL:* $$ W_{HPWL} = 6 + 9 + 0 + 13 + 4 = \boxed{32 \text{ units}} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) #### Q-B(a) Explain the Log-Sum-Exp (LSE) smoothing of HPWL. Derive the gradient of the smoothed cost with respect to a cell's x-coordinate. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Why smoothing is needed.* HPWL is not differentiable because $\max$ and $\min$ have discontinuous derivatives at their optima. Gradient-based analytical placers (mPL, ePlace, NTUplace) require a smooth cost. *Step 2 — Smooth approximation of $\max$.* The Log-Sum-Exp (LSE) approximates $\max$ as: $$ \max(x_1, \dots, x_k) \approx \gamma \log \left( \sum_{p=1}^{k} e^{x_p / \gamma} \right), \quad \gamma > 0 $$ *Step 3 — Smooth approximation of $\min$.* $$ \min(x_1, \dots, x_k) = -\max(-x_1, \dots, -x_k) \approx -\gamma \log \left( \sum_{p=1}^{k} e^{-x_p / \gamma} \right) $$ *Step 4 — Smoothed horizontal span.* $$ \widetilde{x}_{span}(e) = \gamma \log \left( \sum_{p \in P_e} e^{x_p / \gamma} \right) + \gamma \log \left( \sum_{p \in P_e} e^{-x_p / \gamma} \right) $$ *Step 5 — Total smoothed HPWL per net (x + y terms).* $$ L_{LSE}(e) = \gamma \left[ \log \sum_{p} e^{x_p/\gamma} + \log \sum_{p} e^{-x_p/\gamma} + \log \sum_{p} e^{y_p/\gamma} + \log \sum_{p} e^{-y_p/\gamma} \right] $$ *Step 6 — Gradient with respect to $x_i$ (cell $i$).* Let $S^+ = \sum_{p} e^{x_p/\gamma}$ and $S^- = \sum_{p} e^{-x_p/\gamma}$. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial L_{LSE}}{\partial x_i} &= \gamma \cdot \frac{1}{S^+} \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma} e^{x_i/\gamma} + \gamma \cdot \frac{1}{S^-} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\gamma}\right) e^{-x_i/\gamma} \\ &= \frac{e^{x_i/\gamma}}{S^+} - \frac{e^{-x_i/\gamma}}{S^-} \end{aligned} $$ This gradient is bounded in $[-1, +1]$ and is **infinitely differentiable** with respect to all cell coordinates, making it the cost of choice for analytical placers. $\blacksquare$ #### Q-B(b) Compare HPWL and the Quadratic wirelength objective. Under what condition does the quadratic objective become a poor estimator of true wirelength? (7 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Definitions.* **HPWL** is the half-perimeter of the bounding box of a net: $$ L_{HPWL}(e) = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min}) $$ **Quadratic wirelength** is the average of pairwise squared distances: $$ L_Q(e) = \frac{1}{s(e)} \sum_{p [!WARNING] > **Where students typically LOSE marks in Wirelength Estimation questions:** > > 1. **Confusing HPWL with BBox:** HPWL is **half** the perimeter of the bounding box. Many students mistakenly give the full perimeter. Always write the formula explicitly: > $$L_{HPWL} = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min})$$ > (one sum, NOT two sums). > > 2. **Forgetting to compute BOTH x-span and y-span:** HPWL requires the x-extent **and** y-extent. A common mistake is to add them as $2 \times (x_{max} - x_{min}) + 2 \times (y_{max} - y_{min})$ (this is the *full perimeter* or BBox). Read the question carefully! > > 3. **Treating 1-pin / 0-pin nets incorrectly:** A 1-pin net contributes **zero** to HPWL. A 0-pin net (no pins) is an *invalid net* and should not be considered. > > 4. **Forgetting to sum over all nets:** A common pitfall is to compute HPWL for one net and forget the total cost is the *sum over all nets*: > $$W_{HPWL} = \sum_{e \in N} L_{HPWL}(e)$$ > Always state the total. > > 5. **Confusing MST and Steiner Tree:** MST is a polynomial-time *upper bound* on RST; for multi-pin nets, $L_{MST} \leq 1.5 \times L_{RST}$ but $L_{MST} \geq L_{RST}$. Don't write $L_{MST} = L_{RST}$ — they are equal only for 2-pin nets. > > 6. **Units and notation:** Always state "units" (e.g., $\mu m$). Use $\max$ and $\min$, not "maximum" and "minimum" written in words, in your formula sheet. > > 7. **LSE limit behaviour:** When asked "what is $\lim_{\gamma \to 0^+} L_{LSE}$?", the answer is exactly $L_{HPWL}$. Examiners love to test this limit. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Placement: Wirelength Estimation** > > **Core Definitions (memorize verbatim):** > - [ ] HPWL = $(x_{max}-x_{min}) + (y_{max}-y_{min})$ — half the bounding-box perimeter. > - [ ] BBox (full) = $2 \cdot L_{HPWL}$ — full perimeter. > - [ ] Manhattan distance $d_1(A,B) = \vert x_A - x_B \vert + \vert y_A - y_B \vert$. > - [ ] Euclidean distance $d_2(A,B) = \sqrt{(x_A-x_B)^2 + (y_A-y_B)^2}$. > - [ ] Quadratic cost = average pairwise squared distance, divided by net size. > - [ ] LSE smoothing = $\gamma \log \sum e^{x/\gamma}$ approximates $\max$. > > **Estimator Spectrum (in order of tightness):** > 1. **Loosest:** Quadratic (smooth, but over-weights long nets) > 2. **Moderate:** Star / centroid-based > 3. **Tight upper bound:** HPWL > 4. **Tightest polynomial:** MST (≤ 1.5 × RST) > 5. **Exact:** RST (Rectilinear Steiner Tree, NP-hard) > > **Critical Properties of HPWL:** > - [ ] **Additivity** over nets. > - [ ] **Tight upper bound** on RST length. > - [ ] **Tight (= exact)** for 2-pin nets. > - [ ] **Not differentiable** at bounding-box corners. > - [ ] **$O(1)$ amortized incremental update** per cell move. > > **Use-Case Mapping (write this in the exam):** > - [ ] HPWL → Simulated annealing placer, simulated cost engine. > - [ ] Quadratic → KraftWerk, FastPlace (analytical, gradient-based). > - [ ] LSE → mPL, NTUplace, ePlace (modern analytical placers). > - [ ] MST → Dragon, BonnPlace (high quality but slower). > - [ ] RST → Final post-placement evaluation only. > > **LSE Smoothness — the limit to remember:** > $$\lim_{\gamma \to 0^+} L_{LSE}(e) = L_{HPWL}(e)$$ > $$\lim_{\gamma \to \infty} L_{LSE}(e) = \frac{4\gamma \log(s(e))}{\text{(lossy average)}}$$ > > **Stochastic Context:** > - [ ] Rent's Rule: $T = k N^p$ — relates pin-count to cell-count. > - [ ] Donath's hierarchical model — predicts expected wirelength from recursive partitioning. > - [ ] Random placement expected length: $W \sim O(N)$, and $W$ scales with $\sqrt{N/A}$ where $A$ = die area. > > **Key 3-mark recall questions:** > 1. HPWL formula? > 2. Why is LSE needed? > 3. Define Steiner tree / MST / RST. > 4. State Rent's Rule. > 5. Difference between HPWL and Quadratic. > 6. What is the "star-convergence" problem? > > **Numericals to practice before every exam:** > - [ ] HPWL of a 3-pin / 4-pin net from given coordinates. > - [ ] Total HPWL of a placement with 4–5 nets. > - [ ] Quadratic cost of a 2-pin / 3-pin net. > - [ ] Show that $L_{HPWL} = L_{RST}$ for a 2-pin net.

topics.title: Weighted Wirelength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weighted Wirelength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — WEIGHTED WIRELENGTH

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weighted Wirelength (WWL)** is an extension of the conventional wirelength estimation model used in **VLSI placement** where each net $n$ in the circuit is assigned a **non-negative scalar coefficient $w_n$** (a weight) representing the relative criticality, timing urgency, congestion contribution, or design priority of that net. The total placement cost is the sum of the products of these weights with the geometric length of each net.

Formally, given a netlist $N = \{n_1, n_2, \dots, n_m\}$ with placement positions $\{x_i, y_i\}$ for each cell $i$, the **weighted wirelength objective** is:

$$
W_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n \in N} w_n \cdot L(n)
$$

where $L(n)$ is the geometric length estimator of net $n$ (e.g., HPWL, quadratic, or LSE) and $w_n \geq 0$ is the net weight. The unweighted formulation is recovered by setting $w_n = 1$ for all $n \in N$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — City Traffic Engineering:** Imagine you are the chief urban planner of a large metropolitan city. You are laying out roads (interconnects) connecting buildings (cells). Some roads are **critical highways** that connect hospitals, airports, and industrial zones — they MUST be short and direct. Other roads are **residential lanes** — their length is less critical, and the city can tolerate them being a bit longer to save space.
>
> In a standard (unweighted) wirelength model, **every road is treated equally** — the planner only counts total kilometers of asphalt. In the **weighted wirelength model**, the planner **multiplies the length of each road by an importance factor (the weight $w_n$)**. A 10 km hospital road with $w=5$ contributes **50** to the cost, while a 10 km residential lane with $w=1$ contributes only **10**.
>
> Hence, the optimization engine (the planner) is forced to **shorten the high-weight (critical) nets first** because they "hurt" the objective function the most.

### 1.3 Why Weighted Wirelength Is Necessary in Modern Placement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard (unweighted) wirelength minimization is **timing-agnostic**, **congestion-agnostic**, and **signal-integrity-agnostic**. In real chip design, the placement stage must simultaneously satisfy multiple heterogeneous objectives:
> - **Timing-critical nets** (e.g., clock distribution, register-to-register paths) demand very short wires to meet setup/hold slack.
> - **Congested regions** (areas of high pin density) require wirelength spread to relieve routing pressure.
> - **Power-hungry nets** must be short to minimize dynamic power dissipation.
> - **Noise-sensitive nets** (analog, mixed-signal) need close adjacency to avoid coupling.
>
> Assigning a **higher weight $w_n$** to such nets forces the placement optimizer to devote more "effort" (geometric displacement budget) to shortening them.

### 1.4 Categorization of Net Weights

| Weight Type | Driving Objective | Typical Range of $w_n$ |
|---|---|---|
| Timing weight | Critical path delay | $1.0$ – $10.0$ |
| Congestion weight | Routability | $1.0$ – $3.0$ |
| Power weight | Dynamic power | $1.0$ – $2.0$ |
| Crosstalk weight | Signal integrity | $1.5$ – $5.0$ |
| Uniform weight | Pure wirelength | $w_n = 1$ for all $n$ |

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The **most commonly used weight ranges** in industrial EDA tools (e.g., Synopsys ICC2, Cadence Innovus) are:
> - $w_{\text{timing}} \in [2, 8]$ for paths on the critical list.
> - $w_{\text{congestion}} \in [1, 2]$ for nets crossing congested global routing tiles.
> - The total weighted wirelength is reported in **micrometers ($\mu m$)** or **design units** of the technology node (e.g., **nm** for sub-7nm nodes).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Position of Weighted Wirelength in the EDA Flow

The weighted wirelength model is consumed by the **global placer** in the **placement stage** of the RTL-to-GDSII flow. It is consumed by two major families of placement algorithms:

- **Quadratic placers** (e.g., Gordian, FastPlace, SimPL) → use **weighted quadratic wirelength**.
- **Non-linear / analytical placers** (e.g., ePlace, RePlAce, DREAMPlace) → use **weighted LSE (Log-Sum-Exp)** or weighted weighted-average wirelength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The weight $w_n$ is a **multiplicative scaling constant** that does not alter the *shape* of the length function $L(n)$ but changes the *relative importance* of each net in the gradient and Hessian of the placement optimization.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation — Generic

The placement problem with weighted wirelength is the constrained optimization:

$$
\min_{(x, y)} \;\; \Phi(x, y) = \sum_{n \in N} w_n \cdot L_n(x, y)
$$

subject to:
- **Density constraints:** $\rho_r(x, y) \leq \rho_{\max}$ for every bin $r$ in the placement canvas.
- **Legality constraints:** each cell $i$ must be placed on a legal site, row, or region.
- **Fixed-cell constraints:** pre-placed macros must not move.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of the Three Major Models

### Model A — Weighted Half-Perimeter Wire Length (Weighted HPWL)

The **HPWL** of a net $n$ with cells $\{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k\}$ is the half-perimeter of the **bounding box** enclosing all pins:

$$
\text{HPWL}(n) = \left( \max_{i \in n} x_i - \min_{i \in n} x_i \right) + \left( \max_{i \in n} y_i - \min_{i \in n} y_i \right)
$$

When the weight $w_n$ is applied, the **weighted HPWL** is simply:

$$
W_{\text{HPWL}} = \sum_{n \in N} w_n \cdot \text{HPWL}(n)
$$

**Why and How it works:** HPWL is **piecewise-linear and convex but not differentiable**. The optimizer cannot take a smooth gradient. Most modern tools therefore replace it with a smooth approximation. However, the **final reported metric** in placement reports is almost always the weighted HPWL because it correlates well with post-route wirelength.

### Model B — Weighted Quadratic Wirelength

The **quadratic model** connects every pair of pins in a net by a "spring". For a 2-pin net $(i, j)$:

$$
L_q^{(i,j)}(n) = (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2
$$

For multi-pin nets, the **clique model** (all pairs) or the **star model** (one virtual center) is used. With the net weight $w_n$:

$$
W_q = \sum_{n \in N} w_n \cdot \sum_{(i,j) \in n} w_{ij} \left[ (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 \right]
$$

**Why and How it works:** The quadratic form yields a **sparse, symmetric positive-definite system $Ax = b$** that can be solved in $O(n^{1.5})$ time using **conjugate gradient** or **multi-grid** methods. The weight $w_n$ simply scales the corresponding entries in the Laplacian-like matrix $A$.

### Model C — Weighted Log-Sum-Exp (LSE) Wirelength

The **LSE approximation** smooths the max and min operators in HPWL using the log-sum-exp function:

$$
\max_i x_i \approx \frac{1}{\gamma} \ln \left( \sum_i e^{\gamma x_i} \right), \quad \min_i x_i \approx -\frac{1}{\gamma} \ln \left( \sum_i e^{-\gamma x_i} \right)
$$

The **weighted LSE wirelength** for a net $n$ is:

$$
W_{\text{LSE}}(n) = w_n \cdot \gamma \left[ \ln \left( \sum_{i \in n} e^{x_i / \gamma} \right) + \ln \left( \sum_{i \in n} e^{-x_i / \gamma} \right) + \ln \left( \sum_{i \in n} e^{y_i / \gamma} \right) + \ln \left( \sum_{i \in n} e^{-y_i / \gamma} \right) \right]
$$

**Why and How it works:** The parameter $\gamma$ (the *smoothness parameter*) controls the trade-off — small $\gamma \rightarrow$ tight approximation of HPWL, large $\gamma \rightarrow$ smoother but looser approximation. The weight $w_n$ scales the entire smooth cost.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Complete Formula Reference for Weighted Wirelength**

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weighted Total Wirelength | $W = \sum_{n \in N} w_n L(n)$ | $\mu m$ |
| 2 | Weighted HPWL (per net) | $w_n \left[ (\max x - \min x) + (\max y - \min y) \right]$ | $\mu m$ |
| 3 | Weighted Quadratic (per 2-pin net) | $w_n \left[ (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 \right]$ | $\mu m^2$ |
| 4 | Weighted Star Model (per net) | $w_n \sum_{i \in n} \left[ (x_i - x_c)^2 + (y_i - y_c)^2 \right]$, $x_c = \frac{1}{\vert n \vert}\sum x_i$ | $\mu m^2$ |
| 5 | Weighted Clique Model (per net) | $w_n \cdot \frac{2}{\vert n \vert} \sum_{i < j} \left[ (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 \right]$ | $\mu m^2$ |
| 6 | Weighted LSE (per net, x-dir) | $w_n \gamma \left[ \ln \sum e^{x_i/\gamma} + \ln \sum e^{-x_i/\gamma} \right]$ | $\mu m$ |
| 7 | LSE Approximation Error | $E \leq \frac{\ln \vert n \vert}{\gamma}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Gradient of Weighted LSE w.r.t. $x_i$ | $w_n \left[ \frac{e^{x_i/\gamma}}{\sum_k e^{x_k/\gamma}} - \frac{e^{-x_i/\gamma}}{\sum_k e^{-x_k/\gamma}} \right]$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Hessian Diagonal of Weighted LSE w.r.t. $x_i$ | $\frac{w_n}{\gamma} \left[ \frac{e^{x_i/\gamma}}{\sum_k e^{x_k/\gamma}} \left( 1 - \frac{e^{x_i/\gamma}}{\sum_k e^{x_k/\gamma}} \right) + (\text{similar for min term}) \right]$ | $1/\mu m$ |
| 10 | Weighted Quadratic System (matrix form) | $A(x) = b$ where $A_{ij} = w_n \cdot \alpha_{ij}$, $A_{ii} = \sum_{j \neq i} w_n \alpha_{ij}$ | sparse SPD |

> **Notation Note:** In Table 2.1, the symbol $\vert n \vert$ denotes the cardinality (number of pins) of net $n$. We use the LaTeX escape `\vert` to avoid corrupting the markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is weighted wirelength used in industry?**
> 1. **Timing-Driven Placement (TDP):** Slack-based weighting assigns higher $w_n$ to nets on near-critical paths so the placer pulls them short.
> 2. **Congestion-Driven Placement (CDP):** Nets crossing overcongested global-routing regions receive $w_n > 1$ to encourage spreading.
> 3. **Power-Driven Placement:** High-switching-activity nets are weighted higher to reduce $P = \alpha C V^2 f$.
> 4. **Mixed-Signal Placement:** Analog sensitive nets (e.g., clock-to-ADC) are upweighted to enforce proximity.
