> Without step (2), the homogeneous system $A\mathbf{x}=0$ has the trivial solution $\mathbf{x}=0$ (all cells at the origin). The pseudo-nets provide the *boundary conditions* that turn the system into a well-posed one.

## 2.3 The Density / Clustering Problem and Modern Fixes

Pure quadratic placement tends to place **densely connected clusters of cells at the geometric centroid** of their connected terminals, producing:
- Severe **overlap violations**.
- Long global wires between clusters.

Modern analytical placers therefore add a **repulsion / density term** that converts the pure quadratic objective into a **non-linear, non-quadratic** objective (e.g., GORDIAN by Kleinhans et al.):

$$\min_{x,y}\;\; \Phi_{\text{quad}}(x,y) \;+\; \lambda \cdot \Phi_{\text{density}}(x,y)$$

The density term is typically a log-sum-exp function of bin occupancies (similar to the electrostatic potential of a charge distribution). This is solved by an **inner-outer iteration**:
- *Outer iteration:* fix density weights, solve quadratic system.
- *Inner iteration:* update density bin potentials using the current placement.

Other historical improvements include:
- **Kraftwerk** (Eisenmann & Johannes, 1998) — adds a *density gradient* to the gradient of $\Phi$.
- **FastPlace / FastPlace3** (Viswanathan et al., Chu et al.) — uses the Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL) as a global objective but with quadratic internals for speed.
- **RQL (Robust Quadratic Placement)** — adds a stability term for incremental placement.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU answers, when asked "state the limitations of quadratic placement", always list **three points**: (1) cell clustering at the centre, (2) overlap violations, (3) ignores lower-bound / upper-bound density constraints. Then state the fix in *one* line: "Modern placers add a density term (GORDIAN / Kraftwerk)."

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Phi(x,y) = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}\big[(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2\big]$ | Total quadratic cost | µm² |
| 2 | $w_{ij}^{(e)} = 2/k_e$ | Clique weight for a k-pin net | dimensionless |
| 3 | $A_{ii} = \sum_{j \ne i} c_{ij}$ | Diagonal of Laplacian matrix | weight units |
| 4 | $A_{ij} = -c_{ij}$ for $i \ne j$ | Off-diagonal of Laplacian | weight units |
| 5 | $A\,\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ | Sparse linear system in x | Symmetric positive-definite (with pseudo-nets) |
| 6 | $b_i = \sum_{f \in F} c_{if}\, x_f$ | RHS — fixed-terminal contributions | µm · weight |
| 7 | $d_{ij}^{(2)} = (x_i-x_j)^2 + (y_i-y_j)^2$ | Squared Euclidean distance | µm² |
| 8 | $\Phi_{\text{total}} = \Phi_{\text{quad}} + \lambda \Phi_{\text{density}}$ | GORDIAN-style combined objective | mixed units |
| 9 | $\rho_b = \dfrac{\sum_{i \in b} A_i}{A_b}$ | Bin density (target $\rho_b \le 1$) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\nabla^2 \Phi_{\text{quad}} = 2A$ | Hessian of quadratic cost | $n \times n$ matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All matrix formulations above assume movable cells are indexed $1, \dots, n$ and fixed terminals are absorbed into the RHS vector $b$. If a problem statement gives different indexing, **state the assumption clearly** — it is the single most common KTU answer-validation error.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Cost Function to the Linear System

We begin with the global objective restricted to the *x*-coordinate (the *y* derivation is identical):

$$\Phi_x(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}\,(x_i - x_j)^2$$

**Step 1.** Expand the square:

$$(x_i - x_j)^2 \;=\; x_i^2 \;-\; 2 x_i x_j \;+\; x_j^2$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate with respect to $x_k$ (one specific movable cell). The chain rule gives:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x_k} (x_i - x_j)^2 \;=\; 2(x_i - x_j) \cdot \frac{\partial (x_i - x_j)}{\partial x_k} \;=\; 2(x_i - x_j)\cdot (\delta_{ik} - \delta_{jk})$$

where $\delta_{ik}$ is the Kronecker delta. So the derivative is non-zero only when $k = i$ or $k = j$.

**Step 3.** Assemble the gradient. For each pair $(i,j)$, the terms that contain $x_k$ are those where $k=i$ **or** $k=j$:

$$\frac{\partial \Phi_x}{\partial x_k} \;=\; \sum_{i<j} c_{ij} \cdot 2(x_i - x_j) \cdot (\delta_{ik} - \delta_{jk})$$

$$=\; 2 \sum_{j:\, j > k} c_{kj}\,(x_k - x_j) \;+\; 2 \sum_{i:\, i < k} c_{ik}\,(x_i - x_k) \cdot (-1)$$

$$=\; 2 \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\,(x_k - x_j)$$

(where we have used the symmetry $c_{kj}=c_{jk}$).

**Step 4.** Set the gradient to zero to find the stationary point:

$$\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\,(x_k - x_j) \;=\; 0$$

$$x_k \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj} \;-\; \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\, x_j \;=\; 0$$

**Step 5.** Separate fixed and movable indices. Let $F$ denote the fixed-terminal index set and $M$ the movable-cell index set. For $k \in M$:

$$x_k \sum_{j \in M} c_{kj} \;-\; \sum_{j \in M} c_{kj}\, x_j \;=\; \sum_{f \in F} c_{kf}\, x_f$$

Define $A_{kk} = \sum_{j \in M} c_{kj}$, $A_{kj} = -c_{kj}$ for $j \in M, j \ne k$, and $b_k = \sum_{f \in F} c_{kf} x_f$. Then:

$$\sum_{j \in M} A_{kj}\, x_j \;=\; b_k$$

**Step 6.** Stacking all $k \in M$ equations into matrix form yields the canonical result:

$$\boxed{\,A\,\mathbf{x} \;=\; \mathbf{b}\,} \qquad \text{with} \qquad \boxed{\,A_{ii} = \sum_{j} c_{ij}, \quad A_{ij} = -c_{ij}\,}$$

The same derivation with $y$ in place of $x$ gives $A\,\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}'$.

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix $A$ is symmetric, diagonally dominant (so all eigenvalues $\ge 0$), and, provided at least one pseudo-net or fixed terminal exists, **positive definite** — guaranteeing a unique solution.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

Consider a 3-cell, 2-net circuit:
- Net $e_1 = \{1, 2\}$ with weight 1
- Net $e_2 = \{2, 3\}$ with weight 1
- Cell 1 is a **fixed terminal** at $x_1 = 0$
- Cell 3 is a **fixed terminal** at $x_3 = 10$
- Cell 2 is **movable** — find $x_2$.

**Step 1.** Build the cost:

$$\Phi \;=\; 1\cdot(x_1 - x_2)^2 + 1\cdot(x_2 - x_3)^2$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate w.r.t. $x_2$:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dx_2} \;=\; -2(x_1 - x_2) + 2(x_2 - x_3) \;=\; 0$$

**Step 3.** Solve:

$$-2(0 - x_2) + 2(x_2 - 10) \;=\; 0$$

$$2x_2 + 2x_2 - 20 \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 4 x_2 \;=\; 20 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x_2 = 5$$

**Step 4.** Verify using the matrix form. The connectivity Laplacian (rows/cols indexed 1, 2, 3):

$$A \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Eliminating the fixed terminals (Schur-complement) gives the 1×1 system for $x_2$:

$$\begin{aligned} \big[A_{22} - A_{21} A_{11}^{-1} A_{12} - A_{23} A_{33}^{-1} A_{32}\big] x_2 \;=\; -A_{21} A_{11}^{-1} b_1 - A_{23} A_{33}^{-1} b_3 \end{aligned}$$

With $b_1 = 0$, $b_3 = 10$, $A_{11}=A_{33}=1$, $A_{21}=A_{23}=-1$:

$$\big[2 - 1 - 1\big] x_2 \;=\; 0 + 10 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 0\cdot x_2 = 10 \quad \text{(singular!)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This small example demonstrates the **need for pseudo-nets / grounding**: without them, the matrix $A$ restricted to the movable cells alone can be singular. The pseudo-net from cell 2 to a virtual pad at, say, $x_{\text{pad}} = 5$ adds $c_{2,\text{pad}} = 0.1$ to the diagonal, making $A$ positive definite and the system well-posed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — The Hessian and Uniqueness

The Hessian of $\Phi_x$ is the matrix of second partial derivatives. From Step 3 of Section 3.1:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \Phi_x}{\partial x_k \partial x_l} \;=\; 2\, A_{kl}$$

where $A$ is exactly the Laplacian defined above. Therefore:

$$\nabla^2 \Phi_x \;=\; 2A$$

Since $A$ is positive semi-definite (Laplacian property) and positive definite (with pseudo-nets), the Hessian is positive definite, and the stationary point is the **unique global minimum**. This is the formal reason quadratic placement is so robust — there is **no local-minimum problem**, in stark contrast to simulated annealing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Concepts to Equations

| Stage | Input | Mathematical Object | Output | Algorithm Step |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Netlist $N$ | Hypergraph $H = (V, N)$ | — | Read netlist |
| 2 | Hypergraph $H$ | Clique graph $G = (V, E)$ with $w_{ij}^{(e)} = 2/k_e$ | Edge weights $c_{ij}$ | Net expansion |
| 3 | Edge weights $c_{ij}$ + fixed pins | Laplacian $A$, RHS $b$ | $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ | Formulate |
| 4 | $A$, $b$ | Cholesky / CG solve | $\mathbf{x}^\star, \mathbf{y}^\star$ | Solve |
| 5 | $\mathbf{x}^\star, \mathbf{y}^\star$ | Cell coordinates | Legalized placement | Legalize |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Quadratic Placement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Netlist: cells, nets, fixed terminals] --> B[Hypergraph H]
    B --> C[Clique Model: 2/k_e edge weights]
    C --> D[Connectivity Graph G with edge weights c_ij]
    D --> E[Build Laplacian Matrix A]
    D --> F[Form RHS vector b from fixed terminals]
    E --> G[Sparse Symmetric Positive-Definite System A*x = b]
    F --> G
    E --> H[Sparse System A*y = b prime]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Cholesky or Conjugate Gradient Solver]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Optimal Coordinates x*, y*]
    J --> K{Density / Overlap Check}
    K -- Satisfied --> L[Legalized Placement]
    K -- Violated --> M[Add Density / Repulsion Term]
    M --> C
    L --> N[Output: Placed Layout]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The feedback loop (K → M → C) represents the **GORDIAN / Kraftwerk enhancement** to pure quadratic placement. In a textbook KTU answer describing *Breuer's original algorithm*, this loop is absent; mention it only if the question asks about *modern* analytical placement.

## 4.2 Mass-Spring Analogy Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph chip[Die Area]
        direction LR
        cell1((Cell 1)) -- spring1[Net e_ij] --- cell2((Cell 2))
        cell2 -- spring2[Net e_jk] --- cell3((Cell 3))
        cell1 -- spring3[Net e_ik] --- cell3
        padA((Pad TL)) -. pseudoNet1 .-> cell1
        padD((Pad BR)) -. pseudoNet2 .-> cell3
    end
    spring1 --> relax[Minimum-energy configuration]
    spring2 --> relax
    spring3 --> relax
    pseudoNet1 --> relax
    pseudoNet2 --> relax
    relax --> opt[Optimal placement]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **pseudo-nets** (dashed) are the small "anchor springs" that prevent the entire graph from collapsing to its centroid. Without them the system is rank-deficient and the spring system has no unique rest configuration.

## 4.3 Data-Structure View of the Sparse Linear System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Matrix A: n x n sparse]
        direction LR
        diagRow[Diagonal: sum of c_ij on row i]
        offDiag[Off-diagonals: -c_ij]
        sparsity[~5 to 15 percent non-zero]
    end
    A --> solver[Solver Choice]
    subgraph solver
        direction TB
        chol[Cholesky: O to n^1.5 if PSD]
        cg[Conjugate Gradient: O to n to 1.2 if SPD]
    end
    chol --> x[Vector x]
    cg --> x
    b[RHS b: contributions from fixed terminals] --> chol
    b --> cg
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram of Breuer's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Step 0: Read netlist] --> step1[Step 1: Build clique graph]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Add pseudo-nets to 4 corner pads]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Formulate A and b in x and y]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Solve A*x=b and A*y=b prime]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Optional density refinement]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Legalize rows / sites]
    step6 --> output[Output: Legal global placement]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define Quadratic Placement. Why is the squared Euclidean distance used as the wire-length metric instead of the linear (Manhattan / HPWL) metric?*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
Quadratic placement is an analytical global-placement algorithm that minimizes the sum of squared Euclidean distances between connected cells, by solving two sparse linear systems — one for *x* and one for *y* coordinates. The squared metric is preferred because: (i) it is **differentiable everywhere**, so the gradient is smooth; (ii) the gradient is **linear** in the coordinates, which reduces the entire optimization to a closed-form linear system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$; and (iii) it admits a natural **physical analogy** with the elastic (Hooke's-law) energy of a spring network. The linear (L₁) metric is more accurate for rectilinear routing but leads to a non-differentiable objective that must be solved by linear programming.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is a "pseudo-net" in quadratic placement? Why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
A pseudo-net is a **dummy connection** added between a movable cell and a *virtual pad* placed near a corner (or edge) of the die. It is necessary because, in the absence of fixed terminals, the Laplacian matrix restricted to the movable cells is **singular**: the homogeneous system $A\mathbf{x}=0$ admits only the trivial (all-zero) solution and no unique minimum exists. Pseudo-nets act as "grounding springs" that add a small weight to the diagonal of $A$, making it **positive definite** and the system well-posed. They also **spread the cells** outward toward the chip boundary, counteracting the natural tendency of quadratic placement to cluster cells at the centre.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the global quadratic placement objective starting from the squared Euclidean distance model, and show that minimizing it leads to a sparse symmetric positive-definite linear system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$. Define every term. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Consider a 4-cell circuit with nets $\{1,2\}$, $\{2,3\}$, $\{3,4\}$ each of unit weight. Cells 1 and 4 are **fixed** at $x_1 = 0$ and $x_4 = 12$ respectively. Cells 2 and 3 are movable. Formulate the $2\times 2$ system that gives the optimal $x_2, x_3$, and solve for the coordinates. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

1. **Cost function** (1 mark):
   $$\Phi_x = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}\,(x_i - x_j)^2$$

2. **Differentiation** (2 marks): For any movable cell $k$,
   $$\frac{\partial \Phi_x}{\partial x_k} = 2\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\,(x_k - x_j) = 0$$

3. **Rearrangement** (1 mark):
   $$x_k \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj} - \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\, x_j = 0$$

4. **Fixed-terminal separation** (1 mark): Define
   $$b_k = \sum_{f \in F} c_{kf}\, x_f \quad \text{and} \quad A_{kk} = \sum_{j} c_{kj},\; A_{kj} = -c_{kj}$$

5. **Matrix form** (1 mark):
   $$A\,\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$$

6. **Properties** (1 mark): $A$ is symmetric, sparse, and (with pseudo-nets) positive-definite; hence the solution is unique and is the global minimum since $\nabla^2 \Phi_x = 2A \succ 0$.

**(b) — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Build edge weights (1 mark):
- $c_{12} = 1, \; c_{23} = 1, \; c_{34} = 1$; all other $c_{ij} = 0$.

Step 2 — Laplacian for movable cells 2 and 3 (2 marks):
$$A \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} c_{21}+c_{23} & -c_{23} \\ -c_{23} & c_{32}+c_{34} \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Step 3 — RHS from fixed terminals (1 mark):
$$\mathbf{b} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} c_{21}\, x_1 + c_{23}\, x_3 \\ c_{32}\, x_2 + c_{34}\, x_4 \end{pmatrix} \;\to\; \begin{pmatrix} 1\cdot 0 \\ 1\cdot 12 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 12 \end{pmatrix}$$

Step 4 — Solve (2 marks):
$$\begin{aligned} 2x_2 - x_3 &= 0 \\ -x_2 + 2x_3 &= 12 \end{aligned} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x_2 = 4,\; x_3 = 8$$

Step 5 — Verification (1 mark): Substituting back,
$\Phi_x = (0-4)^2 + (4-8)^2 + (8-12)^2 = 16 + 16 + 16 = 48$ µm².

#### Alternative Choice

### Question B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain Breuer's quadratic placement algorithm with a neat flowchart. State the role of pseudo-nets and the structure of the connectivity matrix. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the three main limitations of pure quadratic placement. Describe how the GORDIAN algorithm overcomes each limitation. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution Outline

**(a) — 7 marks**:
- Steps 0–6 of Breuer's algorithm as enumerated in Section 4.4. (3 marks)
- Role of pseudo-nets: rank-deficiency removal + boundary spreading. (2 marks)
- Connectivity matrix structure: $A_{ii} = \sum_j c_{ij}$, $A_{ij} = -c_{ij}$, symmetric PSD. (2 marks)

**(b) — 7 marks**:
- **Limitation 1 — Cell clustering at the centre.** GORDIAN fix: introduces a density (electric-potential-like) term that repels cells from overfull bins. (2 marks)
- **Limitation 2 — Overlap / density violation.** GORDIAN fix: uses a log-sum-exp density penalty and an inner-outer iteration. (2 marks)
- **Limitation 3 — No legalization awareness.** GORDIAN fix: density gradient is computed on the bin grid so the result is legalization-friendly. (2 marks)
- **Conclusion:** Combining a smooth quadratic term with a smooth density term yields a *smooth* objective that is still efficiently solvable. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on Quadratic Placement answers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of off-diagonals** in the Laplacian — they are **negative** ($-c_{ij}$), not positive. Examiner explicitly deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing the RHS incorrectly** — $b_i$ must include contributions only from **fixed terminals** (which are absorbed from the LHS). Including movable cells in $b$ is a fatal sign error.
> 3. **Omitting the pseudo-net discussion** in Breuer's algorithm — the singular-matrix problem is the *single most asked* follow-up.
> 4. **Confusing the clique weight** $2/k$ with $1/k$ or $1/\binom{k}{2}$. The correct weight $2/k$ preserves the total hyperedge contribution.
> 5. **Stating that quadratic placement is "iterative"** like simulated annealing. It is **analytical / closed-form** — the system is solved once (or, with GORDIAN, in an outer loop, but the inner problem is still a single linear solve).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Quadratic placement** = minimize $\sum c_{ij}[(x_i-x_j)^2+(y_i-y_j)^2]$ over all connected pairs.
- **Clique model**: $k$-pin net $\to \binom{k}{2}$ edges, each with weight $2/k$.
- **Separability**: $\Phi(x,y) = \Phi_x(x) + \Phi_y(y)$ — solve $x$ and $y$ independently.
- **Linear system**: $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ with $A_{ii}=\sum_j c_{ij}$, $A_{ij}=-c_{ij}$, and $b_i=\sum_{f \in F} c_{if}x_f$.
- **Hessian**: $\nabla^2 \Phi_x = 2A$ — guarantees **unique global minimum** (no local-minima problem).
- **Pseudo-nets** to virtual pads at chip corners are **mandatory** to remove the rank-deficiency and to spread cells.
- **Solvers**: Cholesky (direct) or Conjugate Gradient (iterative) on the sparse SPD system.
- **Three limitations**: centre clustering, overlap, no density awareness.
- **Three modern fixes**: GORDIAN (density term), Kraftwerk (density gradient), FastPlace (HPWL wrapper).
- **Examiner's one-liner**: *"Pure quadratic placement is closed-form and gives a smooth, unique solution, but ignores density — modern placers (GORDIAN, Kraftwerk) add a repulsion term to fix this."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floorplanning - Slicing Floorplan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Floorplanning - Slicing Floorplan # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Electronic Design Automation (EDA)*, **Floorplanning** is defined as the **early-stage physical design step** in which the dimensions and relative positions of major functional macro-blocks (such as IP cores, RAM, datapath units, control units, and I/O pads) are determined on a silicon die, *before* the detailed placement of standard cells is carried out. It bridges the gap between **logical synthesis** and **detailed placement & routing**. A **Slicing Floorplan** is a specific class of floorplan in which the overall rectangular chip region can be recursively subdivided into two non-overlapping rectangular sub-regions by a sequence of horizontal and vertical straight-line cuts, until each resulting rectangle corresponds to a single leaf macro-block. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Slicing Floorplan (Rigorous Definition):** > A floorplan $F$ is said to be *slicing* if and only if there exists a *recursive slicing structure* (a slicing tree) such that every internal cut is a straight line that is either horizontal ($\mathrm{H}$) or vertical ($\mathrm{V}$) and partitions the parent rectangle into exactly two child rectangles. **Not every floorplan is a slicing floorplan.** Non-slicing (or *mosaic*) floorplans, such as the famous *Five-Block Wheel* configuration, cannot be obtained by a sequence of straight-line recursive cuts. This distinction is a classic exam-favorite in KTU boards. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Pizza Box Analogy:** > Imagine a large rectangular pizza box. You keep cutting it in half with a straight knife: > - Cut it **horizontally** (left-to-right): you get a *top half* and a *bottom half*. > - Cut a piece **vertically** (top-to-bottom): you get a *left part* and a *right part*. > > You keep cutting each piece this way until every small rectangle corresponds to one slice of topping (your circuit block). > > **The sequence of cuts and the order in which they happen form a "Slicing Tree."** If you can always reach the final placement through only straight horizontal/vertical recursive cuts, the floorplan is *slicing*. If at some point you'd need an L-shaped knife (an L-cut), then it's *non-slicing*. ## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Macro-block** | A large pre-designed functional unit (e.g., ALU, memory array). | | **Slicing Tree** | A binary tree encoding the recursive cut structure. | | **Polish Expression** | A postfix (Reverse Polish) string equivalent of the slicing tree. | | **Horizontal Cut (H)** | Splits a rectangle into *Top* and *Bottom* parts. | | **Vertical Cut (V)** | Splits a rectangle into *Left* and *Right* parts. | | **Leaf Node** | Represents a single macro-block in the slicing tree. | | **Internal Node** | Represents an H/V cut operation. | | **Skewed Tree** | All H operators lie on one chain (either left or right). | | **Die / Chip** | The silicon substrate; final outer rectangle of the floorplan. | ## 1.4 Physical / Engineering Constants and Metrics - **Standard Cell Height Unit**: often measured in **$\mu$m** or **nm** in modern nodes. - **Aspect Ratio** of a block $i$: $$\text{AR}_i = \frac{h_i}{w_i}$$ - **Area of a Block**: $A_i = w_i \cdot h_i$ - **Total Die Area Goal**: minimize the bounding rectangle $W_{\text{chip}} \cdot H_{\text{chip}}$. - **Wirelength / Interconnect cost**: proxy measured as the **bounding-box perimeter** between blocks. > [!TIP] > KTU examiners expect you to know that *all* cuts in a slicing floorplan are **straight lines** that are either purely **horizontal** or purely **vertical** (never diagonal, never L-shaped). This is the single most-tested boundary. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Slicing Tree — Structure and Properties A **Slicing Tree** $T$ is a labeled, ordered binary tree such that: 1. **Leaf nodes** are labeled with macro-block names (e.g., $A, B, C, D, E, F, \ldots$). 2. **Internal nodes** are labeled with either: - $\mathrm{H}$ — a *horizontal cut* (rectangle split into top + bottom), **or** - $\mathrm{V}$ — a *vertical cut* (rectangle split into left + right). 3. The **root** represents the entire chip. 4. The tree is **ordered**: the left subtree corresponds to the *left* (or *bottom*) child of a vertical (or horizontal) cut. ### 2.1.1 Two Equivalent Representations Given any slicing tree, two equivalent representations exist: | Representation | Form | Example | |----------------|------|---------| | **Slicing Tree** | Rooted ordered binary tree | (Diagram in §4) | | **Polish Expression** | Postfix (Reverse Polish) string | `12V34H56VH` | > [!NOTE] > A **Polish Expression** is read **postfix** (operator after operands). Scanning it left to right builds the slicing tree: > - Push operands (block names / numbers) onto a stack. > - On operator ($\mathrm{H}$ or $\mathrm{V}$), pop two sub-trees, combine under the operator, push back. A Polish expression $E$ is **valid** if and only if: - It contains exactly $n$ operands and $(n-1)$ binary operators. - While scanning left-to-right, **the number of operands seen so far is always $\geq$ the number of operators seen so far**. - At the end, the count difference equals exactly 1. ## 2.2 Skewed Slicing Trees A slicing tree is called **skewed** if all internal nodes lie on a single root-to-leaf path (i.e., it is a *chain*). Two special cases: | Type | Definition | Count for $n$ blocks | |------|------------|----------------------| | **HV-skewed tree** | H's on one side, V's on the other | $2 \cdot (n-1)!$ | | **Skewed tree (any single-operator dominance)** | All operators are $\mathrm{H}$ OR all are $\mathrm{V}$ (with possible one "cross" operator) | Same as above | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why skewed trees matter in KTU:** The minimum-area floorplan problem is **NP-hard** for general slicing trees, but becomes **polynomial-time solvable** when restricted to **skewed slicing trees** — solved in $O(n)$ by the Stockmeyer algorithm (1973). This is a high-yield topic. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes | |---------|---------------------|---------------| | Total Blocks | $n$ leaf nodes | dimensionless | | Operators Required | $(n-1)$ internal nodes | dimensionless | | Aspect Ratio (block $i$) | $\mathrm{AR}_i = h_i / w_i$ | dimensionless | | Area of block $i$ | $A_i = w_i \cdot h_i$ | $\mu\text{m}^2$ or $\text{nm}^2$ | | Total Chip Area | $A_{\text{chip}} = W_{\text{chip}} \cdot H_{\text{chip}}$ | $\mu\text{m}^2$ | | Horizontal merge dimensions | $w = \max(w_1, w_2)$; $\; h = h_1 + h_2$ | side-by-side vertically stacked | | Vertical merge dimensions | $w = w_1 + w_2$; $\; h = \max(h_1, h_2)$ | side-by-side horizontally placed | | Cost function (area) | $\text{cost}(T) = W_T \cdot H_T$ | minimize | | Number of valid Polish expressions for $n$ blocks | bounded by the Catalan family | exponential in $n$ | | Number of skewed slicing trees for $n$ blocks | $2 \cdot (n-1)!$ | closed form | | Valid Polish scan invariant | $\#\text{operands} \;\geq\; \#\text{operators}$ at every prefix | postfix correctness | > [!WARNING] > When merging two rectangles under an **H operator**, the resulting width is the **maximum** of the two widths, and heights **add up**. Under a **V operator**, widths **add up** and height is the **maximum** of the two heights. Getting this orientation wrong is the #1 mark-loser in KTU boards. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Slicing Floorplans Slicing floorplans are the workhorse of: 1. **Datapath-intensive ASICs** — natural recursive bisection matches regular datapath structure. 2. **Memory + Logic SoCs** — memory arrays stack horizontally; logic on top, easily expressed by skewed trees. 3. **Early-stage die estimation** — slicing structures allow *rapid* area/delay trade-off analysis before committing to a placement. 4. **Hierarchical design** — slicing trees map naturally onto hierarchical design teams and IP integration. In modern commercial EDA tools (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2), slicing floorplans are used as a **starting template** before non-slicing legalization refines the layout. ## 2.5 Logical Steps to Construct a Slicing Floorplan 1. **Identify** all macro-blocks with their (width, height) tuples. 2. **Choose** a slicing tree topology (skewed for tractability). 3. **Encode** the tree as a Polish expression. 4. **Compute** dimensions of each sub-tree bottom-up using the merge rules. 5. **Optimize** by permuting operator positions (e.g., try H-vs-V at each internal node). 6. **Validate** the resulting floorplan by drawing the rectangles in cut order. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building a Floorplan from a Slicing Tree **Problem:** Given slicing tree in Polish expression: `A B V C D V H E F V H` Block dimensions: $A=(2,3)$, $B=(3,1)$, $C=(2,2)$, $D=(1,3)$, $E=(4,1)$, $F=(2,2)$. Find: (i) the final chip dimensions, (ii) the chip area. ### Step 1: Stack-based Polish Scan We scan the expression left to right, maintaining a stack of rectangles. | Step | Token | Stack (bottom → top) | Action | |------|-------|----------------------|--------| | 1 | A | $[(2,3)]$ | Push block A | | 2 | B | $[(2,3),(3,1)]$ | Push block B | | 3 | V | $[(5,3)]$ | Pop two, **vertical merge** → $w=2+3=5$, $h=\max(3,1)=3$ | | 4 | C | $[(5,3),(2,2)]$ | Push block C | | 5 | D | $[(5,3),(2,2),(1,3)]$ | Push block D | | 6 | V | $[(5,3),(3,3)]$ | Vertical merge C,D → $w=2+1=3$, $h=\max(2,3)=3$ | | 7 | H | $[(5,6)]$ | **Horizontal merge** → $w=\max(5,3)=5$, $h=3+3=6$ | | 8 | E | $[(5,6),(4,1)]$ | Push block E | | 9 | F | $[(5,6),(4,1),(2,2)]$ | Push block F | | 10 | V | $[(5,6),(6,2)]$ | Vertical merge E,F → $w=4+2=6$, $h=\max(1,2)=2$ | | 11 | H | $[(6,8)]$ | Horizontal merge → $w=\max(5,6)=6$, $h=6+2=8$ | ### Step 2: Final Chip Dimensions $$ W_{\text{chip}} = 6, \quad H_{\text{chip}} = 8 $$ ### Step 3: Total Chip Area $$ A_{\text{chip}} = W_{\text{chip}} \cdot H_{\text{chip}} = 6 \times 8 = 48 \;\text{sq. units} $$ **Verification — area conservation check:** $$ \sum A_i = (2 \cdot 3)+(3 \cdot 1)+(2 \cdot 2)+(1 \cdot 3)+(4 \cdot 1)+(2 \cdot 2) $$ $$ = 6 + 3 + 4 + 3 + 4 + 4 = 24 \;\text{sq. units} $$ Since the chip is sliced (no holes, no L-shapes), the chip area must be $\geq$ the sum of block areas. Indeed, $48 \geq 24$ — **white-space (dead area) is 24 sq. units**, which is the wasted rectangular margin. ✓ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Polish Validity Check **Problem:** Determine whether `ABVCVHD` is a valid slicing Polish expression for 4 blocks $A,B,C,D$. ### Step 1: Apply the Prefix Invariant We require that at every step, $\#\text{operands seen} \geq \#\text{operators seen}$. | Position | Token | #Operands | #Operators | Diff | Valid? | |----------|-------|-----------|------------|------|--------| | 1 | A | 1 | 0 | 1 | ✓ | | 2 | B | 2 | 0 | 2 | ✓ | | 3 | V | 1 | 1 | 0 | ✓ (exactly equal) | | 4 | C | 2 | 1 | 1 | ✓ | | 5 | V | 1 | 2 | -1 | ✗ **INVALID** | ### Step 2: Conclusion At position 5, the operator count (2) exceeds the operand count (1). Therefore, **the expression is NOT a valid Polish expression** for a slicing tree. > [!NOTE] > KTU exam tip: You do **not** need to draw the tree. The stack-depth invariant is the standard "trick" answer. ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Minimum Area Skewed Floorplan (Stockmeyer Idea) **Problem:** Three blocks with dimensions: $$ A = (2, 4), \quad B = (3, 1), \quad C = (1, 5) $$ Find the slicing floorplan (skewed) that minimizes chip area. ### Step 1: Enumerate All Skewed Trees for $n=3$ For $n=3$, we have $2 \cdot (3-1)! = 4$ skewed trees. The four skewed Polish expressions are: 1. `A B V C V` — both operators V (V-V skewed) 2. `A C V B V` — both operators V (permuted) 3. `A B H C H` — both operators H (H-H skewed) 4. `A C H B H` — both operators H (permuted) > Actually, full skewed count includes both single-operator and mixed forms. The classic *Stockmeyer* algorithm enumerates $2(n-1)!$ configurations total, including mixed H/V chains. ### Step 2: Compute Cost for Each **Case (a): `A B V C V`** — left subtree is `A B V` (vertical merge), right is `C`. - `A B V` → $w=2+3=5$, $h=\max(4,1)=4$ - Final `… C V` → $w=5+1=6$, $h=\max(4,5)=5$ - Chip = $(6, 5)$, Area $= 30$ **Case (b): `A C V B V`** — left subtree is `A C V`, right is `B`. - `A C V` → $w=2+1=3$, $h=\max(4,5)=5$ - Final `… B V` → $w=3+3=6$, $h=\max(5,1)=5$ - Chip = $(6, 5)$, Area $= 30$ **Case (c): `A B H C H`** - `A B H` → $w=\max(2,3)=3$, $h=4+1=5$ - Final `… C H` → $w=\max(3,1)=3$, $h=5+5=10$ - Chip = $(3, 10)$, Area $= 30$ **Case (d): `A C H B H`** - `A C H` → $w=\max(2,1)=2$, $h=4+5=9$ - Final `… B H` → $w=\max(2,3)=3$, $h=9+1=10$ - Chip = $(3, 10)$, Area $= 30$ ### Step 3: Observe and Conclude $$ \boxed{A_{\min} = 30 \;\text{sq. units}} $$ All four configurations yield the same area — but the **chip aspect ratios differ** (square vs. tall). For a square-die requirement, **Case (a) or (b)** would be chosen. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Takeaway:** Minimizing chip *area* alone often gives multiple ties. Industry tools add a *secondary objective* — typically aspect-ratio targeting (e.g., $W_{\text{chip}} \approx H_{\text{chip}}$) or wirelength cost. ## 3.4 General Bottom-Up Dimension Formula For any internal node $u$ with children $L$ and $R$ and operator $\mathrm{op}$: $$ \text{Dims}(u) = \begin{cases} \bigl(\max(w_L, w_R),\; h_L + h_R\bigr) & \text{if } \mathrm{op} = \mathrm{H} \\ \bigl(w_L + w_R,\; \max(h_L, h_R)\bigr) & \text{if } \mathrm{op} = \mathrm{V} \end{cases} $$ This recurrence is evaluated **bottom-up** in $O(n)$ time per slicing tree. To find the minimum area across all valid slicing trees, you would in the worst case enumerate all (exponential in $n$) trees — hence the restriction to **skewed** trees for polynomial time. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Slicing Tree for Polish `ABVCDVHEFVH` ```mermaid graph TD rootNode["H"]:::root leftSub["H"]:::hNode rightSub["H"]:::hNode abV["V"]:::vNode cdV["V"]:::vNode efV["V"]:::vNode leafA["A"]:::leaf leafB["B"]:::leaf leafC["C"]:::leaf leafD["D"]:::leaf leafE["E"]:::leaf leafF["F"]:::leaf rootNode --> leftSub rootNode --> rightSub leftSub --> abV leftSub --> cdV abV --> leafA abV --> leafB cdV --> leafC cdV --> leafD rightSub --> efV rightSub --> leafE efV --> leafF classDef root fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#ffd166,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff; classDef hNode fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff; classDef vNode fill:#e76f51,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff; classDef leaf fill:#f4a261,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:1px,color:#000; ``` **Reading the tree (bottom-up):** - `A B V` ⇒ $(w=5, h=3)$ — left-bottom rectangle. - `C D V` ⇒ $(w=3, h=3)$ — right-bottom rectangle. - `… H` ⇒ top + bottom merge ⇒ $(w=5, h=6)$. - `E F V` ⇒ $(w=6, h=2)$ — top rectangle. - `… H` ⇒ final merge ⇒ $(w=6, h=8)$ — **the chip**. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Floorplan Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR input1["Block A
2x3"]:::blk input2["Block B
3x1"]:::blk input3["Block C
2x2"]:::blk input4["Block D
1x3"]:::blk input5["Block E
4x1"]:::blk input6["Block F
2x2"]:::blk stage1["Stage 1
Vertical Merge Pairs"]:::stage stage2["Stage 2
Horizontal Merge"]:::stage stage3["Stage 3
Vertical Merge"]:::stage stage4["Stage 4
Horizontal Merge Root"]:::stage final["Final Chip
W=6, H=8"]:::final input1 --> stage1 input2 --> stage1 input3 --> stage1 input4 --> stage1 input5 --> stage3 input6 --> stage3 stage1 --> stage2 stage3 --> stage4 stage2 --> stage4 stage4 --> final classDef blk fill:#264653,stroke:#e9c46a,color:#fff; classDef stage fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#1d3557,color:#fff; classDef final fill:#e63946,stroke:#ffd166,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px; ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Floorplan Optimization ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["Start: Input blocks + dims"]):::start gen["Generate candidate slicing trees"]:::proc skew{"Skewed only?"}:::decision full["Enumerate all slicing trees
Exponential time"]:::proc skewed["Enumerate skewed trees
O(n) per tree, 2n-1 factorial total"]:::proc compute["Bottom-up dimension computation"]:::proc min["Track minimum chip area"]:::proc output(["Output: Best slicing tree + dims"]):::end start --> gen --> skew skew -- Yes --> skewed skew -- No --> full full --> compute skewed --> compute --> min --> output classDef start fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#073b4c,color:#000; classDef end fill:#ef476f,stroke:#073b4c,color:#fff; classDef decision fill:#ffd166,stroke:#073b4c,color:#000; classDef proc fill:#118ab2,stroke:#073b4c,color:#fff; ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Polish Expression Validity Checker ```mermaid flowchart LR expr["Polish Expression
String Input"]:::in scan["Linear Scan i=1 to L"]:::proc diff["Compute Diff = Operands - Operators"]:::proc chk{"Diff >= 0
at every step?"}:::dec finalChk{"Final Diff = 1?"}:::dec valid["VALID Slicing Expression"]:::ok invalid["INVALID Expression"]:::bad expr --> scan --> diff --> chk chk -- No --> invalid chk -- Yes --> finalChk finalChk -- Yes --> valid finalChk -- No --> invalid classDef in fill:#7209b7,stroke:#fff,color:#fff; classDef proc fill:#3a0ca3,stroke:#fff,color:#fff; classDef dec fill:#f72585,stroke:#fff,color:#fff; classDef ok fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,color:#000; classDef bad fill:#d62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff; ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** *Define a slicing floorplan. How is it different from a non-slicing floorplan?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **slicing floorplan** is one that can be recursively divided into two non-overlapping rectangular sub-regions by a sequence of horizontal and vertical straight-line cuts, each cut partitioning a parent rectangle exactly into two child rectangles, until each leaf rectangle corresponds to a single macro-block. > > A **non-slicing floorplan** cannot be obtained by such recursive straight-line cuts and typically requires L-shaped or more complex cuts. > > **Example:** A 2×2 grid of equal blocks is slicing (cut horizontally then vertically at each level). A *Five-Block Wheel* (one block in the center surrounded by four) is non-slicing. *(Allocation: 1 mark definition, 1 mark difference, 1 mark example.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** *What is a Polish expression? State the invariant condition for a string to be a valid slicing Polish expression.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **Polish expression** is a postfix (Reverse Polish) representation of a slicing tree, where macro-block names are operands and cut operations ($\mathrm{H}$ or $\mathrm{V}$) are binary operators applied to the two most recent operands. > > **Validity invariant:** While scanning the expression from left to right, at every prefix of the string, the number of operands must always be **greater than or equal to** the number of operators. After the full scan, the net difference must be exactly **1** (one more operand than operator), with exactly $n$ operands and $(n-1)$ operators for $n$ blocks. *(Allocation: 1 mark definition, 2 marks invariant condition.)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Given the Polish expression `ABVHCDVHEFVH` with block dimensions $A=(1,3), B=(4,2), C=(2,2), D=(1,4), E=(3,1), F=(2,3)$: 1. Construct the slicing tree. 2. Compute the chip dimensions. 3. Compute the chip area. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Polish Scan with Stack:** | Step | Token | Stack (top at right) | Computation | |------|-------|----------------------|-------------| | 1 | A | $(1,3)$ | Push | | 2 | B | $(1,3),(4,2)$ | Push | | 3 | V | $(5,3)$ | $w=1+4=5,\;h=\max(3,2)=3$ | | 4 | H | — | Apply on stack top — wait, re-examine | *Correcting the scan order:* Re-scanning `A B V H C D V H E F V H`: | Step | Token | Stack | Computation | |------|-------|-------|-------------| | 1 | A | $(1,3)$ | Push A | | 2 | B | $(1,3),(4,2)$ | Push B | | 3 | V | $(5,3)$ | $w=1+4,\;h=\max(3,2)$ | | 4 | H | $(5,5)$ | $w=\max(5, \text{empty}) = 5$; **H on single rect means stack underflow — expression is INVALID.** | > [!WARNING] > The expression `ABVH...` is **INVALID** because an H operator appears when the stack contains only one rectangle. A valid H or V requires **two operands** on the stack. **Corrected problem statement:** Let us use `A B V C D V H E F V H`: | Step | Token | Stack | Computation | |------|-------|-------|-------------| | 1 | A | $(1,3)$ | Push | | 2 | B | $(1,3),(4,2)$ | Push | | 3 | V | $(5,3)$ | $w=1+4=5,\;h=\max(3,2)=3$ | | 4 | C | $(5,3),(2,2)$ | Push | | 5 | D | $(5,3),(2,2),(1,4)$ | Push | | 6 | V | $(5,3),(3,4)$ | $w=2+1=3,\;h=\max(2,4)=4$ | | 7 | H | $(5,7)$ | $w=\max(5,3)=5,\;h=3+4=7$ | | 8 | E | $(5,7),(3,1)$ | Push | | 9 | F | $(5,7),(3,1),(2,3)$ | Push | | 10 | V | $(5,7),(5,3)$ | $w=3+2=5,\;h=\max(1,3)=3$ | | 11 | H | $(6,10)$ | $w=\max(5,5)=5 \to$ wait, recompute | **Re-evaluating Step 11 carefully:** - Top of stack after step 10: two rectangles — $(5,7)$ and $(5,3)$. - H merge: $w = \max(5, 5) = 5$, $h = 7 + 3 = 10$. - **Final chip: $(W, H) = (5, 10)$**. **Step 2 — Chip dimensions:** $$ \boxed{W_{\text{chip}} = 5, \quad H_{\text{chip}} = 10} $$ **Step 3 — Chip area:** $$ \boxed{A_{\text{chip}} = 5 \times 10 = 50 \;\text{sq. units}} $$ **Area conservation check:** $$ \sum A_i = 1\!\cdot\!3 + 4\!\cdot\!2 + 2\!\cdot\!2 + 1\!\cdot\!4 + 3\!\cdot\!1 + 2\!\cdot\!3 = 3+8+4+4+3+6 = 28 $$ White space $= 50 - 28 = 22$ sq. units. ✓ --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** For the floorplan in part (a), describe the structure of the slicing tree and verify whether the floorplan is *skewed*. Justify. **Model Solution:** **Tree Structure:** - Root: H (cuts the chip into top + bottom). - Left child (bottom): H again. - Left-left: V merging A and B. - Left-right: V merging C and D. - Right child (top): V merging E and F. **Skewedness check:** A skewed slicing tree has all internal nodes on a single chain (root-to-leaf). In our tree, the root H has two children that are both non-leaf internal nodes (H on left, V on right). Hence **this tree is NOT skewed**. **Justification:** The tree has two distinct root-to-leaf paths of length 2: - Root → Left-H → Left-left-V → leaf A - Root → Right-V → leaf E Since the depth is > 1 in multiple branches, it is not a chain → not skewed. **However**, the tree is *HV-skewed* in a weak sense — it is still a *valid* slicing tree, just not amenable to the linear-time Stockmeyer optimization. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Stockmeyer algorithm for minimum-area slicing floorplan. Why is it restricted to skewed slicing trees? What is its time complexity? **Model Solution:** The **Stockmeyer algorithm** (1983) finds the minimum-area slicing floorplan in polynomial time by: 1. **Restricting** the search space to *skewed* slicing trees. 2. For each of the $2 \cdot (n-1)!$ skewed topologies, computing the chip dimensions in $O(n)$ via bottom-up DP. 3. Returning the configuration with minimum chip area (and tie-breaking on aspect ratio, if needed). **Why restrict to skewed trees?** - The total number of general slicing trees for $n$ blocks is *exponential* in $n$ (related to Catalan numbers). - Skewed trees have a **closed-form count** $2 \cdot (n-1)!$, allowing enumeration in factorial time. - For $n \leq 10$ or so, this is acceptable in practice; for larger $n$, even skewed enumeration becomes heavy, but it remains *polynomial* in $n$ **per tree** ($O(n)$). **Time complexity:** - Number of trees: $O((n-1)!)$. - Cost per tree: $O(n)$. - **Total: $O(n \cdot (n-1)!)$**, which is polynomial per configuration but super-polynomial overall; for *fixed* $n$ it is fast. **Key DP recurrence for area:** $$ \text{area}_{\min}(u) = \min_{\text{op} \in \{\mathrm{H},\mathrm{V}\}} \begin{cases} \max(w_L, w_R) \cdot (h_L + h_R) & \text{if op = H} \\ (w_L + w_R) \cdot \max(h_L, h_R) & \text{if op = V} \end{cases} $$ **Industry extension:** Modern tools add a **simulated-annealing** layer on top of Stockmeyer to escape the skewed restriction. *(Allocation: 3 marks algorithm steps, 2 marks restriction rationale, 2 marks complexity.)* --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider four blocks $A=(2,5), B=(3,2), C=(4,3), D=(1,4)$. Compare the chip areas of the following two slicing trees and determine which is better: - **Tree 1 (Polish):** `A B H C D H V` - **Tree 2 (Polish):** `A C V B D V H` **Model Solution:** **Tree 1: `A B H C D H V`** | Step | Token | Stack | Computation | |------|-------|-------|-------------| | 1 | A | $(2,5)$ | Push | | 2 | B | $(2,5),(3,2)$ | Push | | 3 | H | $(3,7)$ | $w=\max(2,3)=3,\;h=5+2=7$ | | 4 | C | $(3,7),(4,3)$ | Push | | 5 | D | $(3,7),(4,3),(1,4)$ | Push | | 6 | H | $(3,7),(4,7)$ | $w=\max(4,1)=4,\;h=3+4=7$ | | 7 | V | $(7,7)$ | $w=3+4=7,\;h=\max(7,7)=7$ | **Chip 1: $(W, H) = (7, 7)$, Area $= 49$ sq. units.** **Tree 2: `A C V B D V H`** | Step | Token | Stack | Computation | |------|-------|-------|-------------| | 1 | A | $(2,5)$ | Push | | 2 | C | $(2,5),(4,3)$ | Push | | 3 | V | $(6,5)$ | $w=2+4=6,\;h=\max(5,3)=5$ | | 4 | B | $(6,5),(3,2)$ | Push | | 5 | D | $(6,5),(3,2),(1,4)$ | Push | | 6 | V | $(6,5),(4,4)$ | $w=3+1=4,\;h=\max(2,4)=4$ | | 7 | H | $(6,9)$ | $w=\max(6,4)=6,\;h=5+4=9$ | **Chip 2: $(W, H) = (6, 9)$, Area $= 54$ sq. units.** **Comparison Table:** | Metric | Tree 1 | Tree 2 | |--------|--------|--------| | Chip Width | 7 | 6 | | Chip Height | 7 | 9 | | Chip Area | **49** | 54 | | Aspect Ratio | 1.0 (square) | 0.67 (tall) | **Conclusion:** **Tree 1 is better** because it has a smaller chip area (49 < 54) and also yields a perfectly square die (aspect ratio 1), which is desirable for packaging and yield. *(Allocation: 4 marks Tree 1 computation, 4 marks Tree 2 computation, 2 marks comparison and conclusion — distributed as 7 marks total.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing H and V merge rules** — H: $w = \max$, $h = \text{sum}$; V: $w = \text{sum}$, $h = \max$. Reversing them gives wrong chip dimensions. *(−2 marks typical)* > 2. **Forgetting to apply max at each merge** — Students sometimes add widths under H or heights under V. *(−1 to −2 marks)* > 3. **Skipping stack drawing in Polish evaluation** — Examiners reward *clear stack traces*. Always show the stack state after each token. *(−1 mark for unclear work)* > 4. **Ignoring the validity invariant** — Computing dimensions for an *invalid* Polish expression loses method marks. *(−2 marks)* > 5. **Not checking area conservation** — White-space (chip area − sum of block areas) must be $\geq 0$. Use it as a *sanity check*. > 6. **Confusing "slicing floorplan" with "slicing tree"** — the tree is the *representation*, the floorplan is the *geometric realization*. > 7. **Forgetting units** in chip dimension statements — write "in $\mu$m" or "in standard cell units". --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-page cheat recap for the night before the exam:** - [x] **Floorplanning** comes *after* partitioning and *before* placement in the VLSI design flow. - [x] A **slicing floorplan** is recursively bisectable by **horizontal (H)** and **vertical (V)** straight-line cuts. - [x] A **slicing tree** is a labeled ordered binary tree; leaves are blocks, internal nodes are H or V. - [x] A **Polish expression** is the postfix (Reverse Polish) form of the slicing tree. - [x] **Validity invariant:** in any prefix of a valid Polish expression, $\#\text{operands} \geq \#\text{operators}$, and at end, difference is exactly 1. - [x] **H merge:** $w = \max(w_L, w_R)$, $h = h_L + h_R$ (rectangles stacked *vertically*, side-by-side on top of each other). - [x] **V merge:** $w = w_L + w_R$, $h = \max(h_L, h_R)$ (rectangles placed *horizontally*, side-by-side). - [x] **Skewed slicing tree:** all internal nodes lie on a single root-to-leaf chain (a path, not a branching tree). - [x] **Count of skewed slicing trees** for $n$ blocks: $2 \cdot (n-1)!$. - [x] **Stockmeyer algorithm** solves minimum-area floorplanning on skewed slicing trees in $O(n)$ per configuration. - [x] **Area conservation:** $\sum A_i \leq A_{\text{chip}}$; the difference is *white space* (dead area, unused silicon). - [x] **Aspect ratio** is $h / w$; targeting $AR \approx 1$ is a common secondary objective. - [x] **Non-slicing floorplans** exist (e.g., wheel topology) — they are *NP-hard* to optimize and require annealing-based methods. - [x] Real EDA tools (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2) use slicing structures as **initial templates**, then legalize with non-slicing optimizations. - [x] Always present your Polish-evaluation work as a **stack table** — examiners award method marks generously for it. --- > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic — "H means Heights, V means Verticals":** > - **H**orizontal cut → rectangles stacked **v**ertically (top/bottom) → **h**eights **a**dd up. > - **V**ertical cut → rectangles placed **h**orizontally (left/right) → **w**idths **a**dd up. > > Or, more simply: under **H**, the rectangle gets *taller* (heights add); under **V**, the rectangle gets *wider* (widths add). *End of Module 3 — Floorplanning: Slicing Floorplan Notes.*

topics.title: Non-Slicing Floorplan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET741 - ELECTRONIC DESIGN AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Non-Slicing Floorplan ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Non-Slicing Floorplan** is a rectangle-packing arrangement of circuit modules (or "blocks") on a chip such that the resulting topology **cannot be obtained by recursively bisecting the chip with straight horizontal or vertical cut lines passing completely through the layout**. In graph-theoretic terms, a slicing floorplan corresponds to a binary tree of recursive cuts, whereas a non-slicing floorplan has no such hierarchical cut-line decomposition. Mathematically, a floorplan is *non-slicing* if and only if there is **no straight line segment drawn horizontally or vertically that traverses the chip from one boundary to the other without crossing any block**. This class strictly subsumes slicing floorplans, meaning **every slicing floorplan is also a valid non-slicing floorplan representation**, but the converse is not true. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Non-slicing floorplans are essential for modern Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) because they allow a wider design space, support arbitrary (curved/L-shaped/T-shaped) modules, and yield superior area utilization compared to slicing floorplans. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine packing luggage into the boot of a car: - A **slicing floorplan** is like organizing the boot with a single, long horizontal divider (top half / bottom half) and then dividing each half vertically, and so on. The cuts are always full-length. - A **non-slicing floorplan** is like Tetris — you slide odd-shaped pieces (T-shapes, L-shapes, rectangles) into the available space such that no single line cuts the whole boot in half. This is **harder**, but uses the space **more efficiently** because you are not forced to make "guillotine cuts." **Another Analogy — Jigsaw Puzzle:** A non-slicing floorplan is like a jigsaw puzzle where each module interlocks with its neighbors in a way that no cut-line cleanly separates one region from the rest. Only by "cutting through the middle of a piece" can you isolate a region. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following bold-faced metrics are universally used in non-slicing floorplan evaluation: - **Cost Function (Φ):** Weighted sum of area and wirelength. - **Total Area (A):** The bounding rectangle's area. - **Total Wirelength (W):** Sum of Manhattan distances between connected module pins. - **Aspect Ratio (r_i):** Height-to-width ratio of module *i*. - **Aspect Ratio Bound:** r_i ∈ [r_min, r_max], typically **[0.5, 2.0]**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Aspect Ratio Bound:** The allowable range within which a flexible (soft) module may be stretched or compressed. KTU problems usually give the range explicitly. ### 1.4 Why Non-Slicing Floorplans Matter | Property | Slicing | Non-Slicing | |---|---|---| | Search space | Small | Very large | | Solution quality | Sub-optimal | Near-optimal | | Handles arbitrary shapes | No | Yes (via CBL, B\*-Tree) | | Representation | Polish notation, Slicing tree | Sequence pair, BSG, O-Tree, B\*-Tree, CBL | | Algorithm complexity | Polynomial | NP-hard | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Concept: Why Non-Slicing? A non-slicing floorplan arises when two or more modules "wrap around" each other such that a full vertical or horizontal cut line cannot pass through the chip. Such configurations are **provably impossible to represent as a slicing tree**, and therefore require entirely different data structures. ### 2.2 The Major Non-Slicing Representations The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3) emphasizes the following representations, each with a different "encoding trick" for the same geometric problem: #### A. Sequence Pair (SP) — Murata et al., 1996 A **Sequence Pair** is a pair of permutations (Γ⁺, Γ⁻) of the *n* module names. The relative order in Γ⁺ defines horizontal relationships; the relative order in Γ⁻ defines vertical relationships. - If module *a* comes before *b* in **Γ⁺**, then *a* is **to the left** of *b*. - If module *a* comes before *b* in **Γ⁻**, then *a* is **below** *b*. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A single (Γ⁺, Γ⁻) pair encodes an entire non-slicing topology. The decoding requires two Constraint Graphs (HCG and VCG) and a longest-path computation. #### B. Bounded Slicing Grid (BSG) — Nakatake et al., 1996 A **BSG** is a grid of horizontal and vertical line segments where each grid line is either "present" or "absent" (binary). A valid floorplan is obtained by placing modules at the intersection of present lines. #### C. O-Tree (Ordered Tree) — Guo et al., 1999 An **O-Tree** is a rooted, ordered tree where each node represents a module. The tree structure directly encodes the non-slicing topology in a compact form (uses only 2n bits for n modules). #### D. B\*-Tree — Chang et al., 2000 A **B\*-Tree** is a binary tree where the left child of a node represents the module placed to its right (horizontally), and the right child represents the module placed above it (vertically). It guarantees **no overlap** by construction. #### E. Corner Block List (CBL) — Hong et al., 2000 A **Corner Block List** is a sequence of T-junction records (corner type, rotation) that uniquely describes a mosaic floorplan. The "corner" concept captures the wrap-around topology of non-slicing structures. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every formula a KTU 2024 student needs for the ESE on this topic. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total Floorplan Area | $A = W_{chip} \times H_{chip}$ | A in μm², W,H in μm | Bounding rectangle | | 2 | Dead Space (Wasted Area) | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{total} - \sum A_i}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$ | η in % | Lower is better | | 3 | Wirelength (HPWL) | $W = \sum_{(u,v) \in Nets} \left( \vert x_u - x_v \vert + \vert y_u - y_v \vert \right)$ | W in μm | Half-Perimeter Wire Length | | 4 | Cost Function | $\Phi = \alpha \cdot A + (1 - \alpha) \cdot W$ | α ∈ [0,1] | Weighted sum | | 5 | Simulated Annealing | $P_{accept} = e^{-\Delta\Phi / T}$ | T = temperature | Metropolis criterion | | 6 | Cooling Schedule | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, α ≈ 0.95 | T in arbitrary units | Geometric cooling | | 7 | SP Search Space Size | $(n!)^2$ | n = # modules | Permutations of n items | | 8 | B\*-Tree Size | $2n - 1$ | n = # modules | Nodes in binary tree | | 9 | CBL Size | $3n$ records | n = # modules | 1 corner + 2 T-junctions / module | | 10 | Aspect Ratio | $r_i = h_i / w_i$ | dimensionless | $r_i \in [r_{min}, r_{max}]$ | | 11 | Soft Module Reshape | $A_i = w_i \cdot h_i = \text{const}$ | A in μm² | Area preserved | | 12 | Constraint Graph Edges | $(n^2 - n)/2$ | n = # modules | Directed edges | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Non-slicing floorplan algorithms are embedded inside commercial EDA tools such as **Cadence Innovus**, **Synopsys ICC2**, and **Mentor Olympus**. They determine the **macro placement** of CPU cores, memory blocks, and IP modules on a System-on-Chip (SoC). The lower the dead-space and wirelength, the **smaller the die size**, the **lower the cost per chip**, and the **shorter the critical path delay**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Decoding a Sequence Pair: Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure Given a **Sequence Pair (Γ⁺, Γ⁻)** and module dimensions $(w_i, h_i)$, the algorithm below derives the (x, y) coordinate of every module. This is the **most-tested derivation** in KTU ESE for this topic. > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup (Sample):** Three modules A, B, C with dimensions: > A = (2, 3), B = (4, 2), C = (3, 4). > Given Sequence Pair: Γ⁺ = (A, B, C), Γ⁻ = (C, B, A). **Step 1 — Build the Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG).** The HCG is a directed graph where the *i*-th node in Γ⁺ gets a horizontal edge to the next module that appears to its right. For our pair, HCG is A → B → C. **Step 2 — Build the Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG).** The VCG is similarly built from Γ⁻, where a module gets a vertical edge to the next module appearing *above* it. From Γ⁻ = (C, B, A), VCG is C → B → A. **Step 3 — Define the Longest-Path Equations.** The x-coordinate of module *i* equals: $$ x_i = w_{prev(i)} + x_{prev(i)} $$ where *prev(i)* is the predecessor of module *i* in the HCG. The y-coordinate is similarly obtained from the VCG. **Step 4 — Solve the HCG Longest Path.** For HCG A → B → C, with x_A = 0 (assume leftmost): $$ \begin{aligned} x_A &= 0 \\ x_B &= x_A + w_A = 0 + 2 = 2 \\ x_C &= x_B + w_B = 2 + 4 = 6 \end{aligned} $$ So: x_A = 0, x_B = 2, x_C = 6. **Step 5 — Solve the VCG Longest Path.** For VCG C → B → A, with y_C = 0 (assume bottommost): $$ \begin{aligned} y_C &= 0 \\ y_B &= y_C + h_C = 0 + 4 = 4 \\ y_A &= y_B + h_B = 4 + 2 = 6 \end{aligned} $$ So: y_C = 0, y_B = 4, y_A = 6. **Step 6 — Compute Final Coordinates and Bounding Box.** | Module | x | y | w | h | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | 0 | 6 | 2 | 3 | | B | 2 | 4 | 4 | 2 | | C | 6 | 0 | 3 | 4 | Bounding box: $$ W_{chip} = x_C + w_C = 6 + 3 = 9 $$ $$ H_{chip} = y_A + h_A = 6 + 3 = 9 $$ $$ A_{total} = 9 \times 9 = 81 \text{ μm}^2 $$ **Step 7 — Compute Dead Space.** $$ \sum A_i = (2 \cdot 3) + (4 \cdot 2) + (3 \cdot 4) = 6 + 8 + 12 = 26 $$ $$ \eta = \frac{81 - 26}{81} \times 100\% \approx 67.9\% $$ > [!NOTE] > The high dead-space here is a function of the chosen SP, not the algorithm. Simulated Annealing perturbs (Γ⁺, Γ⁻) to search for a better pair. ### 3.2 Simulated Annealing (SA) Cost Convergence — Detailed Walk-Through The SA algorithm for non-slicing floorplanning follows this exact operational flow: **Step 1 — Initialization.** Generate an initial solution S₀. Set initial temperature T₀ (e.g., T₀ = 1000). Set cooling constant α = 0.95. Set equilibrium iterations M = 5n for n modules. **Step 2 — Perturbation (Move).** Apply one of three moves to S₀: - **Swap:** Exchange two modules in Γ⁺. - **Reverse:** Reverse a sub-segment of Γ⁺. - **Mirror:** Swap the two sequences (Γ⁺, Γ⁻) → (Γ⁻, Γ⁺). **Step 3 — Cost Evaluation.** $$ \Delta\Phi = \Phi(S_{new}) - \Phi(S_{old}) $$ **Step 4 — Acceptance Criterion (Metropolis).** $$ P_{accept} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \Delta\Phi \le 0 \\ e^{-\Delta\Phi / T}, & \text{if } \Delta\Phi > 0 \end{cases} $$ **Step 5 — Cooling.** $$ T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k $$ **Step 6 — Termination.** Stop when T < T_min (e.g., T_min = 0.1) or no improvement in K iterations. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Non-Slicing Representations | Representation | Inventor | Year | Solution Space | Decoding Time | Rotation | Wirelength | KTU Weightage | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Sequence Pair | Murata et al. | 1996 | $(n!)^2$ | $O(n^2)$ | Yes | Best | High | | BSG | Nakatake et al. | 1996 | $n^{2n}$ | $O(n^2)$ | Yes | Moderate | Medium | | O-Tree | Guo et al. | 1999 | $O(n! \cdot 2^{2n-2}/n^{1.5})$ | $O(n)$ | Yes | Good | Medium | | B\*-Tree | Chang et al. | 2000 | $O(n! \cdot 2^{2n-2}/n^{1.5})$ | $O(n)$ | Yes | Best | High | | CBL | Hong et al. | 2000 | $O(n! \cdot 2^{2n-2}/n^{1.5})$ | $O(n)$ | Yes | Moderate | High | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sequence Pair Decoder ```mermaid flowchart TD inputSP["Input Sequence Pair
GammaPlus and GammaMinus"] --> buildHCG["Build Horizontal Constraint Graph
HCG from GammaPlus"] inputSP --> buildVCG["Build Vertical Constraint Graph
VCG from GammaMinus"] buildHCG["Build Horizontal Constraint Graph
HCG from GammaPlus"] buildVCG["Build Vertical Constraint Graph
VCG from GammaMinus"] buildHCG["Build Horizontal Constraint Graph
HCG from GammaPlus"] buildVCG["Build Vertical Constraint Graph
VCG from GammaMinus"] buildHCG --> longPathX["Longest Path on HCG
Compute x coordinates"] buildVCG --> longPathY["Longest Path on VCG
Compute y coordinates"] longPathX["Longest Path on HCG
Compute x coordinates"] longPathY["Longest Path on VCG
Compute y coordinates"] longPathX["Longest Path on HCG
Compute x coordinates"] longPathY["Longest Path on VCG
Compute y coordinates"] longPathX --> mergeXY["Merge X and Y into Module Positions"] longPathY --> mergeXY["Merge X and Y into Module Positions"] longPathX["Longest Path on HCG
Compute x coordinates"] longPathY["Longest Path on VCG
Compute y coordinates"] mergeXY --> bounding["Compute Bounding Box
Width and Height"] bounding --> metrics["Output Area, Dead Space, Wirelength"] bounding["Compute Bounding Box
Width and Height"] metrics["Output Area, Dead Space, Wirelength"] bounding["Compute Bounding Box
Width and Height"] metrics["Output Area, Dead Space, Wirelength"] metrics --> outputFloorplan["Final Floorplan
with Module Coordinates"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Start at the **Input Sequence Pair** node. Two parallel branches build the HCG and VCG. Each branch performs a longest-path computation. The two paths merge to produce (x, y) coordinates, from which area, dead space, and wirelength are computed. ### 4.2 Non-Slicing vs. Slicing Topology — Conceptual Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Slicing["SLICING FLOORPLAN - guillotine cuts only"] A1["Chip"] --> A2["Top half"] A1["Chip"] --> A3["Bottom half"] A2["Top half"] --> A4["Block X"] A2["Top half"] --> A5["Block Y"] A3["Bottom half"] --> A6["Block Z"] end subgraph NonSlicing["NON-SLICING FLOORPLAN - T-junctions present"] B1["Chip"] --> B2["Block P top left"] B1["Chip"] --> B3["Block Q top right"] B1["Chip"] --> B4["Block R bottom"] B4["Block R bottom"] --> B5["Block S inside R"] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** In the **non-slicing** topology, Block S is nested inside Block R. No straight line can cut the chip without passing through Block R. This is the geometric signature of a non-slicing structure, and Corner Block List (CBL) is specifically designed to encode such nesting. ### 4.3 Simulated Annealing Flow for Non-Slicing Floorplan ```mermaid flowchart TD start0["Start: Initial Solution S0
High Temperature T0"] --> perturb0["Perturb Sequence Pair
Swap or Reverse or Mirror"] start0["Start: Initial Solution S0
High Temperature T0"] perturb0["Perturb Sequence Pair
Swap or Reverse or Mirror"] start0["Start: Initial Solution S0
High Temperature T0"] perturb0["Perturb Sequence Pair
Swap or Reverse or Mirror"] perturb0 --> decode0["Decode SP to Floorplan
HCG plus VCG"] perturb0["Perturb Sequence Pair
Swap or Reverse or Mirror"] decode0["Decode SP to Floorplan
HCG plus VCG"] perturb0["Perturb Sequence Pair
Swap or Reverse or Mirror"] decode0 --> costEval["Evaluate Cost Function
Phi equals alpha times Area plus one minus alpha times Wirelength"] decode0["Decode SP to Floorplan
HCG plus VCG"] costEval["Evaluate Cost Function
Phi equals alpha times Area plus one minus alpha times Wirelength"] decode0["Decode SP to Floorplan
HCG plus VCG"] costEval --> deltaCalc["Compute delta Phi
Phi new minus Phi old"] costEval["Evaluate Cost Function
Phi equals alpha times Area plus one minus alpha times Wirelength"] deltaCalc["Compute delta Phi
Phi new minus Phi old"] costEval["Evaluate Cost Function
Phi equals alpha times Area plus one minus alpha times Wirelength"] deltaCalc --> acceptTest["Acceptance Test
Metropolis Criterion"] deltaCalc["Compute delta Phi
Phi new minus Phi old"] acceptTest["Acceptance Test
Metropolis Criterion"] deltaCalc["Compute delta Phi
Phi new minus Phi old"] acceptTest --> cool["Cool Temperature
T new equals alpha times T old"] acceptTest["Acceptance Test
Metropolis Criterion"] cool["Cool Temperature
T new equals alpha times T old"] acceptTest["Acceptance Test
Metropolis Criterion"] cool --> stopCheck["T less than T min or Frozen?"] cool["Cool Temperature
T new equals alpha times T old"] stopCheck["T less than T min or Frozen?"] cool["Cool Temperature
T new equals alpha times T old"] stopCheck --> answerNo["No"] stopCheck["T less than T min or Frozen?"] answerNo["No"] --> perturb0 stopCheck["T less than T min or Frozen?"] stopCheck --> answerYes["Yes"] answerYes["Yes"] --> finalOutput["Output Best Floorplan
Minimum Phi"] finalOutput["Output Best Floorplan
Minimum Phi"] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Five Non-Slicing Representations ```mermaid flowchart LR rep["Non-Slicing Floorplan Representations"] --> spNode["Sequence Pair SP"] rep["Non-Slicing Floorplan Representations"] --> bsgNode["Bounded Slicing Grid BSG"] rep["Non-Slicing Floorplan Representations"] --> otreeNode["O-Tree"] rep["Non-Slicing Floorplan Representations"] --> btreeNode["B-Tree"] rep["Non-Slicing Floorplan Representations"] --> cblNode["Corner Block List CBL"] spNode["Sequence Pair SP"] --> spDec["Decoded by HCG plus VCG
Longest Path"] bsgNode["Bounded Slicing Grid BSG"] --> bsgDec["Decoded by Grid Sweep"] otreeNode["O-Tree"] --> otreeDec["Decoded by DFS Traversal"] btreeNode["B-Tree"] --> btreeDec["Decoded by Contour Aware Packing"] cblNode["Corner Block List CBL"] --> cblDec["Decoded by T Junction Records"] spDec["Decoded by HCG plus VCG
Longest Path"] --> costFn["Same Cost Function
Phi"] bsgDec["Decoded by Grid Sweep"] --> costFn otreeDec["Decoded by DFS Traversal"] --> costFn btreeDec["Decoded by Contour Aware Packing"] --> costFn cblDec["Decoded by T Junction Records"] --> costFn costFn["Same Cost Function
Phi"] --> saNode["Optimized by Simulated Annealing"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a non-slicing floorplan. How is it different from a slicing floorplan? Give one example representation. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **non-slicing floorplan** is a rectangle-packing of modules in which no straight horizontal or vertical line passes completely through the chip from one edge to the other without cutting any module. - **Slicing floorplan:** Can be recursively bisected by full-length cut lines; represented by a slicing tree or Polish notation. - **Non-slicing floorplan:** Cut lines do not exist; modules interlock (T-junctions or nesting); represented by Sequence Pair, BSG, O-Tree, B\*-Tree, or CBL. **Example:** A floorplan where module S is placed inside the L-shaped space of module R is non-slicing because no full-length cut can isolate S from R. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What is a Sequence Pair? Explain how the pair (Γ⁺, Γ⁻) encodes the relative positions of modules. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Sequence Pair (SP)** is an ordered pair of permutations (Γ⁺, Γ⁻) of *n* module labels. It is a compact, unambiguous encoding of a non-slicing floorplan. - If module *a* appears before module *b* in **Γ⁺**, then *a* is positioned to the **left** of *b*. - If module *a* appears before module *b* in **Γ⁻**, then *a* is positioned **below** *b*. These two orderings together uniquely determine the constraint graph and thus the final (x, y) positions of all modules. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** Explain the Sequence Pair representation for non-slicing floorplans. How is the Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG) and Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG) constructed from a given Sequence Pair? **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Given three modules A, B, C with dimensions A = (3, 2), B = (2, 4), C = (4, 3) and Sequence Pair Γ⁺ = (B, A, C), Γ⁻ = (A, C, B), determine the placement of all modules, the chip dimensions, the total area, and the percentage dead space. --- ### Model Answer to Q3 **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Sequence Pair Construction:** A Sequence Pair consists of two permutations of module names, denoted Γ⁺ and Γ⁻. - **Γ⁺** governs **horizontal** (left-right) relationships. - **Γ⁻** governs **vertical** (bottom-top) relationships. **Building the HCG:** 1. List modules in the order of Γ⁺. 2. For every pair of modules *a*, *b* in Γ⁺, if *a* precedes *b*, add a directed edge *a → b* in the HCG. 3. The HCG defines the x-coordinate of each module as the longest-path length from the source. **Building the VCG:** 1. List modules in the order of Γ⁻. 2. For every pair of modules *a*, *b* in Γ⁻, if *a* precedes *b*, add a directed edge *a → b* in the VCG. 3. The VCG defines the y-coordinate of each module as the longest-path length from the source. The longest path is computed by dynamic programming in **O(n²)** time, where *n* is the number of modules. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Construct HCG from Γ⁺ = (B, A, C):** B → A → C, giving edges: B→A, B→C, A→C. **Step 2 — Construct VCG from Γ⁻ = (A, C, B):** A → C → B, giving edges: A→C, A→B, C→B. **Step 3 — Compute x-coordinates (longest path on HCG):** Let x_B = 0 (B is the leftmost). $$ \begin{aligned} x_B &= 0 \\ x_A &= x_B + w_B = 0 + 2 = 2 \\ x_C &= \max(x_B + w_B, \; x_A + w_A) = \max(0+2, \; 2+3) = \max(2, 5) = 5 \end{aligned} $$ So x_B = 0, x_A = 2, x_C = 5. **Step 4 — Compute y-coordinates (longest path on VCG):** Let y_A = 0 (A is the bottommost). $$ \begin{aligned} y_A &= 0 \\ y_C &= y_A + h_A = 0 + 2 = 2 \\ y_B &= \max(y_A + h_A, \; y_C + h_C) = \max(0+2, \; 2+3) = \max(2, 5) = 5 \end{aligned} $$ So y_A = 0, y_C = 2, y_B = 5. **Step 5 — Tabulate Final Coordinates:** | Module | x | y | w | h | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | 2 | 0 | 3 | 2 | | B | 0 | 5 | 2 | 4 | | C | 5 | 2 | 4 | 3 | **Step 6 — Compute Chip Dimensions:** $$ W_{chip} = \max(x_i + w_i) = \max(5, \; 2, \; 9) = 9 \text{ μm} $$ $$ H_{chip} = \max(y_i + h_i) = \max(2, \; 9, \; 5) = 9 \text{ μm} $$ **Step 7 — Compute Area and Dead Space:** $$ A_{total} = 9 \times 9 = 81 \text{ μm}^2 $$ $$ \sum A_i = (3 \times 2) + (2 \times 4) + (4 \times 3) = 6 + 8 + 12 = 26 \text{ μm}^2 $$ $$ \eta = \frac{81 - 26}{81} \times 100\% = \frac{55}{81} \times 100\% \approx 67.90\% $$ --- **Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Internal Choice Alternative]** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** Describe the Bounded Slicing Grid (BSG) representation for non-slicing floorplans. How does the grid structure encode a valid placement, and what is the size of the search space? **Part (b) — 7 marks:** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the Simulated Annealing (SA) algorithm as applied to non-slicing floorplan optimization. Mention the role of the cooling schedule and the Metropolis acceptance criterion. --- ### Model Answer to Q4 **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Bounded Slicing Grid (BSG):** The BSG representation, introduced by Nakatake et al. in 1996, uses a 2D grid composed of vertical and horizontal line segments. Each grid line is either **present** (1) or **absent** (0), giving a binary encoding. **Encoding Process:** 1. Construct an n × n grid of vertical segments and an n × n grid of horizontal segments. 2. Assign each module to one cell of the grid (one module per cell). 3. Modules occupy rectangular regions formed by adjacent present segments. **Decoding Process:** - A vertical grid sweep determines the x-coordinates of modules. - A horizontal grid sweep determines the y-coordinates. - The packing is obtained by traversing the grid in row-major or column-major order. **Search Space Size:** The BSG representation has a search space of approximately **n^(2n)**, which is much larger than the Sequence Pair's (n!)², but it allows the use of efficient **bitmask-based local search** because each grid segment can be flipped independently. **Advantages:** - Encodes all slicing floorplans and a large subset of non-slicing floorplans. - Permits local moves by flipping a single segment. **Disadvantages:** - Cannot encode all possible non-slicing floorplans. - Decoding requires O(n²) time in the worst case. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Simulated Annealing for Non-Slicing Floorplan:** Simulated Annealing is a stochastic optimization technique that mimics the physical process of cooling molten metal. It is the workhorse of non-slicing floorplan optimization because the search space is NP-hard. **Algorithm Flow:** 1. **Initialize** with a random solution S₀ (e.g., random Sequence Pair) and a high temperature T₀. 2. **Perturb** the current solution to produce a new candidate S' using one of the move operators (swap, reverse, mirror). 3. **Decode** S' into a floorplan and **evaluate the cost function**: $$ \Phi = \alpha \cdot A + (1 - \alpha) \cdot W $$ 4. **Acceptance** (Metropolis Criterion): $$ P_{accept} = \begin{cases} 1, & \Delta\Phi \le 0 \\ e^{-\Delta\Phi / T}, & \Delta\Phi > 0 \end{cases} $$ 5. **Cool the temperature** using a geometric schedule: $$ T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad \alpha \approx 0.95 $$ 6. **Repeat** steps 2–5 until T < T_min or no improvement is observed for K consecutive iterations. **Role of the Cooling Schedule:** The cooling schedule controls the trade-off between **exploration** (high T accepts worse solutions) and **exploitation** (low T only accepts improvements). A geometric schedule is the most common choice. **Role of the Metropolis Criterion:** The Metropolis criterion allows the algorithm to **escape local minima** by occasionally accepting worse solutions. The probability of acceptance decreases as T drops, ensuring eventual convergence to a near-optimal solution. **Convergence Theorem (Hajek, 1988):** SA converges to a global optimum with probability 1 if the cooling schedule satisfies: $$ T_k \geq \frac{c}{\log(k + k_0)} $$ for some constants c, k₀ > 0, although practical implementations use faster geometric cooling. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing HCG and VCG semantics:** A student often writes "if *a* is before *b* in Γ⁺, *a* is above *b*." This is **wrong** — Γ⁺ encodes horizontal (left-right) order, not vertical. Expect 1–2 marks deducted per occurrence. > 2. **Forgetting to take MAX in longest-path computation:** When a module has multiple predecessors, the x or y coordinate is the **maximum** of the incoming paths, not the sum. This is a classic 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Not specifying the source module:** The leftmost module in HCG and the bottommost in VCG are set to coordinate 0. Failing to state this loses 1 mark. > 4. **Mixing up B\*-Tree and BSG:** B\*-Tree is a binary tree, BSG is a grid. They are entirely different data structures. Confusing them in a 14-mark answer can cost 3–4 marks. > 5. **Omitting the area-preservation constraint for soft modules:** When reshaping, $w_i \cdot h_i$ must remain constant. Failing to mention this loses 1 mark. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **non-slicing floorplan** cannot be obtained by recursive guillotine cuts; modules interlock via T-junctions or nesting. - **Sequence Pair (SP):** Two permutations (Γ⁺, Γ⁻); Γ⁺ for left-right, Γ⁻ for below-above. - **HCG** is built from Γ⁺ for x-coordinates; **VCG** is built from Γ⁻ for y-coordinates. - Longest-path computation on HCG and VCG yields module positions in **O(n²)**. - **Bounded Slicing Grid (BSG):** Binary grid of present/absent segments; flip-based local moves. - **O-Tree:** Rooted ordered tree; size O(n); DFS-based decoding. - **B\*-Tree:** Binary tree with left = right-neighbor, right = upper-neighbor; no overlap by construction. - **Corner Block List (CBL):** T-junction records; 3n records; compact for mosaic floorplans. - **Cost Function:** $\Phi = \alpha \cdot A + (1 - \alpha) \cdot W$ balances area and wirelength. - **Simulated Annealing** uses the Metropolis criterion $P_{accept} = e^{-\Delta\Phi / T}$ to escape local minima. - **Cooling schedule:** $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, with α ≈ 0.95. - **Aspect ratio bounds** $r_i \in [r_{min}, r_{max}]$ restrict soft-module reshaping. - **Dead space η** is reported as a percentage; lower is better. - Non-slicing floorplanning is **NP-hard**; SA is the de-facto heuristic used in industry. - Always state the **source module's coordinate is 0** when solving SP problems. - Use **MAX** (not SUM) when computing longest-path coordinates from multiple predecessors. ---
